The Ann Arbor staging system for lymphoma classifies disease into four stages. Which criterion distinguishes Stage III from Stage IV lymphoma?
o: Stage III involves lymph node regions on both sides of the diaphragm (with or without splenic involvement) while Stage IV involves disseminated extranodal involvement (bone marrow, liver, lung parenchyma, or multiple extranodal sites) — the Lugano 2014 modification uses PET-CT for FDG-avid lymphomas and CT for non-avid histologies
Stage III involves bulky mediastinal disease while Stage IV involves any lymph node group above the clavicle
Stage III requires constitutional B symptoms while Stage IV is defined by the absence of B symptoms with widespread nodal disease
Stage III involves >5 nodal sites while Stage IV involves ≥6 nodal sites regardless of diaphragmatic distribution
a: Stage III involves lymph node regions on both sides of the diaphragm (with or without splenic involvement) while Stage IV involves disseminated extranodal involvement (bone marrow, liver, lung parenchyma, or multiple extranodal sites) — the Lugano 2014 modification uses PET-CT for FDG-avid lymphomas and CT for non-avid histologies

q: Classical Hodgkin Lymphoma (cHL) is characterized by which pathognomonic cell type, and what is its immunophenotype?
o: Reed-Sternberg (RS) cells and their mononuclear variants (Hodgkin cells) — immunophenotype: CD15+, CD30+, CD20−/dim, CD45−, PAX5+weak, EBV-LMP1+ in EBV-associated cases; RS cells are derived from germinal center B cells that have lost normal B-cell antigen expression
Reed-Sternberg cells with CD20+, CD19+, CD10+, BCL-6+ immunophenotype — identical to germinal center DLBCL immunophenotype
Lacunar cells with CD5+, CD23+, CD20bright immunophenotype — identical to CLL/SLL surface marker expression on malignant Hodgkin cells
Popcorn cells (lymphocytic and histiocytic cells) with CD20+, CD45+, CD30−, CD15− — the pathognomonic cell of nodular sclerosis Hodgkin Lymphoma exclusively
a: Reed-Sternberg (RS) cells and their mononuclear variants (Hodgkin cells) — immunophenotype: CD15+, CD30+, CD20−/dim, CD45−, PAX5+weak, EBV-LMP1+ in EBV-associated cases; RS cells are derived from germinal center B cells that have lost normal B-cell antigen expression

q: Nodular Lymphocyte-Predominant Hodgkin Lymphoma (NLPHL) is a distinct entity from classical Hodgkin Lymphoma. Which combination of features distinguishes NLPHL from cHL?
o: NLPHL has 'popcorn cells' (LP cells) with CD20+, CD45+, CD79a+, OCT2+, BOB.1+, CD30−, CD15− immunophenotype — indolent behavior with late relapses; unlike cHL, NLPHL responds to rituximab (anti-CD20); risk of transformation to T-cell-rich large B-cell lymphoma (~3–5%)
NLPHL has Reed-Sternberg cells identical to nodular sclerosis cHL but with CD20 co-expression making it sensitive to rituximab-containing regimens
NLPHL is characterized by bimodal age distribution, EBV association >90%, and CD30+ immunophenotype distinguishing it from EBV-negative classical subtypes
NLPHL has the worst prognosis of all Hodgkin Lymphoma subtypes due to its rich lymphocytic background suppressing anti-tumor immune responses
a: NLPHL has 'popcorn cells' (LP cells) with CD20+, CD45+, CD79a+, OCT2+, BOB.1+, CD30−, CD15− immunophenotype — indolent behavior with late relapses; unlike cHL, NLPHL responds to rituximab (anti-CD20); risk of transformation to T-cell-rich large B-cell lymphoma (~3–5%)

q: The ECHELON-1 trial compared A+AVD (brentuximab vedotin + doxorubicin-vinblastine-dacarbazine) versus ABVD for advanced-stage classical Hodgkin Lymphoma. What was the primary outcome and clinical implication?
o: A+AVD demonstrated superior 5-year PFS compared to ABVD (82% vs 75%) with lower rates of disease progression or death — establishing A+AVD as the preferred first-line regimen for advanced-stage (III/IV) cHL; bleomycin is omitted from A+AVD eliminating pulmonary toxicity risk; peripheral neuropathy is the primary toxicity of brentuximab vedotin
A+AVD was inferior to ABVD in overall survival — ABVD remains the standard of care for all stages of Hodgkin Lymphoma based on ECHELON-1 results
A+AVD showed identical PFS to ABVD but reduced bleomycin toxicity — the primary benefit is toxicity reduction rather than improved efficacy
ECHELON-1 demonstrated that brentuximab vedotin monotherapy (without chemotherapy) was non-inferior to ABVD for early-stage Hodgkin Lymphoma
a: A+AVD demonstrated superior 5-year PFS compared to ABVD (82% vs 75%) with lower rates of disease progression or death — establishing A+AVD as the preferred first-line regimen for advanced-stage (III/IV) cHL; bleomycin is omitted from A+AVD eliminating pulmonary toxicity risk; peripheral neuropathy is the primary toxicity of brentuximab vedotin

q: The RATHL trial demonstrated the utility of PET-adapted therapy in Hodgkin Lymphoma. What was the key clinical finding supporting interim PET-guided treatment modification?
o: PET-negative patients after 2 cycles of ABVD (Deauville 1–3) could safely omit bleomycin (switching to AVD) without compromising efficacy — reducing bleomycin pulmonary toxicity while maintaining >95% 3-year PFS; PET-positive patients (Deauville 4–5) were escalated to BEACOPPescalated
PET-negative patients after 2 cycles of ABVD required intensification to BEACOPPescalated to maintain remission depth and reduce late relapse
RATHL showed that all advanced Hodgkin Lymphoma patients benefit from BEACOPPescalated regardless of interim PET result
RATHL demonstrated that interim PET after 2 cycles cannot reliably predict treatment outcome in Hodgkin Lymphoma due to false-positive inflammatory uptake
a: PET-negative patients after 2 cycles of ABVD (Deauville 1–3) could safely omit bleomycin (switching to AVD) without compromising efficacy — reducing bleomycin pulmonary toxicity while maintaining >95% 3-year PFS; PET-positive patients (Deauville 4–5) were escalated to BEACOPPescalated

q: Pembrolizumab and nivolumab (PD-1 checkpoint inhibitors) have demonstrated high response rates in relapsed/refractory classical Hodgkin Lymphoma. Why is cHL uniquely sensitive to PD-1 blockade?
o: Reed-Sternberg cells frequently harbor amplification of chromosome 9p24.1 containing PD-L1 (CD274), PD-L2, and JAK2 genes — causing constitutive PD-L1 overexpression that suppresses anti-tumor T-cell immunity; this genetic vulnerability explains ORR >65–87% in R/R cHL with PD-1 inhibitors
RS cells overexpress PD-1 (not PD-L1) on their surface making them direct targets of anti-PD-1 antibodies causing apoptosis
cHL overexpresses CTLA-4 rather than PD-L1 — ipilimumab is more effective than pembrolizumab in all relapsed cHL patients
PD-L1 overexpression in cHL is driven by EBV-LMP1 signaling exclusively — EBV-negative cHL cases do not respond to PD-1 checkpoint inhibition
a: Reed-Sternberg cells frequently harbor amplification of chromosome 9p24.1 containing PD-L1 (CD274), PD-L2, and JAK2 genes — causing constitutive PD-L1 overexpression that suppresses anti-tumor T-cell immunity; this genetic vulnerability explains ORR >65–87% in R/R cHL with PD-1 inhibitors

q: The International Prognostic Score (IPS) for advanced Hodgkin Lymphoma includes seven adverse prognostic factors. Which of the following is NOT one of the seven IPS variables?
o: Elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR >50 mm/hr) — ESR is a B-symptom surrogate used in early-stage HL EORTC/GHSG scoring (EORTC risk factors) but is NOT part of the seven-variable Hasenclever IPS for advanced-stage HL
Serum albumin <4 g/dL — one of the seven IPS adverse factors in advanced Hodgkin Lymphoma
Hemoglobin <10.5 g/dL — one of the seven IPS adverse factors
Lymphocytopenia (lymphocyte count <600/µL or <8% of WBC differential) — one of the seven IPS adverse factors
a: Elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR >50 mm/hr) — ESR is a B-symptom surrogate used in early-stage HL EORTC/GHSG scoring (EORTC risk factors) but is NOT part of the seven-variable Hasenclever IPS for advanced-stage HL

q: Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma (DLBCL) is classified by cell of origin into GCB and ABC subtypes. Which transcription factor network defines the ABC (activated B-cell-like) subtype and creates potential therapeutic targets?
o: Constitutive NF-κB pathway activation via BCR signaling (CARD11, CD79A/B mutations), MYD88 L265P (particularly in testicular/CNS DLBCL), and TNFAIP3/A20 deletions — aberrant NF-κB signaling sustains ABC-DLBCL survival; ibrutinib targets BTK upstream of NF-κB and shows activity in MYD88-mutated ABC-DLBCL
BCL-6 overexpression with concurrent BCL-2 translocation t(14;18) exclusively defining ABC-DLBCL and differentiating it from GCB-DLBCL
SOX11 and CCND1 overexpression from t(11;14) defining ABC-DLBCL as a mantle cell-like lymphoma variant
EZH2 gain-of-function mutations (Y641) exclusively in ABC-DLBCL causing histone H3K27 trimethylation and silencing of B-cell differentiation genes
a: Constitutive NF-κB pathway activation via BCR signaling (CARD11, CD79A/B mutations), MYD88 L265P (particularly in testicular/CNS DLBCL), and TNFAIP3/A20 deletions — aberrant NF-κB signaling sustains ABC-DLBCL survival; ibrutinib targets BTK upstream of NF-κB and shows activity in MYD88-mutated ABC-DLBCL

q: Polatuzumab vedotin (Polivy) is an anti-CD79b antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) approved for DLBCL. What is its mechanism of action and approved clinical indication?
o: Anti-CD79b antibody conjugated to MMAE (monomethyl auristatin E — microtubule-disrupting cytotoxin) via protease-cleavable linker — causes targeted intracellular delivery of MMAE to CD79b-expressing B cells; approved in combination with bendamustine-rituximab (Pola-BR) for relapsed/refractory DLBCL after ≥2 prior lines and in combination with R-CHP as first-line DLBCL (POLARIX trial)
Anti-CD79b antibody conjugated to calicheamicin (DNA double-strand break agent) — identical ADC mechanism to gemtuzumab ozogamicin targeting AML
Anti-CD79b antibody conjugated to DM1 (emtansine — maytansinoid microtubule inhibitor) — same payload as ado-trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1) in breast cancer
Anti-CD79b naked antibody without cytotoxic payload — depletes B cells by ADCC and CDC mechanisms identical to rituximab targeting a different epitope
a: Anti-CD79b antibody conjugated to MMAE (monomethyl auristatin E — microtubule-disrupting cytotoxin) via protease-cleavable linker — causes targeted intracellular delivery of MMAE to CD79b-expressing B cells; approved in combination with bendamustine-rituximab (Pola-BR) for relapsed/refractory DLBCL after ≥2 prior lines and in combination with R-CHP as first-line DLBCL (POLARIX trial)

q: The POLARIX trial evaluated polatuzumab vedotin + R-CHP (pola-R-CHP) versus R-CHOP for newly diagnosed DLBCL. What was the primary finding and how does it influence clinical practice?
o: Pola-R-CHP demonstrated superior 2-year PFS (76.7% vs 70.2%) compared to R-CHOP in DLBCL patients with IPI ≥2 — vincristine is replaced by polatuzumab vedotin; the regimen is now an approved first-line option for DLBCL particularly in IPI ≥2 patients; no overall survival benefit was demonstrated at primary analysis
Pola-R-CHP demonstrated superior overall survival compared to R-CHOP justifying its universal adoption as the new standard first-line regimen for all DLBCL regardless of IPI score
POLARIX showed pola-R-CHP was inferior to R-CHOP in GCB-DLBCL — its benefit is exclusively in the ABC subtype
POLARIX demonstrated no PFS benefit over R-CHOP — polatuzumab vedotin remains reserved for relapsed/refractory DLBCL only
a: Pola-R-CHP demonstrated superior 2-year PFS (76.7% vs 70.2%) compared to R-CHOP in DLBCL patients with IPI ≥2 — vincristine is replaced by polatuzumab vedotin; the regimen is now an approved first-line option for DLBCL particularly in IPI ≥2 patients; no overall survival benefit was demonstrated at primary analysis

q: Primary Mediastinal B-cell Lymphoma (PMBL) is a distinct DLBCL subtype arising from thymic B cells. Which molecular feature of PMBL overlaps with classical Hodgkin Lymphoma, and what is the preferred first-line chemotherapy regimen?
o: PMBL shares 9p24.1 amplification (PD-L1/PD-L2/JAK2) and constitutive JAK-STAT signaling with cHL — preferred first-line regimen is DA-EPOCH-R (dose-adjusted etoposide-prednisone-vincristine-cyclophosphamide-doxorubicin + rituximab) which may eliminate the need for consolidation mediastinal radiotherapy in PET-negative patients
PMBL is biologically identical to GCB-DLBCL with t(14;18) BCL-2 rearrangement — R-CHOP followed by mediastinal RT is always required
PMBL exclusively expresses CD30 (identical to cHL) and is treated with brentuximab vedotin + AVD replacing all cytotoxic chemotherapy
PMBL has MYC-BCL-2 double-hit rearrangements in >90% of cases requiring DA-EPOCH-R + CNS prophylaxis identical to double-hit DLBCL
a: PMBL shares 9p24.1 amplification (PD-L1/PD-L2/JAK2) and constitutive JAK-STAT signaling with cHL — preferred first-line regimen is DA-EPOCH-R (dose-adjusted etoposide-prednisone-vincristine-cyclophosphamide-doxorubicin + rituximab) which may eliminate the need for consolidation mediastinal radiotherapy in PET-negative patients

q: CNS prophylaxis for DLBCL is recommended in high CNS-IPI risk patients. Which sites of DLBCL involvement are considered 'CNS-IPI high-risk' independent of the standard IPI score?
o: Renal/adrenal involvement, testicular DLBCL, breast DLBCL, epidural involvement, bone marrow involvement with concurrent MYD88 L265P — CNS-IPI score combines 4 IPI factors (LDH, PS, extranodal sites, stage) + kidney/adrenal involvement; CNS-IPI ≥4 has ~10–15% CNS relapse risk without prophylaxis
Splenic and mediastinal involvement — both sites have high rates of CNS dissemination due to proximity to neurological structures
Bone marrow involvement alone — the most common extranodal site predicting CNS relapse in all DLBCL subtypes
Liver involvement — hepatic DLBCL has the highest CNS relapse rate through portal-to-CNS vascular communication
a: Renal/adrenal involvement, testicular DLBCL, breast DLBCL, epidural involvement, bone marrow involvement with concurrent MYD88 L265P — CNS-IPI score combines 4 IPI factors (LDH, PS, extranodal sites, stage) + kidney/adrenal involvement; CNS-IPI ≥4 has ~10–15% CNS relapse risk without prophylaxis

q: Follicular Lymphoma Grade 3B (FL3B) differs from FL Grades 1, 2, and 3A in which clinically important aspect?
o: FL Grade 3B is characterized by solid sheets of centroblasts without admixed centrocytes — it behaves clinically like DLBCL (aggressive, potentially curable), is treated with R-CHOP rather than watchful waiting, and lacks the t(14;18) BCL-2 rearrangement found in lower-grade FL; BCL-6 rearrangements are more common in FL3B
FL3B has the same indolent behavior as FL Grade 1–2 but requires higher-dose chemotherapy to achieve remission
FL3B is exclusively diagnosed in elderly patients over 70 years due to progressive histological transformation from FL1 after decades of indolent disease
FL3B has a better prognosis than FL Grade 1–2 because the higher-grade cellular composition triggers stronger anti-tumor immune responses
a: FL Grade 3B is characterized by solid sheets of centroblasts without admixed centrocytes — it behaves clinically like DLBCL (aggressive, potentially curable), is treated with R-CHOP rather than watchful waiting, and lacks the t(14;18) BCL-2 rearrangement found in lower-grade FL; BCL-6 rearrangements are more common in FL3B

q: The PRIMA trial established the role of rituximab maintenance in follicular lymphoma. What was the key finding and for how long is maintenance rituximab administered?
o: Rituximab maintenance 375 mg/m² every 2 months for 2 years after induction chemoimmunotherapy (R-CHOP or R-CVP) significantly improved PFS (51 months vs 14 months) compared to observation — PFS benefit was sustained though no overall survival advantage was demonstrated at primary analysis; now standard of care for responding advanced-stage FL
Rituximab maintenance administered for 5 years significantly improved overall survival and is now curative in advanced-stage FL
Rituximab maintenance is only beneficial after R-bendamustine induction — the PRIMA trial exclusively used bendamustine as the induction backbone
PRIMA demonstrated that rituximab maintenance is inferior to observation in FL patients achieving CR after induction — the immunosuppressive effects of maintenance outweigh the PFS benefit
a: Rituximab maintenance 375 mg/m² every 2 months for 2 years after induction chemoimmunotherapy (R-CHOP or R-CVP) significantly improved PFS (51 months vs 14 months) compared to observation — PFS benefit was sustained though no overall survival advantage was demonstrated at primary analysis; now standard of care for responding advanced-stage FL

q: Tazemetostat, an EZH2 inhibitor, is approved for relapsed/refractory follicular lymphoma. What is the molecular basis for its selective activity in EZH2-mutated FL?
o: EZH2 Y641 gain-of-function mutations (present in ~20–25% of FL) cause aberrant histone H3K27 trimethylation silencing tumor suppressor genes (CDKN1A, CDKN1B) — tazemetostat selectively inhibits mutant EZH2 enzyme activity reversing H3K27me3 silencing; also active in EZH2 wild-type FL via alternative mechanism; FDA-approved for relapsed/refractory FL after ≥2 prior lines
EZH2 mutations exclusively occur in DLBCL transformation of FL — tazemetostat activity in transformed FL is higher than in low-grade disease
EZH2 inhibition causes direct DNA double-strand breaks in FL cells independently of histone methylation status
Tazemetostat blocks EZH2 ubiquitin ligase activity preventing proteasomal degradation of p53 — active exclusively in TP53 wild-type FL
a: EZH2 Y641 gain-of-function mutations (present in ~20–25% of FL) cause aberrant histone H3K27 trimethylation silencing tumor suppressor genes (CDKN1A, CDKN1B) — tazemetostat selectively inhibits mutant EZH2 enzyme activity reversing H3K27me3 silencing; also active in EZH2 wild-type FL via alternative mechanism; FDA-approved for relapsed/refractory FL after ≥2 prior lines

q: Mantle Cell Lymphoma (MCL) exhibits a biologically variable course from indolent (leukemic non-nodal MCL with SOX11−, IGHV mutated) to highly aggressive (blastoid/pleomorphic variants). Which molecular finding portends the worst prognosis in MCL?
o: TP53 mutation/deletion combined with complex karyotype (≥3 chromosomal aberrations) — TP53-mutated MCL responds poorly to all conventional chemotherapy including high-dose cytarabine-based regimens; median OS <2 years; ibrutinib + venetoclax combinations show activity but outcomes remain poor; HSCT consolidation may not overcome TP53-driven resistance
SOX11 overexpression — the worst prognostic marker in MCL regardless of other molecular features
t(11;14) translocation intensity — higher CCND1 expression levels correlate directly with inferior overall survival in all MCL subtypes
Blastoid morphology alone — the histological variant is more prognostically important than any molecular marker in MCL
a: TP53 mutation/deletion combined with complex karyotype (≥3 chromosomal aberrations) — TP53-mutated MCL responds poorly to all conventional chemotherapy including high-dose cytarabine-based regimens; median OS <2 years; ibrutinib + venetoclax combinations show activity but outcomes remain poor; HSCT consolidation may not overcome TP53-driven resistance

q: The TRIANGLE trial evaluated the role of ibrutinib in newly diagnosed younger MCL patients eligible for autologous HSCT. What were the key findings?
o: Adding ibrutinib to induction (R-CHOP/R-DHAP) and maintenance significantly improved failure-free survival compared to standard immunochemotherapy + auto-HSCT; the ibrutinib + auto-HSCT arm appeared to outperform ibrutinib alone, suggesting auto-HSCT remains valuable in the ibrutinib era for eligible patients
TRIANGLE demonstrated ibrutinib monotherapy was superior to auto-HSCT in all younger MCL patients, permanently eliminating HSCT from MCL treatment algorithms
TRIANGLE showed no benefit of adding ibrutinib to standard MCL immunochemotherapy — auto-HSCT consolidation remains the sole effective intensification strategy
TRIANGLE established acalabrutinib (not ibrutinib) as the preferred BTK inhibitor in newly diagnosed MCL due to superior CNS penetration
a: Adding ibrutinib to induction (R-CHOP/R-DHAP) and maintenance significantly improved failure-free survival compared to standard immunochemotherapy + auto-HSCT; the ibrutinib + auto-HSCT arm appeared to outperform ibrutinib alone, suggesting auto-HSCT remains valuable in the ibrutinib era for eligible patients

q: Burkitt Lymphoma (BL) requires urgent treatment due to its extremely rapid doubling time. Which regimen is considered the most effective for adult Burkitt Lymphoma, and what is the most feared early treatment complication?
o: DA-EPOCH-R (dose-adjusted EPOCH + rituximab) or R-CODOX-M/IVAC (cyclophosphamide-doxorubicin-vincristine-methotrexate alternating with ifosfamide-etoposide-cytarabine + rituximab) — tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) is the most feared early complication given BL's extremely high proliferative rate; rasburicase prophylaxis is mandatory; rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency
R-CHOP × 6 cycles — the standard regimen for all aggressive B-cell lymphomas including Burkitt Lymphoma in adults
Chlorambucil + prednisolone — the preferred low-intensity approach for endemic Burkitt Lymphoma in all geographic regions
R-CODOX-M/IVAC for stages III/IV only — early-stage BL (stage I/II) requires observation without chemotherapy given frequent spontaneous remissions
a: DA-EPOCH-R (dose-adjusted EPOCH + rituximab) or R-CODOX-M/IVAC (cyclophosphamide-doxorubicin-vincristine-methotrexate alternating with ifosfamide-etoposide-cytarabine + rituximab) — tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) is the most feared early complication given BL's extremely high proliferative rate; rasburicase prophylaxis is mandatory; rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency

q: Marginal Zone Lymphoma (MZL) of the MALT type arising at various extranodal sites is associated with specific chronic antigenic stimulations beyond Helicobacter pylori. Which pathogen-site association is correctly paired?
o: Borrelia burgdorferi — cutaneous MALT lymphoma of the skin (primary cutaneous MZL); Chlamydia psittaci — ocular adnexal MALT lymphoma of the conjunctiva/orbit; Campylobacter jejuni — immunoproliferative small intestinal disease (IPSID/alpha-heavy chain disease); Hepatitis C virus — splenic MZL and some MALT lymphomas
Helicobacter pylori — salivary gland MALT lymphoma; Borrelia burgdorferi — gastric MALT lymphoma; EBV — thyroid MALT lymphoma
CMV — hepatic MALT lymphoma; HTLV-1 — pulmonary MALT lymphoma; Treponema pallidum — ocular adnexal MALT lymphoma
All MALT lymphomas are exclusively caused by H. pylori infection regardless of anatomical site — antibiotic eradication therapy is first-line for all MALT lymphoma sites
a: Borrelia burgdorferi — cutaneous MALT lymphoma of the skin (primary cutaneous MZL); Chlamydia psittaci — ocular adnexal MALT lymphoma of the conjunctiva/orbit; Campylobacter jejuni — immunoproliferative small intestinal disease (IPSID/alpha-heavy chain disease); Hepatitis C virus — splenic MZL and some MALT lymphomas

q: Splenic Marginal Zone Lymphoma (SMZL) presents with massive splenomegaly and peripheral blood involvement. Which molecular finding is characteristic of SMZL and differentiates it from other splenic B-cell lymphoproliferative disorders?
o: NOTCH2 mutations (~25% of SMZL) causing constitutive Notch signaling promoting marginal zone B-cell differentiation; KLF2 mutations (~40%); 7q31-32 deletion — immunophenotype: CD19+, CD20+, CD5−, CD10−, CD23−, CD103−, CD123−, DBA44+ (weak), IgM+IgD+; villous lymphocytes on peripheral smear
MYD88 L265P mutation in >90% of SMZL — the same mutation found in Waldenström Macroglobulinemia confirming their shared molecular pathogenesis
BRAF V600E mutation in >95% of SMZL — tazemetostat inhibition is the primary therapeutic target in SMZL
SOX11 overexpression and t(11;14) translocation in SMZL — identical molecular features to mantle cell lymphoma but with splenic predominance
a: NOTCH2 mutations (~25% of SMZL) causing constitutive Notch signaling promoting marginal zone B-cell differentiation; KLF2 mutations (~40%); 7q31-32 deletion — immunophenotype: CD19+, CD20+, CD5−, CD10−, CD23−, CD103−, CD123−, DBA44+ (weak), IgM+IgD+; villous lymphocytes on peripheral smear

q: Angioimmunoblastic T-cell Lymphoma (AITL) is the most common nodal peripheral T-cell lymphoma in the Western world. Which molecular feature is most characteristic of AITL and has prognostic/therapeutic implications?
o: TET2 (up to 76%), DNMT3A, and IDH2 R172 mutations (up to 20–33%) in AITL — IDH2 mutations are targetable by enasidenib (IDH2 inhibitor); RHOA G17V mutation (~68%) causes a dominant-negative effect; AITL arises from follicular helper T cells (TFH) with CD10+, BCL-6+, CXCL13+, PD-1+, ICOS+, SAP+ immunophenotype
ALK rearrangements (NPM1-ALK) in >90% of AITL — distinguishing it from ALK-negative ALCL and making it crizotinib-sensitive
MYC rearrangements in AITL cause aggressive double-hit biology requiring DA-EPOCH chemotherapy identical to double-hit DLBCL
JAK2 V617F mutations in >80% of AITL — ruxolitinib (JAK1/2 inhibitor) achieves complete remission as monotherapy in all AITL patients
a: TET2 (up to 76%), DNMT3A, and IDH2 R172 mutations (up to 20–33%) in AITL — IDH2 mutations are targetable by enasidenib (IDH2 inhibitor); RHOA G17V mutation (~68%) causes a dominant-negative effect; AITL arises from follicular helper T cells (TFH) with CD10+, BCL-6+, CXCL13+, PD-1+, ICOS+, SAP+ immunophenotype

q: Anaplastic Large Cell Lymphoma (ALCL) is divided into ALK-positive and ALK-negative subtypes. Which statement correctly contrasts these two subtypes in terms of prognosis and molecular pathogenesis?
o: ALK+ ALCL (predominantly young patients, t(2;5) NPM1-ALK in ~85%, CD30+) has significantly better prognosis (5-year OS ~70–80%) than ALK− ALCL (5-year OS ~40–50%); ALK− ALCL is associated with DUSP22 rearrangements (better prognosis subgroup) and TP63 rearrangements (worst prognosis subgroup); breast implant-associated ALCL (BIA-ALCL) is a distinct indolent ALK− entity with excellent prognosis after implant removal
ALK+ ALCL has worse prognosis than ALK− ALCL because ALK fusion protein drives chemotherapy resistance through constitutive JAK-STAT3 signaling
ALK+ and ALK− ALCL have identical prognosis when treated with CHOP — the distinction is purely molecular without clinical prognostic relevance
ALK− ALCL exclusively affects elderly men over 70 years while ALK+ ALCL exclusively affects women under 30 years
a: ALK+ ALCL (predominantly young patients, t(2;5) NPM1-ALK in ~85%, CD30+) has significantly better prognosis (5-year OS ~70–80%) than ALK− ALCL (5-year OS ~40–50%); ALK− ALCL is associated with DUSP22 rearrangements (better prognosis subgroup) and TP63 rearrangements (worst prognosis subgroup); breast implant-associated ALCL (BIA-ALCL) is a distinct indolent ALK− entity with excellent prognosis after implant removal

q: Extranodal NK/T-cell Lymphoma (ENKTL), nasal type is strongly associated with EBV and occurs predominantly in which geographic populations? What is the preferred first-line treatment regimen?
o: Predominantly affects Asian and Latin American populations — associated with EBV in virtually 100% of cases; preferred first-line regimen is SMILE (dexamethasone-methotrexate-ifosfamide-L-asparaginase-etoposide) or non-anthracycline-based regimens (P-GEMOX: pegaspargase-gemcitabine-oxaliplatin) due to MDR1/P-glycoprotein-mediated anthracycline resistance in ENKTL; plasma EBV DNA is a sensitive response biomarker
Predominantly affects Northern European populations — EBV association is present in only 30% of cases; R-CHOP is the standard first-line regimen
ENKTL predominantly affects sub-Saharan African populations through Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV/HHV-8) rather than EBV
ENKTL exclusively occurs in immunocompromised HIV-positive patients — antiretroviral therapy achieves tumor regression without chemotherapy in most cases
a: Predominantly affects Asian and Latin American populations — associated with EBV in virtually 100% of cases; preferred first-line regimen is SMILE (dexamethasone-methotrexate-ifosfamide-L-asparaginase-etoposide) or non-anthracycline-based regimens (P-GEMOX: pegaspargase-gemcitabine-oxaliplatin) due to MDR1/P-glycoprotein-mediated anthracycline resistance in ENKTL; plasma EBV DNA is a sensitive response biomarker

q: The ZUMA-7 trial compared axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel) CAR-T therapy versus standard of care (salvage chemoimmunotherapy + auto-HSCT) as second-line treatment for relapsed/refractory DLBCL. What were the key results?
o: Axi-cel demonstrated superior event-free survival (EFS) at 24 months (41% vs 16%) compared to standard second-line treatment — supporting CAR-T as the preferred second-line therapy for DLBCL relapsing within 12 months of first-line or primary refractory; no benefit was shown for late relapsers (>12 months) who still benefit from salvage + auto-HSCT
Axi-cel showed inferior EFS compared to auto-HSCT — standard salvage chemotherapy + auto-HSCT remains preferred for all relapsed DLBCL patients in the second-line setting
ZUMA-7 demonstrated axi-cel superiority only in GCB-DLBCL subtype — ABC-DLBCL continues to benefit more from salvage chemotherapy
ZUMA-7 established CAR-T therapy as first-line treatment for all high-risk DLBCL (IPI ≥3) based on superior PFS without prior salvage chemotherapy requirement
a: Axi-cel demonstrated superior event-free survival (EFS) at 24 months (41% vs 16%) compared to standard second-line treatment — supporting CAR-T as the preferred second-line therapy for DLBCL relapsing within 12 months of first-line or primary refractory; no benefit was shown for late relapsers (>12 months) who still benefit from salvage + auto-HSCT

q: Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS) is a major toxicity of CAR-T cell therapy. Which grading system and management approach is used for CRS in lymphoma patients receiving CD19-directed CAR-T therapy?
o: ASTCT 2019 consensus grading (Grade 1: fever ≥38°C; Grade 2: fever + hypotension responding to fluids or low-dose vasopressors or O2 <6L; Grade 3: vasopressors for hypotension or O2 ≥6L; Grade 4: life-threatening with mechanical ventilation) — managed with tocilizumab (IL-6 receptor antagonist) for Grade ≥2 CRS and corticosteroids for refractory cases
CRS is managed exclusively with high-dose corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily) — tocilizumab is contraindicated as it abrogates CAR-T anti-tumor efficacy
CRS grading requires ICU admission and plasma exchange for all grades above Grade 1 to prevent irreversible cytokine-mediated organ damage
Tocilizumab worsens CAR-T cell expansion by blocking IL-6 trans-signaling — anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist) is preferred for all grades of CRS after CD19 CAR-T infusion
a: ASTCT 2019 consensus grading (Grade 1: fever ≥38°C; Grade 2: fever + hypotension responding to fluids or low-dose vasopressors or O2 <6L; Grade 3: vasopressors for hypotension or O2 ≥6L; Grade 4: life-threatening with mechanical ventilation) — managed with tocilizumab (IL-6 receptor antagonist) for Grade ≥2 CRS and corticosteroids for refractory cases

q: Immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) is a CNS toxicity of CAR-T therapy. What are its characteristic clinical features and management?
o: ICANS features: encephalopathy (confusion, aphasia, delirium), tremor, seizures, raised intracranial pressure, and cerebral edema in severe cases — graded by immune effector cell-associated encephalopathy (ICE) score and level of consciousness; managed with corticosteroids (dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h); seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam; unlike CRS, tocilizumab is NOT effective for ICANS
ICANS is treated identically to CRS — tocilizumab is effective for all grades of both CRS and ICANS toxicity
ICANS exclusively presents as isolated Grade 4 cerebral edema — lower-grade neurological symptoms after CAR-T are always attributable to CRS rather than independent ICANS
ICANS is caused by direct CAR-T cell invasion of the CNS through the blood-brain barrier — intrathecal cytarabine is required for all Grade ≥2 ICANS
a: ICANS features: encephalopathy (confusion, aphasia, delirium), tremor, seizures, raised intracranial pressure, and cerebral edema in severe cases — graded by immune effector cell-associated encephalopathy (ICE) score and level of consciousness; managed with corticosteroids (dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h); seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam; unlike CRS, tocilizumab is NOT effective for ICANS

q: Lymphoplasmacytic Lymphoma (LPL)/Waldenström Macroglobulinemia (WM) is treated with which combination regimens, and which mutation determines ibrutinib sensitivity?
o: Ibrutinib (BTK inhibitor) is highly active in WM — superior responses in MYD88 L265P mutated/CXCR4 wild-type patients (ORR ~100%); reduced but significant responses in CXCR4 mutated patients; first-line options include BR (bendamustine-rituximab), DRC (dexamethasone-rituximab-cyclophosphamide), and ibrutinib ± rituximab (iNNOVATE trial); bortezomib-based regimens also active
CXCR4 mutation alone determines ibrutinib sensitivity — MYD88 mutational status is irrelevant for BTK inhibitor treatment selection in WM
Ibrutinib is contraindicated in WM due to paradoxical IgM flare causing acute hyperviscosity within 24 hours of initiation in all patients
FCR (fludarabine-cyclophosphamide-rituximab) is the preferred first-line regimen for all WM patients regardless of MYD88 or CXCR4 status
a: Ibrutinib (BTK inhibitor) is highly active in WM — superior responses in MYD88 L265P mutated/CXCR4 wild-type patients (ORR ~100%); reduced but significant responses in CXCR4 mutated patients; first-line options include BR (bendamustine-rituximab), DRC (dexamethasone-rituximab-cyclophosphamide), and ibrutinib ± rituximab (iNNOVATE trial); bortezomib-based regimens also active

q: Brentuximab vedotin (BV) is an anti-CD30 ADC delivering MMAE. Beyond Hodgkin Lymphoma, in which T-cell lymphoma subtype did brentuximab vedotin combined with CHP (cyclophosphamide-doxorubicin-prednisone) demonstrate superiority over CHOP in the ECHELON-2 trial?
o: CD30-positive peripheral T-cell lymphomas (PTCL) — specifically ALCL (ALK+ and ALK−), AITL, and PTCL-NOS with CD30 expression; ECHELON-2 demonstrated BV-CHP superior PFS and OS compared to CHOP in CD30+ PTCL; BV-CHP is now the standard first-line regimen for CD30-positive PTCL
Exclusively ALK+ ALCL — the ECHELON-2 benefit was limited to ALK-positive disease and does not extend to other T-cell lymphomas
Cutaneous T-cell lymphomas (mycosis fungoides/Sézary syndrome) — CD30 expression in advanced MF/SS makes BV-CHP the preferred systemic treatment
ENKTL nasal type — CD30 expression in ENKTL provides therapeutic sensitivity to brentuximab vedotin combined with asparaginase-based regimens
a: CD30-positive peripheral T-cell lymphomas (PTCL) — specifically ALCL (ALK+ and ALK−), AITL, and PTCL-NOS with CD30 expression; ECHELON-2 demonstrated BV-CHP superior PFS and OS compared to CHOP in CD30+ PTCL; BV-CHP is now the standard first-line regimen for CD30-positive PTCL

q: Mycosis Fungoides (MF) is the most common primary cutaneous T-cell lymphoma. The ISCL/EORTC staging system for MF incorporates which four parameters into the TNMB classification?
o: T (skin involvement — patches/plaques/tumors/erythroderma, percentage BSA); N (lymph node involvement and histological grade); M (visceral metastasis); B (blood involvement — Sézary cell count and flow cytometry) — Stage IVA1 denotes Sézary Syndrome (B2 blood involvement: ≥1000 Sézary cells/µL with CD4:CD8 ratio ≥10 or >40% CD4+CD7− or >30% CD4+CD26− T cells)
T (tumor number), N (nodal bulk in cm), M (metastatic organ involvement), B (bone marrow involvement percentage)
T (thickness of skin plaque in mm), N (number of involved nodal regions), M (MRI-confirmed visceral disease), B (Beta-2 microglobulin level)
T (total body surface area involvement only), N (nodes above vs. below diaphragm), M (PET avidity of extranodal disease), B (blood eosinophil count as inflammatory marker)
a: T (skin involvement — patches/plaques/tumors/erythroderma, percentage BSA); N (lymph node involvement and histological grade); M (visceral metastasis); B (blood involvement — Sézary cell count and flow cytometry) — Stage IVA1 denotes Sézary Syndrome (B2 blood involvement: ≥1000 Sézary cells/µL with CD4:CD8 ratio ≥10 or >40% CD4+CD7− or >30% CD4+CD26− T cells)

q: Mogamulizumab (anti-CCR4 monoclonal antibody) is approved for mycosis fungoides and Sézary Syndrome. What is its mechanism of action and which lymphoma subtypes are most likely to respond?
o: Mogamulizumab targets CCR4 (CC chemokine receptor 4) expressed on malignant T cells — afucosylated IgG1 antibody with enhanced ADCC; approved for MF/SS after ≥1 prior systemic therapy; particularly effective for blood (B2) involvement in Sézary Syndrome; CCR4 is also highly expressed in ATLL (adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma) — FDA-approved for ATLL in Japan
Mogamulizumab targets CCR7 expressed on central memory T-cell lymphomas — exclusively active in nodal PTCL subtypes without skin involvement
Mogamulizumab is an anti-PD-1 antibody identical to pembrolizumab mechanism — distinguished only by T-cell subtype targeting
Mogamulizumab targets CD52 (identical to alemtuzumab) but with preferential activity against CD4+ malignant T cells in cutaneous lymphomas
a: Mogamulizumab targets CCR4 (CC chemokine receptor 4) expressed on malignant T cells — afucosylated IgG1 antibody with enhanced ADCC; approved for MF/SS after ≥1 prior systemic therapy; particularly effective for blood (B2) involvement in Sézary Syndrome; CCR4 is also highly expressed in ATLL (adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma) — FDA-approved for ATLL in Japan

It simultaneously chelates both iron and zinc
a: It efficiently penetrates cardiomyocytes and chelates intracellular cardiac iron

q: Betibeglogene autotemcel (Zynteglo) is a gene therapy for transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia that works by:
o: Inserting a functional beta-globin gene via lentiviral vector into autologous stem cells
Using CRISPR-Cas9 to reactivate gamma-globin gene expression
Correcting beta-globin point mutations via base editing
Delivering normal beta-globin mRNA into erythroid precursors
a: Inserting a functional beta-globin gene via lentiviral vector into autologous stem cells

q: Luspatercept (Reblozyl) is approved for treating anemia in beta-thalassemia by targeting which pathway?
o: TGF-β/SMAD2/3 signaling pathway to reduce ineffective erythropoiesis
JAK-STAT pathway to increase erythropoietin sensitivity
BCL-2 pathway to reduce erythroid apoptosis
mTOR pathway to increase erythroid proliferation
a: TGF-β/SMAD2/3 signaling pathway to reduce ineffective erythropoiesis

q: The Southeast Asian deletion (--SEA) is the most common cause of alpha-thalassemia in Southeast Asia and involves deletion of:
o: Both alpha-globin genes on the same chromosome 16 (cis deletion)
One alpha-globin gene on each chromosome 16 (trans deletion)
The beta-globin gene cluster on chromosome 11
The delta and beta-globin genes on chromosome 11
a: Both alpha-globin genes on the same chromosome 16 (cis deletion)

q: Which of the following red cell indices is most characteristic of thalassemia trait (minor) and helps distinguish it from iron deficiency anemia?
o: Low MCV with normal or elevated RBC count and low RDW
Low MCV with low RBC count and high RDW
High MCV with normal RBC count
a: Low MCV with normal or elevated RBC count and low RDW

q: The Mentzer Index (MCV/RBC) is used to distinguish thalassemia trait from iron deficiency anemia. A value less than 13 suggests:
In beta-thalassemia intermedia, patients typically:
o: Maintain hemoglobin of 7–10 g/dL without or with infrequent transfusions
Require regular transfusions every 2–4 weeks from early childhood
Are completely asymptomatic with normal hemoglobin levels
Present at birth with severe hydrops fetalis
a: Maintain hemoglobin of 7–10 g/dL without or with infrequent transfusions

q: Hemoglobin Constant Spring (HbCS) is a non-deletional alpha-thalassemia caused by:
o: A point mutation in the alpha2-globin stop codon producing an elongated alpha-globin chain
A large deletion removing both alpha-globin genes
A frameshift mutation causing premature termination of alpha-globin synthesis
A splice site mutation reducing alpha-globin mRNA stability
a: A point mutation in the alpha2-globin stop codon producing an elongated alpha-globin chain

q: Splenomegaly in thalassemia major is primarily caused by:
o: Extramedullary hematopoiesis and accelerated destruction of abnormal red cells
a: Extramedullary hematopoiesis and accelerated destruction of abnormal red cells

q: Which complication of repeated blood transfusions in thalassemia is responsible for the majority of deaths in inadequately chelated patients?
o: Cardiac iron overload causing dilated cardiomyopathy and arrhythmias
Endocrine iron deposition causing diabetes mellitus
a: Cardiac iron overload causing dilated cardiomyopathy and arrhythmias

q: Which of the following best describes the inheritance pattern of beta-thalassemia major (Cooley's anemia)?
o: Autosomal recessive — both parents must carry the trait
Autosomal dominant — one mutant allele is sufficient
Mitochondrial — transmitted through the maternal line only
a: Autosomal recessive — both parents must carry the trait

q: The HbA1c (glycated hemoglobin) test is unreliable for monitoring glycemic control in thalassemia patients because:
o: Shortened red cell lifespan and transfused cells lead to falsely low HbA1c values
Excess HbF interferes with HbA1c measurement giving falsely high results
Iron overload glycates hemoglobin non-specifically giving falsely elevated values
Splenomegaly causes sequestration of glycated red cells lowering measured HbA1c
a: Shortened red cell lifespan and transfused cells lead to falsely low HbA1c values

q: Hydroxyurea is used in some thalassemia patients to increase which hemoglobin that compensates for reduced beta-globin chain production?

Which Mediterranean country has implemented the most successful national thalassemia prevention program reducing the incidence of thalassemia major by over 90% through carrier screening and prenatal diagnosis?
The CRISPR-Cas9-based gene therapy Exagamglogene autotemcel (Casgevy), approved in 2023 for both sickle cell disease and transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia, works by:
o: Disrupting the BCL11A enhancer to reactivate fetal hemoglobin (HbF) production
Correcting the specific beta-globin point mutation directly
Inserting a functional beta-globin transgene via viral vector
Silencing the alpha-globin genes to rebalance globin chain synthesis
a: Disrupting the BCL11A enhancer to reactivate fetal hemoglobin (HbF) production

q: Which of the following is a compound heterozygous state in thalassemia?
HbS/beta-thalassemia is most prevalent in which population?
The clinical severity of HbS/beta-thalassemia depends primarily on:
o: The amount of HbA produced by the beta-thalassemia allele
a: The amount of HbA produced by the beta-thalassemia allele

q: HbS/beta-zero thalassemia (β0) is characterized by:
Which form of HbS/beta-thalassemia has a milder clinical presentation?
HbC/beta-thalassemia is most commonly found in:
The hemoglobin electrophoresis pattern in HbS/beta-thalassemia typically shows:
Which of the following is a thalassemia intermedia form?
Alpha-thalassemia with beta-thalassemia results in:
HbE/beta-thalassemia is most prevalent in:
The clinical severity of HbE/beta-thalassemia ranges from:
Which compound heterozygous condition is often as severe as beta-thalassemia major?
HbH disease results from deletion of how many alpha-globin genes?
Coinheritance of alpha and beta thalassemia can result in:
The pathophysiology of HbS/beta-thalassemia involves:
Which of the following is a 'double heterozygous' thalassemia state?
Patients with HbS/beta-thalassemia are at risk for:
HbC/beta-thalassemia typically presents with:
The term 'thalassemia intermedia' refers to:
Coinheritance of HbS with alpha-thalassemia results in:
Which hemoglobin variant combined with beta-thalassemia produces a clinical picture similar to sickle cell disease?
HbE/beta-thalassemia is classified into how many clinical phenotypes?
The diagnostic approach for thalassemic hemoglobinopathies includes:
o: CBC, hemoglobin electrophoresis, and genetic testing
a: CBC, hemoglobin electrophoresis, and genetic testing

q: HbS/beta-thalassemia patients with high HbA levels typically have:
The 'African' form of HbS/beta-thalassemia is usually:
Which iron overload condition is associated with HbS/beta-thalassemia?
The World Health Organization (WHO) classifies HbE/beta-thalassemia as a:
Prenatal diagnosis for thalassemic hemoglobinopathies is typically done using:
o: Amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling with genetic analysis
a: Amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling with genetic analysis

q: HbF levels in HbS/beta-thalassemia are typically:
The most common cause of severe anemia in HbE/beta-thalassemia is:
Hereditary Persistence of Fetal Hemoglobin (HPFH) is defined as a benign condition characterized by:
o: Continued production of fetal hemoglobin (HbF) beyond the neonatal period into adult life without significant hematological abnormality
Pathological overproduction of HbF causing hemolytic anemia
Failure of gamma-to-beta globin switching causing beta-thalassemia major
Compensatory HbF elevation in response to severe anemia
a: Continued production of fetal hemoglobin (HbF) beyond the neonatal period into adult life without significant hematological abnormality

q: Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) is composed of which globin chain tetramer?
o: Two alpha chains and two gamma chains (α2γ2)
Two alpha chains and two beta chains (α2β2)
Two alpha chains and two delta chains (α2δ2)
a: Two alpha chains and two gamma chains (α2γ2)

q: The normal switch from fetal hemoglobin (HbF) to adult hemoglobin (HbA) production occurs:
o: Around the time of birth, completing by 6 months of age with HbF falling to <1–2% in adults
At 3 years of age when bone marrow fully matures
During the first trimester of fetal development
a: Around the time of birth, completing by 6 months of age with HbF falling to <1–2% in adults

q: The gamma-to-beta globin switching process is primarily regulated by which transcriptional repressor that silences gamma-globin gene expression in adult erythropoiesis?
Which of the following best classifies the two major types of HPFH?
o: Deletional HPFH and Non-deletional (point mutation) HPFH
a: Deletional HPFH and Non-deletional (point mutation) HPFH

q: In deletional HPFH, the large deletions in the beta-globin gene cluster remove which regulatory sequences, leading to continued gamma-globin gene expression?
o: Sequences that normally silence the gamma-globin genes in adult erythropoiesis (silencer elements)
The entire beta-globin gene cluster including alpha-globin genes
Only the delta-globin gene leaving beta-globin intact
Locus Control Region (LCR) causing global beta-cluster silencing
a: Sequences that normally silence the gamma-globin genes in adult erythropoiesis (silencer elements)

q: Pancellular HPFH is characterized by HbF distribution in red cells as:
o: Uniform HbF distribution in virtually all red blood cells (all erythrocytes contain HbF)
HbF present in only a subset of red blood cells (F-cells)
HbF present only in nucleated red cell precursors
a: Uniform HbF distribution in virtually all red blood cells (all erythrocytes contain HbF)

q: Heterocellular HPFH differs from pancellular HPFH in that HbF is:
o: Distributed unevenly, present in only a proportion of red blood cells (F-cells) at elevated levels
Uniformly distributed across all erythrocytes at very high levels
Absent from peripheral blood but present in bone marrow precursors
Only detectable by HPLC and not by immunofluorescence
a: Distributed unevenly, present in only a proportion of red blood cells (F-cells) at elevated levels

q: Non-deletional HPFH is most commonly caused by point mutations in which region of the gamma-globin gene promoter?
o: The proximal promoter region of the Aγ or Gγ globin genes (particularly at positions -114 to -202)
The 3' untranslated region of the gamma-globin gene
The coding exon 2 of the gamma-globin gene
The Locus Control Region (LCR) hypersensitive site HS2
a: The proximal promoter region of the Aγ or Gγ globin genes (particularly at positions -114 to -202)

q: In heterozygous deletional HPFH, the HbF level in adults typically ranges from:
Individuals with homozygous deletional HPFH have which hemoglobin composition?
o: Nearly 100% HbF with no HbA and no clinical disease
~50% HbF and ~50% HbA with mild hemolytic anemia
100% HbA2 with absent HbF and HbA
~70% HbF and ~30% HbA2 with thalassemia intermedia phenotype
a: Nearly 100% HbF with no HbA and no clinical disease

q: Which of the following is the most clinically significant interaction of HPFH with a hemoglobinopathy?
o: HPFH with HbS (HbS/HPFH) producing a mild sickle phenotype due to HbF inhibiting sickling
HPFH with HbC producing severe hemolytic anemia
HPFH with HbE producing thalassemia major-like disease
a: HPFH with HbS (HbS/HPFH) producing a mild sickle phenotype due to HbF inhibiting sickling

q: Compound heterozygosity for HPFH and beta-thalassemia typically results in which clinical phenotype?
o: Thalassemia intermedia with elevated HbF partially compensating for reduced beta-globin production
A completely silent carrier state with normal CBC
Severe hemolytic anemia with hydrops fetalis
a: Thalassemia intermedia with elevated HbF partially compensating for reduced beta-globin production

q: The F-cell (red blood cell containing HbF) proportion in normal healthy adults is approximately:
Which of the following quantitative laboratory methods is the gold standard for measuring HbF levels in clinical practice?
The Kleihauer-Betke (acid elution) test detects HbF at the cellular level based on which principle?
o: HbF is resistant to acid elution and stains darkly while HbA is eluted leaving ghost cells
HbF precipitates selectively under acidic conditions forming visible inclusions
HbA is resistant to acid elution while HbF is completely eluted
HbF fluoresces under ultraviolet light after acid treatment
a: HbF is resistant to acid elution and stains darkly while HbA is eluted leaving ghost cells

q: Quantitative trait loci (QTL) studies have identified three major genomic loci that regulate HbF levels in adults. Which of the following is NOT one of these loci?
o: The beta-globin gene cluster (HBB) on chromosome 11p15

q: The MYB transcription factor regulates HbF levels by:
o: Promoting erythroid differentiation which reduces cycling time and increases HbF in less mature progenitors
Directly binding the gamma-globin gene promoter to activate transcription
Inhibiting BCL11A expression to derepress gamma-globin genes
Methylating the beta-globin gene promoter to silence adult hemoglobin
a: Promoting erythroid differentiation which reduces cycling time and increases HbF in less mature progenitors

q: KLF1 (Krüppel-like factor 1) mutations associated with heterocellular HPFH cause elevated HbF by which mechanism?
o: Reduced KLF1 activity decreases BCL11A expression, de-repressing gamma-globin gene transcription
KLF1 directly binds the gamma-globin promoter to activate HbF transcription
KLF1 mutations increase gamma-globin mRNA stability
KLF1 haploinsufficiency causes compensatory upregulation of fetal erythropoiesis
a: Reduced KLF1 activity decreases BCL11A expression, de-repressing gamma-globin gene transcription

q: Which of the following acquired (non-hereditary) conditions is associated with elevated HbF levels mimicking HPFH?
o: Juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia (JMML), aplastic anemia, and pregnancy
Chronic lymphocytic leukemia and multiple myeloma
Iron deficiency anemia and vitamin B12 deficiency
a: Juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia (JMML), aplastic anemia, and pregnancy

q: HbF has a higher oxygen affinity than HbA because fetal hemoglobin gamma chains:
o: Bind 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate (2,3-BPG) less avidly than beta chains, resulting in higher oxygen affinity
Have a structural modification that increases heme-oxygen binding energy
Contain iron in a different oxidation state that binds oxygen more tightly
Lack the Bohr effect making them insensitive to pH changes
a: Bind 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate (2,3-BPG) less avidly than beta chains, resulting in higher oxygen affinity

q: The two types of gamma-globin chains present in HbF are Gγ and Aγ, which differ by which amino acid at position 136?
o: Gγ has glycine and Aγ has alanine at position 136
Gγ has alanine and Aγ has glycine at position 136
Gγ has valine and Aγ has leucine at position 136
Gγ has serine and Aγ has threonine at position 136
a: Gγ has glycine and Aγ has alanine at position 136

q: Hydroxyurea increases HbF production in sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia by which primary mechanism?
o: Cytotoxic stress on erythroid progenitors recruits stress erythropoiesis pathways preferentially expressing gamma-globin
Direct binding to the gamma-globin promoter activating HbF transcription
Demethylating the gamma-globin gene CpG promoter islands
a: Cytotoxic stress on erythroid progenitors recruits stress erythropoiesis pathways preferentially expressing gamma-globin

q: HPFH-1 (African type) is a large deletion of approximately how many kilobases from the beta-globin gene cluster?
o: ~105 kb deletion removing the delta and beta-globin genes
~7 kb deletion removing only the beta-globin gene
~200 kb deletion removing the entire beta-globin cluster
~3.7 kb deletion removing one alpha-globin gene
a: ~105 kb deletion removing the delta and beta-globin genes

q: Greek HPFH (non-deletional HPFH) is caused by a point mutation at position -117 of the Aγ-globin promoter that creates a binding site for which transcription factor, activating HbF expression?
o: GATA-1 (creating a de novo GATA binding site)
a: GATA-1 (creating a de novo GATA binding site)

q: British HPFH is caused by a point mutation at position -198 of the Aγ-globin promoter that creates a binding site for which ubiquitous transcription factor?
The CRISPR-Cas9 gene therapy Exagamglogene autotemcel (Casgevy) approved for sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia mimics the effect of HPFH by:
o: Disrupting the BCL11A erythroid enhancer in autologous HSCs to reactivate gamma-globin (HbF) expression
Introducing an HPFH-like large deletion in the beta-globin gene cluster
Correcting the specific disease-causing beta-globin point mutation
Inserting a functional gamma-globin transgene to replace defective beta-globin
a: Disrupting the BCL11A erythroid enhancer in autologous HSCs to reactivate gamma-globin (HbF) expression

q: Which of the following clinical observations provided the earliest evidence that HbF induction could be therapeutically beneficial in sickle cell disease?
o: Infants with HbSS and HPFH or high HbF had fewer crises, and Saudi/Indian haplotype patients with naturally high HbF had milder disease
Patients receiving blood transfusions had reduced sickling due to dilution of HbS
Neonates with sickle cell disease were asymptomatic at birth when predominantly HbF
Patients in malaria-endemic regions had higher HbF due to selective pressure
a: Infants with HbSS and HPFH or high HbF had fewer crises, and Saudi/Indian haplotype patients with naturally high HbF had milder disease

q: What is the minimum level of HbF per red blood cell (pancellular distribution) required to significantly inhibit HbS polymerization and prevent sickling?
o: ~10 pg of HbF per cell (approximately 20–30% HbF per cell)
~1 pg of HbF per cell (approximately 2–3% HbF per cell)
~20 pg of HbF per cell (approximately 50% HbF per cell)
Any detectable amount of HbF is sufficient to prevent polymerization
a: ~10 pg of HbF per cell (approximately 20–30% HbF per cell)

q: LRF (Leukemia/Lymphoma-Related Factor, also known as ZBTB7A) is a recently identified HbF repressor that acts:
o: Independently of BCL11A to directly repress gamma-globin gene expression in adult erythroid cells
Upstream of BCL11A to activate its repressor function
By methylating gamma-globin gene CpG islands
a: Independently of BCL11A to directly repress gamma-globin gene expression in adult erythroid cells

q: Which of the following naturally occurring human genetic variants in the BCL11A gene is associated with elevated HbF and provides protection against the severity of sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia?
o: Single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the BCL11A erythroid-specific enhancer region at chromosome 2p16
Heterozygous deletion of the entire BCL11A gene locus
Gain-of-function mutations in the BCL11A coding sequence
Trinucleotide repeat expansions within BCL11A intron 2
a: Single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the BCL11A erythroid-specific enhancer region at chromosome 2p16

q: Which of the following best defines the peripheral blood smear (PBS) as a diagnostic specimen in hematopathology?
o: A thin film of anticoagulated peripheral blood spread on a glass slide, stained, and examined microscopically for cellular morphology and differentials
A centrifuged blood sample used exclusively to measure hematocrit and plasma proteins
A buffy coat preparation used only for leukocyte enumeration by flow cytometry
A dried blood spot collected on filter paper for neonatal screening purposes only
a: A thin film of anticoagulated peripheral blood spread on a glass slide, stained, and examined microscopically for cellular morphology and differentials

q: The Wright-Giemsa stain used for peripheral blood smears and bone marrow aspirates is classified as which type of stain?
o: Romanowsky-type stain combining acidic eosin and basic methylene blue dyes
a: Romanowsky-type stain combining acidic eosin and basic methylene blue dyes

q: A bone marrow aspirate specimen is optimally collected from which anatomical site in adult patients?
o: Posterior superior iliac spine (PSIS) of the iliac crest
a: Posterior superior iliac spine (PSIS) of the iliac crest

q: What is the primary diagnostic advantage of a bone marrow trephine biopsy over a bone marrow aspirate?
o: It preserves tissue architecture, cellularity, and trabecular bone relationships that are destroyed in aspirate preparation
It provides better cytological detail of individual cell morphology
It allows flow cytometric immunophenotyping of marrow cells
It yields more material for cytogenetic analysis
a: It preserves tissue architecture, cellularity, and trabecular bone relationships that are destroyed in aspirate preparation

q: A 'dry tap' during bone marrow aspiration, where no marrow particles are obtained, is most commonly associated with which pathological condition?
o: Primary myelofibrosis with extensive reticulin fibrosis replacing marrow spaces
Acute myeloid leukemia with >90% blast infiltration
Multiple myeloma with diffuse plasma cell infiltration
a: Primary myelofibrosis with extensive reticulin fibrosis replacing marrow spaces

q: The normal bone marrow cellularity in a healthy adult is approximately:
o: 30–70% (cellularity decreasing with age, roughly 100 minus age as a rule of thumb)
50–60% (constant throughout adult life regardless of age
a: 30–70% (cellularity decreasing with age, roughly 100 minus age as a rule of thumb)

q: Which anticoagulant is preferred for collecting blood specimens destined for complete blood count (CBC) and peripheral blood smear preparation?
o: Sodium citrate — light blue top tube
Sodium fluoride/potassium oxalate — grey top tube

q: Lymph node biopsy specimens in hematopathology are ideally processed by which combination of methods to allow morphological, immunophenotypic, and molecular analysis?
o: Fresh tissue divided for formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) histology, flow cytometry, cytogenetics, and molecular studies
Formalin fixation of the entire specimen for FFPE histology only
Immediate snap-freezing of the entire specimen in liquid nitrogen
a: Fresh tissue divided for formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) histology, flow cytometry, cytogenetics, and molecular studies

q: Which of the following is the most appropriate fixative for lymph node and bone marrow trephine biopsy specimens intended for routine histopathological processing?
Touch imprint (touch prep) cytology from a freshly cut lymph node biopsy provides which diagnostic advantage over histological sections?
o: Superior nuclear and cytoplasmic detail of individual cells comparable to aspirate cytology while awaiting tissue processing
Better assessment of tissue architecture and nodal compartments
Provides material for FISH cytogenetic analysis
a: Superior nuclear and cytoplasmic detail of individual cells comparable to aspirate cytology while awaiting tissue processing

q: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) specimens submitted to hematopathology for suspected CNS lymphoma or leukemia must be processed within what timeframe to preserve cellular morphology?
o: Within 1 hour of collection due to rapid cell lysis and degeneration in CSF
Within 72 hours if mixed with tissue culture medium
Within 6 hours regardless of storage conditions
a: Within 1 hour of collection due to rapid cell lysis and degeneration in CSF

q: Which specialized cytocentrifugation technique is used to concentrate and deposit cells from low-cellularity specimens like CSF onto glass slides for morphological examination?
Fine needle aspiration (FNA) cytology of a lymph node has which major limitation compared to core needle or excisional biopsy?
o: Loss of tissue architecture making classification of lymphomas requiring architectural patterns unreliable
Inability to obtain sufficient cells for cytological examination
Higher risk of serious complications including bleeding and infection
Inability to perform immunocytochemistry on FNA material
a: Loss of tissue architecture making classification of lymphomas requiring architectural patterns unreliable

q: The minimum recommended length of a bone marrow trephine biopsy core for adequate hematopathological assessment according to the British Committee for Standards in Haematology (BCSH) guidelines is:
Decalcification of bone marrow trephine biopsy cores before histological sectioning is performed to:
o: Remove calcium salts from mineralized bone to allow sectioning without fragmenting the tissue
Fix and preserve cellular antigens for immunohistochemistry
Remove fat cells to improve visualization of hematopoietic elements
a: Remove calcium salts from mineralized bone to allow sectioning without fragmenting the tissue

q: Which decalcification method best preserves antigenicity for immunohistochemistry (IHC) in bone marrow trephine biopsies?
o: Strong acid decalcification (hydrochloric or nitric acid)

q: Flow cytometry specimens in hematopathology require which type of specimen preparation to generate single-cell suspensions for immunophenotyping?
o: Fresh anticoagulated blood, bone marrow aspirate, or mechanically disaggregated fresh tissue in isotonic buffer
Decalcified bone marrow trephine biopsy sections
a: Fresh anticoagulated blood, bone marrow aspirate, or mechanically disaggregated fresh tissue in isotonic buffer

q: For conventional cytogenetic karyotyping (G-banding) from bone marrow specimens, cells must be:
o: Viable and actively dividing — specimens must be fresh and submitted immediately in transport medium without fixation
Fixed in 10% formalin to preserve chromosomal integrity
Decalcified and paraffin-embedded before chromosome extraction
a: Viable and actively dividing — specimens must be fresh and submitted immediately in transport medium without fixation

q: Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH) for hematological malignancies can be performed on which of the following specimen types?
o: Both fresh interphase cells AND formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue sections
Only fresh peripheral blood lymphocytes stimulated with phytohemagglutinin
a: Both fresh interphase cells AND formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue sections

q: Pleural fluid submitted for hematopathological evaluation to exclude lymphomatous effusion should be collected in which container and processed how?
o: EDTA or heparin anticoagulated container; processed fresh for cytology, flow cytometry, and cell block preparation
Plain (no additive) red top tube; allowed to clot before centrifugation
Formalin container; fixed immediately before transport
Sterile container with no anticoagulant; processed within 48 hours
a: EDTA or heparin anticoagulated container; processed fresh for cytology, flow cytometry, and cell block preparation

q: A cell block preparation from effusion fluids (pleural, peritoneal, pericardial) in hematopathology offers which advantage over cytospin preparations alone?
o: Allows immunohistochemistry (IHC) on tissue-like sections, preserving cell clusters and architectural relationships
Provides better single-cell morphological detail than cytospin
Allows flow cytometric immunophenotyping of effusion cells
Enables conventional cytogenetic karyotyping from effusion cells
a: Allows immunohistochemistry (IHC) on tissue-like sections, preserving cell clusters and architectural relationships

q: Spleen specimens received in hematopathology are most commonly evaluated for which clinical indications?
o: Staging and diagnosis of lymphomas, myeloproliferative neoplasms, storage disorders, and hemolytic anemias
Diagnosis of acute leukemias requiring splenectomy for disease control
Assessment of iron stores and erythropoiesis exclusively
Evaluation of splenic trauma and vascular lesions only
a: Staging and diagnosis of lymphomas, myeloproliferative neoplasms, storage disorders, and hemolytic anemias

q: Vitreous fluid biopsy submitted for suspected intraocular lymphoma (vitreoretinal lymphoma) requires which urgent processing consideration?
o: Immediate processing within 24 hours due to rapid cell degeneration; cytospin, flow cytometry, and IL-10/IL-6 ratio measurement
Formalin fixation immediately after collection for routine histology
Freezing at −20°C for later batch processing with other specimens
Standard processing within 72 hours as with other body fluids
a: Immediate processing within 24 hours due to rapid cell degeneration; cytospin, flow cytometry, and IL-10/IL-6 ratio measurement

q: Next-generation sequencing (NGS) panels for hematological malignancies can be performed on which of the following specimen types?
o: FFPE tissue, fresh/frozen tissue, peripheral blood, bone marrow aspirate, and cell-free DNA from plasma
Fresh tissue only due to DNA fragmentation in fixed specimens
Peripheral blood only for somatic mutation detection
Bone marrow aspirate only, as other specimens yield insufficient DNA
a: FFPE tissue, fresh/frozen tissue, peripheral blood, bone marrow aspirate, and cell-free DNA from plasma

q: Minimal residual disease (MRD) monitoring in leukemia and lymphoma is most sensitively performed using which combination of specimen types and methodologies?
o: Peripheral blood or bone marrow aspirate analyzed by multiparameter flow cytometry (MFC) or PCR/NGS for clonal markers
Lymph node biopsy analyzed by conventional histology and IHC
Bone marrow trephine biopsy evaluated by H&E morphology alone
Peripheral blood smear examined by light microscopy for residual blasts
a: Peripheral blood or bone marrow aspirate analyzed by multiparameter flow cytometry (MFC) or PCR/NGS for clonal markers

q: The buffy coat layer obtained after centrifugation of anticoagulated blood is enriched for which cellular components?
o: Leukocytes and platelets (the white layer between the red cell pellet and plasma)
Circulating tumor cells and cell-free DNA
a: Leukocytes and platelets (the white layer between the red cell pellet and plasma)

q: Which special stain is used on bone marrow aspirate smears to quantify iron stores and identify ring sideroblasts?
Reticulin silver stain (Gordon and Sweet's or Gomori's method) on bone marrow trephine biopsy sections is graded 0 to 3 (MF-0 to MF-3) to assess:
o: Degree of marrow fibrosis, critical for diagnosing and grading primary myelofibrosis and other fibrotic marrow conditions
Proportion of blast cells in the marrow
Degree of plasma cell infiltration in myeloma
a: Degree of marrow fibrosis, critical for diagnosing and grading primary myelofibrosis and other fibrotic marrow conditions

q: Liquid biopsy in hematopathology refers to the analysis of which components from peripheral blood for diagnosis and monitoring of hematological malignancies?
o: Cell-free circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA), circulating tumor cells (CTCs), and exosomes from plasma
Leukocytes from the buffy coat for conventional cytogenetics
Platelet-poor plasma for coagulation factor assays
Serum protein electrophoresis for paraprotein detection only
a: Cell-free circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA), circulating tumor cells (CTCs), and exosomes from plasma

q: The optimal needle gauge for performing a bone marrow trephine biopsy to obtain a core of adequate length and quality is:
o: 11-gauge trephine needle (e.g., Jamshidi or Islam needle)
8-gauge core needle used in solid organ biopsies
a: 11-gauge trephine needle (e.g., Jamshidi or Islam needle)

q: Which of the following is the most important pre-analytical variable affecting the quality of bone marrow aspirate smear interpretation in hematopathology?
o: Hemodilution of the aspirate with peripheral blood due to excessive aspiration volume, diluting marrow particles and cells
Use of EDTA anticoagulant instead of heparin for aspirate collection
Delay of more than 2 hours before smear preparation
Use of glass slides instead of poly-L-lysine coated slides for smear preparation
a: Hemodilution of the aspirate with peripheral blood due to excessive aspiration volume, diluting marrow particles and cells

q: Immunohistochemistry (IHC) in hematopathology detects specific antigens in tissue sections by utilizing which fundamental principle?
o: Antigen-antibody binding visualized by enzyme-linked chromogenic reactions (e.g., DAB) or fluorescent labels on formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded sections
Radioactive isotope-labeled antibodies detected by autoradiography on frozen sections
Lectin-carbohydrate binding reactions visualized by silver enhancement on decalcified bone marrow
Complement fixation assays measuring antibody-antigen binding on fresh tissue cryosections
a: Antigen-antibody binding visualized by enzyme-linked chromogenic reactions (e.g., DAB) or fluorescent labels on formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded sections

q: Which of the following antigen retrieval methods is most commonly used to unmask epitopes in formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue sections for immunohistochemistry?
o: Heat-induced epitope retrieval (HIER) using citrate buffer (pH 6.0) or EDTA buffer (pH 9.0) in a pressure cooker or microwave
Enzymatic retrieval using proteinase K or trypsin digestion only
Acid-induced retrieval using hydrochloric acid treatment
No retrieval needed — formalin fixation preserves all epitopes intact
a: Heat-induced epitope retrieval (HIER) using citrate buffer (pH 6.0) or EDTA buffer (pH 9.0) in a pressure cooker or microwave

q: Flow cytometry immunophenotyping in hematopathology identifies and quantifies cell populations based on which principles?
o: Light scatter properties (cell size and granularity) combined with fluorochrome-conjugated antibody binding to specific surface and intracellular antigens
Enzymatic activity of myeloperoxidase and esterase detected by chromogenic substrates
Sedimentation rate of cells in density gradient centrifugation correlating with antigen expression
Radiofrequency impedance changes as cells pass through an aperture measuring antigen density
a: Light scatter properties (cell size and granularity) combined with fluorochrome-conjugated antibody binding to specific surface and intracellular antigens

q: In flow cytometry, forward scatter (FSC) and side scatter (SSC) parameters measure which cellular properties respectively?
o: FSC measures cell size (volume); SSC measures internal complexity and granularity
FSC measures cell granularity; SSC measures cell surface antigen density
FSC measures nuclear size; SSC measures cytoplasmic volume
FSC measures membrane integrity; SSC measures mitochondrial content
a: FSC measures cell size (volume); SSC measures internal complexity and granularity

q: Conventional cytogenetic karyotyping (G-banding) in hematological malignancies requires cells to be arrested in which phase of the cell cycle for chromosome analysis?
o: Metaphase — when chromosomes are maximally condensed and visible as distinct structures
Interphase — when chromosomes are decondensed within the intact nucleus
S-phase — when DNA replication allows visualization of sister chromatids
G1 phase — when chromosomes begin condensation preparatory to division
a: Metaphase — when chromosomes are maximally condensed and visible as distinct structures

q: Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH) in hematopathology uses fluorescently labeled DNA probes to detect which types of chromosomal abnormalities?
o: Translocations, deletions, amplifications, and gene fusions at the single-cell level in both metaphase and interphase nuclei
Only numerical chromosome abnormalities (aneuploidy) detectable by chromosome counting
Point mutations in oncogenes requiring single nucleotide resolution
Only large structural rearrangements visible on G-banded karyotype
a: Translocations, deletions, amplifications, and gene fusions at the single-cell level in both metaphase and interphase nuclei

q: Which FISH probe design uses two differentially colored probes flanking a chromosomal breakpoint such that a translocation is detected by separation of the two signals?
Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) for BCR-ABL1 transcript quantification in CML monitoring uses which specific technique to measure transcript levels with high sensitivity?
o: Quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) measuring BCR-ABL1/ABL1 ratio on the International Scale (IS)
Conventional end-point PCR with gel electrophoresis band intensity quantification
Digital droplet PCR (ddPCR) exclusively used for all CML monitoring
Nested PCR with Southern blot hybridization for transcript detection
a: Quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) measuring BCR-ABL1/ABL1 ratio on the International Scale (IS)

q: Sanger sequencing, used historically for mutation detection in hematological malignancies, has a sensitivity limit of approximately:
o: ~15–20% mutant allele frequency — unable to detect mutations present in fewer than 15–20% of cells
~1% mutant allele frequency — highly sensitive for minor clones
~50% mutant allele frequency — only detects homozygous mutations
~0.01% mutant allele frequency — the most sensitive sequencing method available
a: ~15–20% mutant allele frequency — unable to detect mutations present in fewer than 15–20% of cells

q: Next-generation sequencing (NGS) panels in hematopathology offer which major advantage over single-gene testing approaches?
o: Simultaneous detection of hundreds of mutations across multiple clinically relevant genes in a single assay with high sensitivity (~1–5% VAF)
Higher sensitivity than digital droplet PCR for single target detection
Ability to detect chromosomal translocations more accurately than FISH
Lower cost per specimen than single-gene Sanger sequencing for one target
a: Simultaneous detection of hundreds of mutations across multiple clinically relevant genes in a single assay with high sensitivity (~1–5% VAF)

q: Clonality assessment in B-cell lymphoproliferative disorders by PCR or NGS detects clonal populations by demonstrating:
o: A dominant rearrangement of immunoglobulin heavy chain (IgH) or light chain (IgK/IgL) genes producing a monoclonal peak or sequence
Overexpression of BCL-2 protein by immunohistochemistry
Deletion of the CDKN2A tumor suppressor gene by FISH
Aneuploidy of chromosome 12 detected by G-banding karyotype
a: A dominant rearrangement of immunoglobulin heavy chain (IgH) or light chain (IgK/IgL) genes producing a monoclonal peak or sequence

q: T-cell clonality in suspected T-cell lymphoproliferative disorders is assessed by PCR-based analysis of rearrangements in which gene?
o: T-cell receptor gamma (TCRγ) or T-cell receptor beta (TCRβ) gene rearrangements
Immunoglobulin kappa light chain gene rearrangements
MYC gene translocation by FISH analysis
a: T-cell receptor gamma (TCRγ) or T-cell receptor beta (TCRβ) gene rearrangements

q: Cytochemical staining with myeloperoxidase (MPO) in hematopathology is used primarily to:
o: Confirm myeloid differentiation of blasts — MPO positivity (≥3% blasts) indicates myeloid lineage in AML diagnosis
Identify lymphoid blasts in ALL as MPO-positive
Detect iron stores in marrow macrophages
Stain plasma cells in myeloma for prognostic assessment
a: Confirm myeloid differentiation of blasts — MPO positivity (≥3% blasts) indicates myeloid lineage in AML diagnosis

q: The non-specific esterase (NSE) cytochemical stain is characteristically positive in which hematological malignancy, demonstrating monocytic differentiation?
o: Acute monoblastic/monocytic leukemia (AML-M4/M5) — NSE strongly positive and inhibited by sodium fluoride
Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) — NSE diffusely positive
Acute myeloid leukemia with maturation (AML-M2) — NSE focally positive
Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) — NSE granularly positive
a: Acute monoblastic/monocytic leukemia (AML-M4/M5) — NSE strongly positive and inhibited by sodium fluoride

q: Periodic Acid-Schiff (PAS) stain in hematopathology detects glycogen and complex carbohydrates. Coarse block-like PAS positivity in lymphoblasts is characteristically associated with which condition?
o: B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (B-ALL) — block PAS positivity in lymphoblasts
Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) — diffuse PAS positivity in myeloblasts
Erythroleukemia (AML-M6) — PAS positivity in abnormal erythroblasts
Multiple myeloma — PAS positivity in plasma cells
a: B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (B-ALL) — block PAS positivity in lymphoblasts

q: Tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase (TRAP) cytochemical stain is pathognomonic of which hematological malignancy?
o: Hairy cell leukemia (HCL) — neoplastic B-cells show strong TRAP positivity resistant to L-tartrate inhibition
Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) — TRAP positivity distinguishes CLL from other lymphomas
Splenic marginal zone lymphoma (SMZL) — TRAP positivity in villous lymphocytes
T-cell prolymphocytic leukemia (T-PLL) — TRAP positivity confirms T-cell derivation
a: Hairy cell leukemia (HCL) — neoplastic B-cells show strong TRAP positivity resistant to L-tartrate inhibition

q: Chromogenic in situ hybridization (CISH) in hematopathology detects EBV (Epstein-Barr virus) in lymphoid tissues using probes targeting which viral RNA?
o: EBV-encoded small RNAs (EBERs) — highly expressed in latently infected cells and detectable by EBER ISH
EBV late lytic cycle mRNAs encoding viral capsid antigens
EBV DNA integrated into host chromosomes
EBV microRNAs encoded in the BART region
a: EBV-encoded small RNAs (EBERs) — highly expressed in latently infected cells and detectable by EBER ISH

q: Comparative Genomic Hybridization (CGH) and chromosomal microarray analysis in hematopathology detect which types of genomic abnormalities?
