o: Copy number variations (gains and losses) across the entire genome at high resolution, including sub-microscopic deletions and amplifications undetectable by conventional karyotype
Balanced translocations and inversions throughout the genome
Point mutations and single nucleotide variants in coding genes
Epigenetic methylation changes at CpG islands genome-wide
a: Copy number variations (gains and losses) across the entire genome at high resolution, including sub-microscopic deletions and amplifications undetectable by conventional karyotype

q: Digital pathology and whole slide imaging (WSI) in hematopathology offers which primary advantage in diagnostic practice?
o: Remote consultation, AI-assisted analysis, image archiving, and quantitative assessment of IHC staining without glass slide handling
Higher magnification resolution than conventional light microscopy for cytological detail
Replacement of all special stains with digital stain simulation algorithms
Real-time 3D reconstruction of bone marrow architecture from 2D sections
a: Remote consultation, AI-assisted analysis, image archiving, and quantitative assessment of IHC staining without glass slide handling

q: Mass spectrometry-based proteomics in hematopathology is increasingly used for which specific clinical application?
o: Identification and quantification of monoclonal immunoglobulins (M-proteins) in plasma cell disorders with higher sensitivity than serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP)
Real-time intraoperative diagnosis of lymph node biopsies
Detection of chromosomal translocations in acute leukemias
Quantification of minimal residual disease in ALL by protein expression profiling
a: Identification and quantification of monoclonal immunoglobulins (M-proteins) in plasma cell disorders with higher sensitivity than serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP)

q: Multiparameter flow cytometry (MFC) for minimal residual disease (MRD) detection in B-ALL can achieve sensitivity levels of:
o: 1 leukemic cell in 10,000–100,000 normal cells (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ sensitivity)
1 leukemic cell in 100 normal cells (10⁻² sensitivity)
1 leukemic cell in 1,000 normal cells (10⁻³ sensitivity)
1 leukemic cell in 1,000,000 normal cells (10⁻⁶ sensitivity)
a: 1 leukemic cell in 10,000–100,000 normal cells (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ sensitivity)

q: Laser capture microdissection (LCM) in hematopathology allows which specialized analysis?
o: Precise isolation of specific cell populations or microanatomical regions from tissue sections for downstream molecular analysis (PCR, sequencing, proteomics)
High-throughput automated counting of marrow blast percentages
Real-time visualization of chromosome dynamics during mitosis
Three-dimensional reconstruction of lymph node architecture from serial sections
a: Precise isolation of specific cell populations or microanatomical regions from tissue sections for downstream molecular analysis (PCR, sequencing, proteomics)

q: RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) transcriptomics in hematopathology provides which diagnostic information beyond standard DNA mutation panels?
o: Gene expression profiling, novel fusion transcript detection, alternative splicing variants, and pathway activation analysis
Detection of somatic mutations with higher sensitivity than DNA-based NGS panels
Quantification of chromosomal copy number variations genome-wide
Identification of epigenetic methylation patterns at CpG islands
a: Gene expression profiling, novel fusion transcript detection, alternative splicing variants, and pathway activation analysis

q: Electron microscopy (EM) in hematopathology, though rarely used today, was historically essential for diagnosing which condition by demonstrating characteristic ultrastructural features?
o: Acute megakaryoblastic leukemia (AML-M7) — platelet peroxidase activity visible only by EM; and Langerhans cell histiocytosis — Birbeck granules
Chronic lymphocytic leukemia — ribosome-lamella complexes in prolymphocytes
Hairy cell leukemia — TRAP enzyme localization in lysosomes
Acute promyelocytic leukemia — Auer rods confirmed by EM morphology
a: Acute megakaryoblastic leukemia (AML-M7) — platelet peroxidase activity visible only by EM; and Langerhans cell histiocytosis — Birbeck granules

q: Single-cell sequencing technologies applied to hematological malignancies provide which unique insight unavailable from bulk tissue analysis?
o: Clonal architecture, tumor heterogeneity, and evolutionary relationships between subclones at single-cell genomic and transcriptomic resolution
Higher sensitivity for detecting low-frequency mutations than bulk NGS panels
Better assessment of tissue microenvironment than immunohistochemistry
More accurate quantification of chromosome copy number than microarray CGH
a: Clonal architecture, tumor heterogeneity, and evolutionary relationships between subclones at single-cell genomic and transcriptomic resolution

q: The EuroFlow consortium standardized 8-color antibody panels for flow cytometric immunophenotyping of hematological malignancies to achieve which primary goal?
o: Harmonized, reproducible MRD detection and disease classification across different laboratories and instruments worldwide
Development of panels exclusively for T-cell malignancy diagnosis
Creation of cost-effective 4-color panels for resource-limited settings
Standardization of FISH probe selection for chromosomal abnormality detection
a: Harmonized, reproducible MRD detection and disease classification across different laboratories and instruments worldwide

q: Methylation profiling by whole-genome bisulfite sequencing or methylation arrays in hematopathology is most valuable for which diagnostic application?
o: Classification of CNS lymphomas and ambiguous hematological malignancies where morphology and standard IHC are inconclusive
Detection of BCR-ABL1 kinase domain mutations in TKI-resistant CML
Quantification of HbF levels in hemoglobinopathies
Assessment of bone marrow fibrosis grade in myeloproliferative neoplasms
a: Classification of CNS lymphomas and ambiguous hematological malignancies where morphology and standard IHC are inconclusive

q: Digital droplet PCR (ddPCR) in hematopathology offers which key advantage over quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) for MRD monitoring?
o: Absolute quantification without need for standard curves, higher precision at very low target concentrations, and sensitivity reaching 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁶
Higher throughput allowing more targets per assay than qRT-PCR
Ability to detect unknown mutations without prior sequence knowledge
Lower cost per reaction making it preferred for routine monitoring
a: Absolute quantification without need for standard curves, higher precision at very low target concentrations, and sensitivity reaching 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁶

q: Spatial transcriptomics applied to hematopathology tissue sections provides which novel diagnostic dimension?
o: Gene expression data mapped to precise anatomical locations within tissue, revealing tumor microenvironment interactions and spatial clonal heterogeneity
Higher resolution chromosome imaging than conventional FISH on tissue sections
Automated blast counting integrated with morphological diagnosis
Real-time assessment of apoptosis and cell death in lymphoma tissue
a: Gene expression data mapped to precise anatomical locations within tissue, revealing tumor microenvironment interactions and spatial clonal heterogeneity

q: Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning algorithms in hematopathology have demonstrated utility in which of the following diagnostic tasks?
o: Automated white blood cell differential counting, blast identification on peripheral blood smears, and lymphoma subtype classification on digitized histological sections
Replacing flow cytometry for immunophenotyping of leukemia and lymphoma
Performing molecular clonality analysis from H&E-stained sections without ancillary testing
Generating karyotype reports directly from whole slide images of bone marrow biopsies
a: Automated white blood cell differential counting, blast identification on peripheral blood smears, and lymphoma subtype classification on digitized histological sections

q: The International Consensus Classification (ICC) 2022 and WHO 5th Edition 2022 classifications of hematological malignancies both emphasize integration of which combination of diagnostic modalities for accurate disease classification?
o: Morphology, immunophenotype (IHC/flow cytometry), cytogenetics (karyotype/FISH), and molecular genetics (PCR/NGS) — the integrated 'MICM' approach
Morphology and IHC alone are sufficient for all WHO 2022 diagnoses
Clinical presentation and treatment response as primary diagnostic criteria
Flow cytometry immunophenotyping alone provides definitive classification in all cases
a: Morphology, immunophenotype (IHC/flow cytometry), cytogenetics (karyotype/FISH), and molecular genetics (PCR/NGS) — the integrated 'MICM' approach

q: The first widely accepted classification system for acute leukemias, based primarily on morphology and cytochemistry, was the:
In modern hematopathology, the 'Integrated Diagnosis' combines morphology with which of the following?
Which major group in the WHO classification includes Polycythemia Vera, Essential Thrombocythemia, and Myelofibrosis?
According to the FAB system, what was the minimum blast percentage required to diagnose Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML)?
In the 5th Edition WHO classification, which genetic rearrangement defines a specific category of AML regardless of blast count?
Chronic Myelomonocytic Leukemia (CMML) is classified under which hybrid group?
Which category of B-cell neoplasms includes Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) and Small Lymphocytic Lymphoma (SLL)?
The 'Starry Sky' pattern is a classic morphological hallmark of which disease group?
Hodgkin Lymphoma is pathologically divided into two main groups: Classic Hodgkin Lymphoma and:
Which translocation is the defining feature for the classification of Mantle Cell Lymphoma?
Plasma cell neoplasms are classified based on the production of a monoclonal protein. Which condition is considered the precursor to Multiple Myeloma?
Which specific mutation is required for the classification of Hairy Cell Leukemia in the majority of cases?
The 'Rye Classification' was historically used to subclassify which disease?
In the WHO classification, 'Blast Crisis' in CML is pathologically defined as a blast count of at least:
Which cell surface marker is most commonly used to identify 'blasts' of myeloid or lymphoid origin in flow cytometry?
Follicular Lymphoma is classified primarily by the presence of which translocation involving the BCL2 gene?
Which group of disorders is characterized by cytopenias and dysplastic changes in one or more cell lines?
Which aggressive T-cell lymphoma is characteristically associated with HTLV-1 infection?
Myeloid and lymphoid neoplasms with eosinophilia are often defined by rearrangements in which genes?
What is the defining mutation for Waldenström Macroglobulinemia in the vast majority of patients?
A diagnosis of Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia (APL) is classified as a medical emergency due to the high risk of:
Which of the following is a 'mature T-cell and NK-cell neoplasm' that primarily involves the skin?
The WHO 5th Edition moved away from using which term to describe certain low-grade MDS cases?
Reed-Sternberg cells are the diagnostic feature for which classification group?
In the classification of Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), which translocation is most common in pediatric B-ALL and carries a favorable prognosis?
Which neoplastic group is characterized by the 'Birbeck granule' on electron microscopy?
Extranodal Marginal Zone Lymphoma is also frequently referred to by which acronym?
Which classification group includes neoplasms arising after organ or hematopoietic stem cell transplantation?
The Ann Arbor staging system is primarily used to describe the extent of which disease group?
In the modern era, the classification of AML with 'favorable' risk often involves mutations in which two genes?
Which of the following best defines Congenital Bone Marrow Failure Syndromes (CBMFS)?
o: Inherited disorders characterized by impaired hematopoiesis causing single or multilineage cytopenias, often associated with physical anomalies and increased cancer predisposition
Acquired aplastic anemia developing in the first year of life due to immune-mediated marrow destruction
Chromosomal aneuploidy syndromes causing transient neonatal pancytopenia resolving spontaneously
Nutritional deficiencies of vitamin B12 and folate causing megaloblastic bone marrow failure in infancy
a: Inherited disorders characterized by impaired hematopoiesis causing single or multilineage cytopenias, often associated with physical anomalies and increased cancer predisposition

q: Fanconi Anemia (FA) is caused by mutations in genes encoding proteins involved in which fundamental cellular pathway?
o: DNA interstrand crosslink (ICL) repair — the Fanconi Anemia/BRCA DNA damage repair pathway
Telomere maintenance and elongation by telomerase
a: DNA interstrand crosslink (ICL) repair — the Fanconi Anemia/BRCA DNA damage repair pathway

q: Which of the following is the most common complementation group (gene) responsible for Fanconi Anemia?
The gold standard diagnostic test for Fanconi Anemia is based on demonstrating cellular hypersensitivity to which clastogenic agents?
o: Diepoxybutane (DEB) and mitomycin C (MMC) — causing excessive chromosomal breakage in FA cells
Hydroxyurea and aphidicolin — causing S-phase arrest in FA cells
Ionizing radiation — causing double-strand breaks repaired abnormally in FA cells
Ultraviolet light (UV) — causing pyrimidine dimer accumulation in FA cells
a: Diepoxybutane (DEB) and mitomycin C (MMC) — causing excessive chromosomal breakage in FA cells

q: Which of the following physical anomalies is most classically associated with Fanconi Anemia?
o: Radial ray defects (absent or hypoplastic thumbs/radii), café-au-lait spots, short stature, and microcephaly
Absent radius with thrombocytopenia but preserved thumbs
Webbed neck, shield chest, and short fourth metacarpals
Cleft palate, micrognathia, and cardiac septal defects
a: Radial ray defects (absent or hypoplastic thumbs/radii), café-au-lait spots, short stature, and microcephaly

q: Adults with previously undiagnosed Fanconi Anemia who present de novo most commonly present with which manifestation?
o: Squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck or gynecological tract, or myelodysplastic syndrome/AML
Severe aplastic anemia indistinguishable from acquired aplastic anemia
Isolated thrombocytopenia mimicking immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP)
Diamond-Blackfan anemia-like pure red cell aplasia
a: Squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck or gynecological tract, or myelodysplastic syndrome/AML

q: Diamond-Blackfan Anemia (DBA) is classified as which type of bone marrow failure based on the affected cell lineage?
o: Pure red cell aplasia — selective failure of erythroid progenitors with normal myeloid and megakaryocyte development
Selective neutropenia with preserved erythroid and platelet lineages
Selective thrombocytopenia with preserved erythroid and myeloid lineages
a: Pure red cell aplasia — selective failure of erythroid progenitors with normal myeloid and megakaryocyte development

q: The majority of Diamond-Blackfan Anemia cases are caused by mutations in genes encoding which class of proteins?
o: Ribosomal proteins (most commonly RPS19, accounting for ~25% of cases)
DNA repair proteins of the FA/BRCA pathway
Transcription factors regulating erythroid differentiation (GATA-1, KLF1)
a: Ribosomal proteins (most commonly RPS19, accounting for ~25% of cases)

q: Which laboratory finding is a characteristic biomarker elevated in Diamond-Blackfan Anemia that also serves as a marker of disease activity and response to treatment?
o: Elevated serum ferritin with low transferrin saturation
Elevated fetal hemoglobin (HbF) beyond the neonatal period
Elevated serum erythropoietin (EPO) with absent erythroid precursors on marrow

q: What is the first-line treatment for Diamond-Blackfan Anemia in children older than 1 year of age?
o: Corticosteroids (prednisolone) — approximately 80% of patients respond initially
a: Corticosteroids (prednisolone) — approximately 80% of patients respond initially

q: Dyskeratosis Congenita (DC) is caused by mutations affecting which fundamental cellular mechanism?
o: Telomere maintenance — mutations in telomerase complex components (DKC1, TERT, TERC) or shelterin proteins cause critically short telomeres
DNA interstrand crosslink repair causing chromosomal instability
Ribosome biogenesis leading to nucleolar stress and p53 activation
RNA splicing causing defective mRNA processing in hematopoietic progenitors
a: Telomere maintenance — mutations in telomerase complex components (DKC1, TERT, TERC) or shelterin proteins cause critically short telomeres

q: The classic clinical triad of Dyskeratosis Congenita consists of which three mucocutaneous features?
o: Nail dystrophy, oral leukoplakia, and abnormal skin pigmentation (lacy reticular pattern)
Café-au-lait spots, radial ray defects, and short stature
Erythematous malar rash, oral ulcers, and alopecia
Hypopigmented macules, ash-leaf spots, and periungual fibromas
a: Nail dystrophy, oral leukoplakia, and abnormal skin pigmentation (lacy reticular pattern)

q: The most severe and rapidly lethal form of Dyskeratosis Congenita, presenting in infancy with cerebellar hypoplasia and early-onset bone marrow failure, is known as:
Telomere length measurement in Dyskeratosis Congenita is most sensitively assessed by which laboratory method?
o: Multicolor flow cytometry FISH (flow-FISH) measuring telomere length in specific leukocyte subsets
Southern blot analysis of terminal restriction fragments (TRF)
Quantitative PCR (qPCR) measuring average telomere length from bulk DNA
Telomerase repeat amplification protocol (TRAP) assay measuring enzyme activity
a: Multicolor flow cytometry FISH (flow-FISH) measuring telomere length in specific leukocyte subsets

q: Severe Congenital Neutropenia (Kostmann Syndrome) most commonly results from mutations in which gene?
o: ELANE (ELA2) — encoding neutrophil elastase, causing misfolded protein accumulation and neutrophil precursor apoptosis
HAX1 — encoding a mitochondrial anti-apoptotic protein
a: ELANE (ELA2) — encoding neutrophil elastase, causing misfolded protein accumulation and neutrophil precursor apoptosis

q: Newborn screening for sickle cell disease (SCD) using heel-prick blood samples identifies which hemoglobin pattern that is diagnostic of HbSS disease?
o: FS pattern (Fetal hemoglobin + Sickle hemoglobin with absent HbA) on HPLC or isoelectric focusing
FSA pattern (HbF + HbS + HbA) indicating sickle cell trait
FA pattern (HbF + HbA with absent HbS) indicating normal newborn
FSC pattern (HbF + HbS + HbC) indicating HbSC disease
a: FS pattern (Fetal hemoglobin + Sickle hemoglobin with absent HbA) on HPLC or isoelectric focusing

q: Why are infants with sickle cell disease (HbSS) asymptomatic at birth and during the first 4–6 months of life?
o: High levels of fetal hemoglobin (HbF) inhibit HbS polymerization, protecting against sickling until HbF is replaced by HbS
Neonatal red cells have shorter lifespan preventing accumulation of sickled cells
Maternal antibodies cross the placenta protecting fetal red cells from sickling
Low oxygen demand in neonates reduces deoxygenation-induced sickling
a: High levels of fetal hemoglobin (HbF) inhibit HbS polymerization, protecting against sickling until HbF is replaced by HbS

q: According to current guidelines, penicillin prophylaxis in children with sickle cell disease (HbSS) should begin at what age and continue until what age?
o: Begin at 2–3 months of age and continue until at least 5 years of age
Begin at 6 months and continue until 3 years of age
Begin at birth and continue lifelong regardless of spleen status
Begin at 1 year and continue until 10 years of age
a: Begin at 2–3 months of age and continue until at least 5 years of age

q: The rationale for prophylactic penicillin in young children with sickle cell disease is prevention of infection by which organisms that are particularly dangerous due to functional asplenia?
o: Encapsulated bacteria — Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae type b, and Neisseria meningitidis
Gram-negative enteric organisms — Escherichia coli and Klebsiella pneumoniae
Opportunistic fungi — Aspergillus fumigatus and Candida albicans
Intracellular pathogens — Mycobacterium tuberculosis and Salmonella typhi
a: Encapsulated bacteria — Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae type b, and Neisseria meningitidis

q: The BABY HUG trial (2011) established which landmark finding regarding hydroxyurea use in infants with sickle cell disease?
o: Hydroxyurea given from 9–18 months of age reduced pain episodes, hospitalizations, dactylitis, and acute chest syndrome regardless of disease severity
Hydroxyurea was unsafe in infants under 2 years due to bone marrow suppression
Hydroxyurea only benefited infants with HbF levels below 10% at enrollment
Hydroxyurea was inferior to chronic transfusion therapy in preventing organ damage in infants
a: Hydroxyurea given from 9–18 months of age reduced pain episodes, hospitalizations, dactylitis, and acute chest syndrome regardless of disease severity

q: Dactylitis (hand-foot syndrome) in infants with sickle cell disease is caused by which pathological mechanism and represents which clinical significance?
o: Vaso-occlusion in the small bones of hands and feet causing ischemic pain — it is often the earliest clinical manifestation of SCD in infants aged 6–24 months
Osteomyelitis from Salmonella infection of the metacarpal bones in immunocompromised infants
Periosteal hemorrhage from thrombocytopenia causing swelling of the hands and feet
Marrow expansion from extramedullary hematopoiesis causing cortical bone thinning
a: Vaso-occlusion in the small bones of hands and feet causing ischemic pain — it is often the earliest clinical manifestation of SCD in infants aged 6–24 months

q: Transcranial Doppler (TCD) ultrasonography screening is recommended annually in children with HbSS disease between which ages to assess stroke risk?
o: 2–16 years of age — time-averaged mean maximum velocity (TAMMV) ≥200 cm/s identifies high stroke risk
6 months to 2 years of age — to detect early cerebrovascular disease
16–25 years of age — to identify young adults transitioning to adult care
Birth to 6 months — to detect antenatal cerebrovascular injury
a: 2–16 years of age — time-averaged mean maximum velocity (TAMMV) ≥200 cm/s identifies high stroke risk

q: The STOP trial (Stroke Prevention in Sickle Cell Anemia) demonstrated that in children with abnormal TCD velocities (≥200 cm/s), which intervention reduced the risk of first overt stroke by approximately 90%?
o: Regular chronic red blood cell transfusion therapy maintaining HbS <30% of total hemoglobin
Hydroxyurea therapy increasing HbF to above 20%
Aspirin antiplatelet therapy with low-molecular-weight heparin prophylaxis
Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation as primary stroke prevention
a: Regular chronic red blood cell transfusion therapy maintaining HbS <30% of total hemoglobin

q: The TWiTCH trial demonstrated that in children with abnormal TCD and no severe vasculopathy who were already on chronic transfusion, which alternative therapy was non-inferior for maintaining TCD velocities?
o: Hydroxyurea therapy — allowing safe transition from chronic transfusion while maintaining stroke protection
Aspirin combined with clopidogrel antiplatelet therapy
Deferasirox monotherapy for iron chelation with TCD monitoring
Allogeneic HSCT as definitive treatment replacing chronic transfusion
a: Hydroxyurea therapy — allowing safe transition from chronic transfusion while maintaining stroke protection

q: Splenic sequestration crisis in young children with sickle cell disease is defined by which clinical and laboratory criteria?
o: Acute enlargement of the spleen with a drop in hemoglobin ≥2 g/dL from baseline, reticulocytosis, and signs of hypovolemia
Spleen size >5 cm below costal margin with normal hemoglobin and absent reticulocytosis
Progressive splenomegaly over months with thrombocytopenia and mild anemia
Acute splenic pain with fever and leukocytosis without hemoglobin drop
a: Acute enlargement of the spleen with a drop in hemoglobin ≥2 g/dL from baseline, reticulocytosis, and signs of hypovolemia

q: Aplastic crisis in sickle cell disease, causing a sudden severe drop in hemoglobin with absent reticulocytes, is most commonly triggered by infection with which pathogen?
o: Parvovirus B19 — which infects and destroys erythroid progenitor cells (BFU-E and CFU-E) in the bone marrow
Streptococcus pneumoniae — causing overwhelming sepsis and marrow suppression
Epstein-Barr virus — causing lymphocytic marrow infiltration and erythroid suppression
Plasmodium falciparum — causing malarial hemolysis and marrow dyserythropoiesis
a: Parvovirus B19 — which infects and destroys erythroid progenitor cells (BFU-E and CFU-E) in the bone marrow

q: In the management of acute vaso-occlusive pain crisis in both pediatric and adult SCD patients, which of the following represents the current best practice analgesic approach?
o: Individualized multimodal analgesia with rapid opioid administration within 30–60 minutes of triage, non-opioid adjuncts, and patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) for severe pain
Parenteral NSAIDs (ketorolac) exclusively, avoiding opioids due to dependency risk
Delayed analgesia pending laboratory confirmation of sickling episode
Oral acetaminophen alone for mild-to-moderate crises with IV opioids only for severe crises after 4 hours
a: Individualized multimodal analgesia with rapid opioid administration within 30–60 minutes of triage, non-opioid adjuncts, and patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) for severe pain

q: Neonatal screening programs for beta-thalassemia major allow early intervention. Without treatment, children with beta-thalassemia major develop severe anemia by what age?
o: 6–24 months of age — when HbF production declines and absent/reduced beta-globin synthesis becomes clinically apparent
At birth — presenting with hydrops fetalis requiring intrauterine transfusion
3–5 years of age — when increased physiological demands outpace compensatory erythropoiesis
Adolescence — when rapid growth increases hemoglobin requirements beyond marrow capacity
a: 6–24 months of age — when HbF production declines and absent/reduced beta-globin synthesis becomes clinically apparent

q: The target pre-transfusion hemoglobin level for children with beta-thalassemia major on regular transfusion programs is:
o: 9–10.5 g/dL pre-transfusion — maintaining hemoglobin above this level suppresses ineffective erythropoiesis and prevents extramedullary hematopoiesis
7–8 g/dL pre-transfusion — minimizing transfusion volume and iron loading
>12 g/dL pre-transfusion — achieving near-normal hemoglobin levels
6–7 g/dL pre-transfusion — transfusing only when symptomatic
a: 9–10.5 g/dL pre-transfusion — maintaining hemoglobin above this level suppresses ineffective erythropoiesis and prevents extramedullary hematopoiesis

q: At what serum ferritin level should iron chelation therapy be initiated in transfusion-dependent thalassemia patients?
o: Serum ferritin >1000 ng/mL or after approximately 10–20 transfusion units (typically after 1 year of regular transfusion)
Serum ferritin >5000 ng/mL indicating severe iron overload with end-organ damage
Serum ferritin >500 ng/mL from the start of the first transfusion
Serum ferritin >2500 ng/mL with evidence of cardiac iron on MRI T2*
a: Serum ferritin >1000 ng/mL or after approximately 10–20 transfusion units (typically after 1 year of regular transfusion)

q: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) is the most common malignancy in children. What percentage of all childhood cancers does it represent?
o: ~25–30% of all childhood cancers making it the single most common pediatric malignancy
~10–15% of all childhood cancers second only to brain tumors
~40–50% of all childhood cancers representing the majority of pediatric malignancies
~5–8% of all childhood cancers with equal incidence to pediatric AML
a: ~25–30% of all childhood cancers making it the single most common pediatric malignancy

q: The peak incidence of B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (B-ALL) in children occurs at which age group?
o: 2–5 years of age — the 'childhood peak' associated with TEL-AML1 (ETV6-RUNX1) and hyperdiploidy
Birth to 12 months — infant ALL associated with KMT2A (MLL) rearrangements
10–14 years — adolescent ALL with Philadelphia chromosome-like features
6–9 years — middle childhood peak associated with IKZF1 deletions
a: 2–5 years of age — the 'childhood peak' associated with TEL-AML1 (ETV6-RUNX1) and hyperdiploidy

q: Which cytogenetic abnormality in pediatric B-ALL carries the most favorable prognosis and is the most common recurrent genetic alteration in childhood ALL?
o: High hyperdiploidy (51–65 chromosomes) and ETV6-RUNX1 (TEL-AML1) fusion — both conferring excellent prognosis with >90% cure rates
Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) BCR-ABL1 — associated with superior outcomes in children vs adults
KMT2A (MLL) rearrangements — favorable in children under 1 year of age
BCR-ABL1-like (Ph-like) ALL — favorable due to JAK inhibitor sensitivity
a: High hyperdiploidy (51–65 chromosomes) and ETV6-RUNX1 (TEL-AML1) fusion — both conferring excellent prognosis with >90% cure rates

q: Infant ALL (occurring in children under 12 months) carries the worst prognosis among ALL subtypes primarily because of which genetic hallmark?
o: KMT2A (MLL) gene rearrangements present in >70% of infant ALL cases, associated with pro-B immunophenotype, hyperleukocytosis, and extreme chemoresistance
Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) occurring in >80% of infants conferring imatinib resistance
Hypodiploidy (<44 chromosomes) causing profound chemotherapy resistance in infant marrow
ETV6-RUNX1 fusion occurring in infants with high relapse rate unlike older children
a: KMT2A (MLL) gene rearrangements present in >70% of infant ALL cases, associated with pro-B immunophenotype, hyperleukocytosis, and extreme chemoresistance

q: The overall cure rate for childhood B-ALL treated with contemporary risk-stratified multiagent chemotherapy protocols is approximately:
o: ~90% overall survival in high-income countries with modern risk-stratified therapy
~60–70% overall survival similar to adult ALL outcomes
~75–80% overall survival with significant late relapse rates
~95–98% overall survival across all risk groups including high-risk subgroups
a: ~90% overall survival in high-income countries with modern risk-stratified therapy

q: BCR-ABL1-like (Philadelphia chromosome-like) ALL is a high-risk subtype of B-ALL defined by which characteristic?
o: Gene expression profile resembling Ph+ ALL but lacking BCR-ABL1 fusion, driven by diverse kinase-activating alterations (JAK2, CRLF2, ABL1, PDGFRB rearrangements) targetable by TKIs
Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) present but expressing a novel BCR-ABL1 fusion protein resistant to imatinib
Overexpression of ABL1 without chromosomal translocation causing constitutive kinase activation
BCR gene rearrangement without ABL1 involvement causing a distinct signaling pathway activation
a: Gene expression profile resembling Ph+ ALL but lacking BCR-ABL1 fusion, driven by diverse kinase-activating alterations (JAK2, CRLF2, ABL1, PDGFRB rearrangements) targetable by TKIs

q: Central nervous system (CNS) prophylaxis in contemporary pediatric ALL treatment protocols has largely replaced cranial irradiation with which approach to prevent CNS relapse while avoiding neurocognitive late effects?
o: Intrathecal chemotherapy (methotrexate ± cytarabine ± hydrocortisone) combined with high-dose systemic methotrexate achieving CNS penetration
Prophylactic whole brain irradiation at reduced doses (12 Gy) for all risk groups
Intrathecal rituximab combined with standard intrathecal chemotherapy
High-dose systemic cytarabine alone providing adequate CNS sanctuary penetration
a: Intrathecal chemotherapy (methotrexate ± cytarabine ± hydrocortisone) combined with high-dose systemic methotrexate achieving CNS penetration

q: Blinatumomab (Blincyto), a bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE) approved for relapsed/refractory B-ALL, works by which mechanism?
o: Simultaneously binding CD19 on B-ALL blasts and CD3 on T cells, redirecting cytotoxic T cells to kill leukemic cells independent of MHC-peptide recognition
Blocking CD20 on B-ALL blasts activating complement-mediated cytotoxicity
Inhibiting BCR-ABL1 kinase in Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL
Delivering a cytotoxic payload (calicheamicin) directly to CD22-expressing ALL blasts
a: Simultaneously binding CD19 on B-ALL blasts and CD3 on T cells, redirecting cytotoxic T cells to kill leukemic cells independent of MHC-peptide recognition

q: Inotuzumab ozogamicin (Besylomi) is an antibody-drug conjugate approved for relapsed/refractory B-ALL that targets which antigen?
o: CD22 — delivering calicheamicin (cytotoxic antibiotic) directly to CD22-expressing ALL blasts causing DNA double-strand breaks
CD19 — delivering doxorubicin to B-ALL blasts via CD19-mediated internalization
CD33 — targeting both myeloid and lymphoid blasts in mixed phenotype leukemia
CD20 — delivering methotrexate to mature B-cell ALL with CD20 expression
a: CD22 — delivering calicheamicin (cytotoxic antibiotic) directly to CD22-expressing ALL blasts causing DNA double-strand breaks

q: T-cell ALL (T-ALL) in children differs from B-ALL in which of the following clinical and biological characteristics?
o: T-ALL presents with mediastinal mass, high WBC, older age (adolescent males), and carries intermediate-to-poor prognosis compared to favorable-risk B-ALL
T-ALL is more common in girls under 5 years and carries superior prognosis to B-ALL
T-ALL responds better to L-asparaginase therapy than B-ALL due to asparagine synthetase deficiency
T-ALL is associated with ETV6-RUNX1 fusion and hyperdiploidy identical to B-ALL
a: T-ALL presents with mediastinal mass, high WBC, older age (adolescent males), and carries intermediate-to-poor prognosis compared to favorable-risk B-ALL

q: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) in children differs from adult AML in which important prognostic molecular feature?
o: Pediatric AML has higher frequency of favorable cytogenetics (t(8;21), inv(16), t(15;17), NPM1 mutations) and lower frequency of adverse TP53 mutations compared to adult AML
Pediatric AML has higher FLT3-ITD mutation frequency conferring worse prognosis than adult AML
Adult AML has higher frequency of core-binding factor AML with better overall outcomes than pediatric AML
Pediatric AML has identical mutational landscape to adult AML with age being the only prognostic difference
a: Pediatric AML has higher frequency of favorable cytogenetics (t(8;21), inv(16), t(15;17), NPM1 mutations) and lower frequency of adverse TP53 mutations compared to adult AML

q: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) is associated with a unique spectrum of leukemia in children. Which combination correctly describes the leukemia types and their timing?
o: Transient Abnormal Myelopoiesis (TAM/TMD) in neonates resolving by 3 months; followed by risk of Myeloid Leukemia associated with Down syndrome (ML-DS/AMKL) before age 4 years
B-ALL exclusively occurring in Down syndrome children under 2 years with KMT2A rearrangements
T-ALL presenting in Down syndrome adolescents with mediastinal involvement and NOTCH1 mutations
CML-like myeloproliferative disorder in Down syndrome neonates progressing to blast crisis by age 5
a: Transient Abnormal Myelopoiesis (TAM/TMD) in neonates resolving by 3 months; followed by risk of Myeloid Leukemia associated with Down syndrome (ML-DS/AMKL) before age 4 years

q: GATA1 somatic mutations are found in virtually all cases of which pediatric leukemia uniquely associated with Down syndrome?
o: Myeloid Leukemia of Down syndrome (ML-DS) and Transient Abnormal Myelopoiesis (TAM) — GATA1 mutations in exon 2/3 cause truncated GATA1s protein driving megakaryoblastic differentiation
B-ALL in Down syndrome — GATA1 mutations cause lymphoid differentiation block
T-ALL in Down syndrome — GATA1 mutations impair thymic T-cell development
Juvenile Myelomonocytic Leukemia (JMML) in Down syndrome — GATA1 drives monocytic differentiation
a: Myeloid Leukemia of Down syndrome (ML-DS) and Transient Abnormal Myelopoiesis (TAM) — GATA1 mutations in exon 2/3 cause truncated GATA1s protein driving megakaryoblastic differentiation

q: Juvenile Myelomonocytic Leukemia (JMML) is a rare aggressive clonal disorder of early childhood associated with mutations in the RAS signaling pathway. Which genetic syndrome is most commonly associated with JMML?
o: Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) — NF1 mutations causing RAS hyperactivation; also PTPN11, NRAS, KRAS, and CBL mutations in non-syndromic JMML
Down syndrome — trisomy 21 causing RAS pathway dysregulation
Noonan syndrome exclusively with PTPN11 mutations causing 100% JMML penetrance
Fanconi Anemia — DNA repair defects predisposing to JMML exclusively
a: Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) — NF1 mutations causing RAS hyperactivation; also PTPN11, NRAS, KRAS, and CBL mutations in non-syndromic JMML

q: Hodgkin Lymphoma (HL) in children and adolescents differs from adult HL in which important aspect of treatment approach?
o: Pediatric HL protocols use lower cumulative doses of radiation and anthracyclines with response-adapted therapy to minimize late effects of cardiopulmonary toxicity and secondary malignancies in growing children
Pediatric HL requires more aggressive chemotherapy with higher cumulative doses than adult protocols due to faster tumor cell proliferation
Adult HL uses combined modality treatment while pediatric HL relies on chemotherapy alone regardless of stage
Pediatric HL has worse outcomes than adult HL due to greater chemoresistance in immature lymphocytes
a: Pediatric HL protocols use lower cumulative doses of radiation and anthracyclines with response-adapted therapy to minimize late effects of cardiopulmonary toxicity and secondary malignancies in growing children

q: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) in children differs fundamentally from adult ITP in which key clinical characteristic?
o: Childhood ITP is typically acute, self-limiting, and follows a viral illness or vaccination with spontaneous resolution in >80% of cases within 6–12 months; adult ITP is predominantly chronic requiring long-term treatment
Childhood ITP is predominantly chronic requiring immunosuppression while adult ITP resolves spontaneously in >80% of cases
Childhood ITP exclusively affects infants under 12 months while adult ITP affects all age groups equally
Childhood ITP is always secondary to autoimmune disease while adult ITP is primarily idiopathic
a: Childhood ITP is typically acute, self-limiting, and follows a viral illness or vaccination with spontaneous resolution in >80% of cases within 6–12 months; adult ITP is predominantly chronic requiring long-term treatment

q: According to the American Society of Hematology (ASH) 2019 ITP guidelines, which approach is recommended for an otherwise well child with newly diagnosed ITP and a platelet count of 15 × 10⁹/L with only minor mucocutaneous bleeding?
o: Observation without pharmacological treatment — the majority of children with non-severe ITP can be safely observed given the high rate of spontaneous remission and low risk of serious bleeding
Immediate intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) to rapidly raise platelet count above 50 × 10⁹/L
Urgent corticosteroids (prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day) for 14 days to prevent intracranial hemorrhage
Platelet transfusion to maintain count above 30 × 10⁹/L during the acute phase
a: Observation without pharmacological treatment — the majority of children with non-severe ITP can be safely observed given the high rate of spontaneous remission and low risk of serious bleeding

q: The most feared complication of childhood ITP is intracranial hemorrhage (ICH). What is the approximate incidence of ICH in pediatric ITP?
o: 0.1–0.5% — ICH is rare in childhood ITP but drives the decision-making threshold for treatment in severe thrombocytopenia
5–10% — ICH is common in children with platelet counts below 20 × 10⁹/L
1–2% — ICH occurs in approximately 1 in 50 children with newly diagnosed ITP
10–15% — ICH risk justifies aggressive treatment in all children with ITP regardless of symptoms
a: 0.1–0.5% — ICH is rare in childhood ITP but drives the decision-making threshold for treatment in severe thrombocytopenia

q: Neonatal Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia (NAIT) is the platelet equivalent of hemolytic disease of the newborn. Which platelet antigen incompatibility accounts for the majority of NAIT cases in Caucasian populations?
o: HPA-1a (PlA1) antigen incompatibility — ~80% of NAIT cases involve HPA-1a-negative mothers sensitized to HPA-1a-positive fetal platelets
HPA-5b antigen incompatibility — the most common cause in all ethnic populations
ABO blood group incompatibility causing platelet destruction via isohemagglutinins
HLA-A2 antigen incompatibility causing maternal anti-HLA antibody-mediated platelet destruction
a: HPA-1a (PlA1) antigen incompatibility — ~80% of NAIT cases involve HPA-1a-negative mothers sensitized to HPA-1a-positive fetal platelets

q: NAIT can cause intracranial hemorrhage (ICH) in utero or at birth. The risk of fetal ICH in NAIT is highest in which clinical scenario?
o: Previous sibling affected with severe NAIT or ICH — recurrence risk approaches 100% with equal or greater severity in subsequent pregnancies
First pregnancy with NAIT — primigravida sensitization carries the highest ICH risk
Maternal platelet count below 50 × 10⁹/L during pregnancy indicating severe alloimmunization
NAIT caused by HPA-5b incompatibility which has a higher ICH risk than HPA-1a incompatibility
a: Previous sibling affected with severe NAIT or ICH — recurrence risk approaches 100% with equal or greater severity in subsequent pregnancies

q: The management of a neonate with confirmed or suspected NAIT and platelet count below 30 × 10⁹/L includes which of the following as the most appropriate immediate treatment?
o: Transfusion of HPA-1a-negative, HPA-5b-negative irradiated platelet concentrate (or maternal platelets if available and irradiated) with IVIG 1 g/kg
Transfusion of random donor platelets from blood bank inventory without HPA matching
IVIG alone without platelet transfusion regardless of platelet count or clinical status
Corticosteroids (dexamethasone) administered to the neonate to suppress alloantibody production
a: Transfusion of HPA-1a-negative, HPA-5b-negative irradiated platelet concentrate (or maternal platelets if available and irradiated) with IVIG 1 g/kg

q: Glanzmann Thrombasthenia (GT) is a rare inherited platelet disorder caused by deficiency or dysfunction of which platelet receptor?
o: Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa (GPIIb/IIIa, αIIbβ3 integrin) — the fibrinogen receptor essential for platelet aggregation
Glycoprotein Ib/IX/V (GPIb-IX-V) — the von Willebrand factor receptor mediating platelet adhesion
Glycoprotein VI (GPVI) — the collagen receptor initiating platelet activation
Protease-activated receptor 1 (PAR-1) — the thrombin receptor triggering platelet shape change
a: Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa (GPIIb/IIIa, αIIbβ3 integrin) — the fibrinogen receptor essential for platelet aggregation

q: Bernard-Soulier Syndrome (BSS) is a rare inherited platelet disorder characterized by thrombocytopenia with giant platelets. It results from deficiency of which platelet receptor?
o: Glycoprotein Ib/IX/V (GPIb-IX-V) — required for von Willebrand factor-mediated platelet adhesion to subendothelial collagen under high shear stress
Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa (GPIIb/IIIa) — required for fibrinogen-mediated platelet aggregation
P2Y12 receptor — required for ADP-mediated platelet activation and amplification
Thromboxane A2 receptor (TP receptor) — required for arachidonic acid-mediated platelet activation
a: Glycoprotein Ib/IX/V (GPIb-IX-V) — required for von Willebrand factor-mediated platelet adhesion to subendothelial collagen under high shear stress

q: In platelet aggregometry, Glanzmann Thrombasthenia shows which characteristic aggregation pattern that distinguishes it from Bernard-Soulier Syndrome?
o: GT shows absent aggregation to ALL agonists (ADP, collagen, arachidonic acid, epinephrine) with normal ristocetin agglutination; BSS shows absent ristocetin agglutination with normal aggregation to other agonists
GT shows absent ristocetin agglutination with normal ADP aggregation; BSS shows absent ADP aggregation with normal ristocetin
GT and BSS both show absent ristocetin agglutination distinguishable only by flow cytometry
GT shows absent collagen aggregation only; BSS shows absent all agonist aggregation
a: GT shows absent aggregation to ALL agonists (ADP, collagen, arachidonic acid, epinephrine) with normal ristocetin agglutination; BSS shows absent ristocetin agglutination with normal aggregation to other agonists

q: Hemophilia A is caused by deficiency of which coagulation factor, and what is the approximate incidence in the general population?
o: Factor VIII deficiency — affecting approximately 1 in 5,000 male births making it the most common severe inherited coagulation disorder
Factor IX deficiency — affecting approximately 1 in 5,000 male births identical to Hemophilia A incidence
Factor XI deficiency — affecting 1 in 100,000 births predominantly in Ashkenazi Jewish populations
Factor XIII deficiency — affecting 1 in 1,000,000 births causing umbilical stump bleeding in neonates
a: Factor VIII deficiency — affecting approximately 1 in 5,000 male births making it the most common severe inherited coagulation disorder

q: Severe hemophilia A is defined by factor VIII activity of less than what level, and what is the characteristic clinical presentation in untreated children?
o: Factor VIII <1% of normal — presenting with spontaneous hemarthroses (joint bleeds), intramuscular hematomas, and life-threatening bleeding from minor trauma or procedures
Factor VIII <5% of normal — presenting with post-surgical bleeding only without spontaneous hemorrhage
Factor VIII <10% of normal — presenting with excessive bruising but no spontaneous hemarthroses
Factor VIII <30% of normal — presenting identically to severe disease but with less frequency
a: Factor VIII <1% of normal — presenting with spontaneous hemarthroses (joint bleeds), intramuscular hematomas, and life-threatening bleeding from minor trauma or procedures

q: Primary prophylaxis in children with severe hemophilia A is recommended to begin at which age to prevent joint disease (hemophilic arthropathy)?
o: Before age 2–3 years and before the first joint bleed — early prophylaxis prevents the establishment of a 'target joint' and preserves long-term joint function
After the first hemarthrosis occurs — using the first bleed as a trigger to initiate prophylaxis
At school entry (age 5–6) when physical activity and trauma risk increase significantly
At puberty when hormonal changes increase bleeding risk in adolescent males with hemophilia
a: Before age 2–3 years and before the first joint bleed — early prophylaxis prevents the establishment of a 'target joint' and preserves long-term joint function

q: The most significant complication of factor replacement therapy in pediatric hemophilia A is development of inhibitors (alloantibodies against FVIII). What percentage of severe hemophilia A patients develop inhibitors?
o: ~25–30% of previously untreated patients with severe hemophilia A develop inhibitors, typically within the first 50 exposure days
~5–10% of all hemophilia A patients regardless of severity develop inhibitors
~50–60% of severe hemophilia A patients develop inhibitors within the first year of treatment
~1–2% of severe hemophilia A patients develop inhibitors — inhibitors are rare but serious complications
a: ~25–30% of previously untreated patients with severe hemophilia A develop inhibitors, typically within the first 50 exposure days

q: Immune Tolerance Induction (ITI) therapy for eradicating inhibitors in hemophilia A involves which therapeutic principle?
o: Regular high-dose FVIII administration (50–200 IU/kg/day) to induce immune tolerance by overwhelming the inhibitor and promoting regulatory T-cell responses
Rituximab (anti-CD20) monotherapy to deplete inhibitor-producing B cells
Mycophenolate mofetil and prednisolone immunosuppression to suppress inhibitor synthesis
Single high-dose FVIII infusion followed by prolonged drug holiday allowing natural inhibitor decay
a: Regular high-dose FVIII administration (50–200 IU/kg/day) to induce immune tolerance by overwhelming the inhibitor and promoting regulatory T-cell responses

q: Emicizumab (Hemlibra) is a bispecific antibody approved for hemophilia A prophylaxis with and without inhibitors. What is its mechanism of action?
o: Simultaneously binds activated Factor IX (FIXa) and Factor X (FX), bridging them to mimic the cofactor function of FVIII and restore thrombin generation independent of FVIII
Inhibits TFPI (tissue factor pathway inhibitor) to enhance thrombin generation through the extrinsic pathway
Binds von Willebrand factor to stabilize endogenous residual FVIII activity in mild-moderate hemophilia
Activates Factor XI bypassing the FVIII-dependent step of the intrinsic coagulation pathway
a: Simultaneously binds activated Factor IX (FIXa) and Factor X (FX), bridging them to mimic the cofactor function of FVIII and restore thrombin generation independent of FVIII

q: Von Willebrand Disease (VWD) is the most common inherited bleeding disorder. Type 1 VWD, accounting for ~75% of cases, is characterized by which laboratory findings?
o: Proportional reduction in VWF antigen (VWF:Ag) and VWF activity (VWF:RCo) with normal multimer pattern — quantitative partial deficiency of structurally normal VWF
Absence of high-molecular-weight VWF multimers with disproportionately low VWF activity relative to antigen
Complete absence of VWF antigen and activity with undetectable FVIII due to VWF carrier function loss
Normal VWF antigen with reduced activity due to a qualitative functional defect in the VWF A1 domain
a: Proportional reduction in VWF antigen (VWF:Ag) and VWF activity (VWF:RCo) with normal multimer pattern — quantitative partial deficiency of structurally normal VWF

q: What is the normal hemoglobin range for a healthy term neonate at birth, and how does it differ from adult normal values?
o: 14–22 g/dL in term neonates — significantly higher than adult values (12–17 g/dL) due to fetal erythropoiesis driven by relative intrauterine hypoxia
10–12 g/dL in term neonates — lower than adult values due to immature erythropoiesis in the newborn marrow
12–14 g/dL in term neonates — identical to adult female reference ranges
8–10 g/dL in term neonates — physiologically low due to hemodilution from placental transfusion at birth
a: 14–22 g/dL in term neonates — significantly higher than adult values (12–17 g/dL) due to fetal erythropoiesis driven by relative intrauterine hypoxia

q: Physiological anemia of infancy occurs at approximately 8–12 weeks of life in term infants with hemoglobin nadir of approximately 9–11 g/dL. What is the primary mechanism responsible for this physiological decline?
o: Post-birth increase in oxygenation suppresses erythropoietin (EPO) production, shortened neonatal RBC lifespan (60–70 days vs 120 days), and rapid hemodilution from somatic growth
Iron deficiency from inadequate dietary iron intake during exclusive breastfeeding in the first 2 months
Destruction of fetal red cells by maternal anti-HbF antibodies crossing the placenta postnatally
Bone marrow failure from physiological suppression of hematopoiesis during the transition from fetal to adult hematopoiesis
a: Post-birth increase in oxygenation suppresses erythropoietin (EPO) production, shortened neonatal RBC lifespan (60–70 days vs 120 days), and rapid hemodilution from somatic growth

q: Hemolytic Disease of the Fetus and Newborn (HDFN) caused by Rh(D) incompatibility occurs when which immunological mechanism is activated?
o: Rh(D)-negative mother previously sensitized to Rh(D) antigen produces IgG anti-D antibodies that cross the placenta and destroy Rh(D)-positive fetal red blood cells
Rh(D)-positive mother produces IgM anti-D antibodies that activate complement on fetal red cells
Rh(D)-negative fetus produces anti-D antibodies against maternal Rh(D)-positive red cells entering fetal circulation
ABO-incompatible maternal IgG antibodies cross the placenta destroying Rh(D)-antigen-expressing fetal erythrocytes
a: Rh(D)-negative mother previously sensitized to Rh(D) antigen produces IgG anti-D antibodies that cross the placenta and destroy Rh(D)-positive fetal red blood cells

q: Anti-D immunoglobulin (RhIG) prophylaxis is administered to Rh(D)-negative mothers at 28 weeks gestation and within 72 hours of delivery of an Rh(D)-positive infant. Its mechanism of prevention is:
o: Passive anti-D antibody clears fetal Rh(D)-positive red cells from maternal circulation before they can trigger active maternal immune sensitization (epitope masking/clearance theory)
Anti-D directly neutralizes fetal anti-Rh(D) antibodies produced in utero before they can damage maternal erythrocytes
Anti-D suppresses maternal B-cell activation by competitive inhibition of antigen presentation to helper T cells
Anti-D induces maternal regulatory T-cell tolerance to Rh(D) antigen through immunological memory suppression
a: Passive anti-D antibody clears fetal Rh(D)-positive red cells from maternal circulation before they can trigger active maternal immune sensitization (epitope masking/clearance theory)

q: ABO hemolytic disease of the newborn (ABO HDN) differs from Rh HDN in which important clinical and immunological characteristic?
o: ABO HDN can occur in first pregnancies (mothers have pre-existing ABO isohemagglutinins without prior sensitization), is typically milder, and rarely causes hydrops fetalis unlike severe Rh HDN
ABO HDN is exclusively caused by IgM antibodies that cannot cross the placenta making it detectable only postnatally
ABO HDN is more severe than Rh HDN requiring intrauterine transfusion in the majority of affected pregnancies
ABO HDN only occurs in blood group O mothers carrying blood group AB fetuses and never in other ABO combinations
a: ABO HDN can occur in first pregnancies (mothers have pre-existing ABO isohemagglutinins without prior sensitization), is typically milder, and rarely causes hydrops fetalis unlike severe Rh HDN

q: Neonatal jaundice requiring phototherapy is caused by unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Phototherapy works by converting bilirubin through which mechanism?
o: Photooxidation and photoisomerization of lipid-soluble unconjugated bilirubin into water-soluble configurational and structural isomers (lumirubin) excreted in bile and urine without hepatic conjugation
Direct conjugation of bilirubin to glucuronic acid by light-activated hepatic UDP-glucuronosyltransferase
Destruction of excess bilirubin molecules by ultraviolet light-induced free radical generation
Stimulation of neonatal liver maturation by blue light wavelengths accelerating bilirubin conjugation capacity
a: Photooxidation and photoisomerization of lipid-soluble unconjugated bilirubin into water-soluble configurational and structural isomers (lumirubin) excreted in bile and urine without hepatic conjugation

q: Exchange transfusion in severe neonatal hyperbilirubinemia (bilirubin-induced neurological dysfunction — BIND) serves which dual therapeutic purpose?
o: Removes bilirubin-laden red cells and free bilirubin from circulation while simultaneously replacing sensitized neonatal erythrocytes with donor cells, correcting anemia and removing circulating maternal antibodies in HDFN
Provides high-dose albumin to bind free bilirubin and prevent its CNS penetration without removing sensitized cells
Replaces neonatal coagulation factors consumed during hemolysis while removing excess bilirubin
Corrects neonatal polycythemia and hyperviscosity by diluting hematocrit while removing bilirubin
a: Removes bilirubin-laden red cells and free bilirubin from circulation while simultaneously replacing sensitized neonatal erythrocytes with donor cells, correcting anemia and removing circulating maternal antibodies in HDFN

q: Kernicterus (bilirubin encephalopathy) occurs when unconjugated bilirubin crosses the blood-brain barrier. Which clinical findings constitute the acute phase of bilirubin encephalopathy in neonates?
o: Hypotonia progressing to hypertonia, retrocollis-opisthotonos, high-pitched cry, poor feeding, and seizures in severe cases — followed by chronic Kernicterus with hearing loss, choreoathetoid cerebral palsy, and upward gaze palsy
Generalized petechiae, purpura, and intracranial hemorrhage from bilirubin-induced platelet dysfunction
Sudden onset apnea, bradycardia, and cyanosis from bilirubin-mediated cardiac conduction dysfunction
Progressive jaundice with direct hyperbilirubinemia indicating bilirubin-induced hepatocellular damage
a: Hypotonia progressing to hypertonia, retrocollis-opisthotonos, high-pitched cry, poor feeding, and seizures in severe cases — followed by chronic Kernicterus with hearing loss, choreoathetoid cerebral palsy, and upward gaze palsy

q: Polycythemia in neonates is defined as venous hematocrit above what threshold, and what is the most common clinical presentation?
o: Venous hematocrit >65% — presenting with hyperviscosity syndrome causing respiratory distress, plethora, jitteriness, feeding difficulties, hypoglycemia, and risk of thrombosis
Venous hematocrit >55% — presenting with severe anemia and heart failure in the first hours of life
Venous hematocrit >75% — presenting with shock and cardiovascular collapse requiring immediate exchange transfusion
Venous hematocrit >60% — an incidental laboratory finding never requiring treatment in asymptomatic neonates
a: Venous hematocrit >65% — presenting with hyperviscosity syndrome causing respiratory distress, plethora, jitteriness, feeding difficulties, hypoglycemia, and risk of thrombosis

q: Which of the following conditions is a recognized cause of neonatal polycythemia?
o: Delayed cord clamping, twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (recipient twin), maternal diabetes, intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), and trisomy 21
Prematurity — preterm neonates uniformly develop polycythemia from hypoxic intrauterine environment
Rh hemolytic disease of the newborn — causing compensatory erythropoiesis resulting in polycythemia
Congenital hypothyroidism — causing EPO-driven erythrocytosis in the newborn period
a: Delayed cord clamping, twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (recipient twin), maternal diabetes, intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), and trisomy 21

q: The normal platelet count range in term neonates is similar to adults (150–450 × 10⁹/L). Neonatal thrombocytopenia is defined as platelet count below 150 × 10⁹/L. What is the most common cause of thrombocytopenia in term neonates admitted to the NICU?
o: Placental insufficiency and fetal hypoxia causing megakaryocyte suppression — the most common cause in sick term neonates, followed by sepsis and NAIT
Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) from maternal anti-platelet antibodies crossing the placenta
Congenital amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia (CAMT) from MPL receptor mutations
Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) from birth asphyxia in all thrombocytopenic neonates
a: Placental insufficiency and fetal hypoxia causing megakaryocyte suppression — the most common cause in sick term neonates, followed by sepsis and NAIT

q: Congenital Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia (CAMT) presents at birth with profound thrombocytopenia and progresses to aplastic anemia. It is caused by mutations in which gene?
o: MPL — encoding the thrombopoietin receptor (c-Mpl), causing absent megakaryopoiesis and progressive marrow failure affecting all hematopoietic lineages
RUNX1 — causing megakaryocyte differentiation arrest with preserved erythroid and myeloid lineages
GATA-1 — causing combined megakaryocyte and erythroid failure with preserved granulopoiesis
FLI1 — encoding an ETS transcription factor essential for megakaryocyte terminal differentiation
a: MPL — encoding the thrombopoietin receptor (c-Mpl), causing absent megakaryopoiesis and progressive marrow failure affecting all hematopoietic lineages

q: Anemia of prematurity (AOP) differs from physiological anemia of term infancy in which critical aspect requiring therapeutic intervention?
o: Preterm infants develop more severe anemia (Hgb 7–9 g/dL) at 4–8 weeks due to lower EPO production relative to degree of anemia, phlebotomy losses from frequent blood sampling, and shorter RBC lifespan — symptomatic AOP requires erythropoietin and/or red cell transfusion
Preterm infants develop milder anemia than term infants due to preserved fetal HbF protecting RBCs from destruction
Anemia of prematurity resolves spontaneously by 2 weeks without treatment in all preterm infants
Preterm infants have normal EPO responses but impaired iron absorption requiring oral supplementation only
a: Preterm infants develop more severe anemia (Hgb 7–9 g/dL) at 4–8 weeks due to lower EPO production relative to degree of anemia, phlebotomy losses from frequent blood sampling, and shorter RBC lifespan — symptomatic AOP requires erythropoietin and/or red cell transfusion

q: The normal white blood cell count in healthy term neonates at birth can reach up to 30 × 10⁹/L with a left shift. This physiological leukocytosis is primarily caused by:
o: Stress-induced demargination of neutrophils during labor and delivery, combined with physiological release of neutrophil precursors from fetal bone marrow stores
Maternal steroid administration before delivery causing sustained leukocytosis lasting 2–3 weeks
Placental infection (chorioamnionitis) causing universal neonatal septicemia requiring antibiotic treatment
Physiological T-lymphocyte expansion in response to antigen exposure after birth
a: Stress-induced demargination of neutrophils during labor and delivery, combined with physiological release of neutrophil precursors from fetal bone marrow stores

q: Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency presenting in the neonatal period is a significant cause of severe neonatal jaundice in which geographic populations, and what triggers hemolytic crises?
o: Common in Mediterranean, African, Middle Eastern, and Asian populations — triggered by oxidant exposure including infections, fava beans, and certain drugs (primaquine, nitrofurantoin, dapsone) causing Heinz body hemolytic anemia
Exclusively affects sub-Saharan African neonates — triggered only by blood transfusion reactions in the neonatal period
Affects all ethnic groups equally — triggered exclusively by Rh incompatibility in G6PD-deficient neonates
Common only in Northern European populations — triggered by cold exposure causing cold-agglutinin hemolysis
a: Common in Mediterranean, African, Middle Eastern, and Asian populations — triggered by oxidant exposure including infections, fava beans, and certain drugs (primaquine, nitrofurantoin, dapsone) causing Heinz body hemolytic anemia

q: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) is predominantly a disease of adults. What is the median age at diagnosis, and how does this differ from pediatric AML?
o: Median age ~68 years in adults — the incidence increases sharply with age due to accumulation of somatic mutations, clonal hematopoiesis, and age-related epigenetic dysregulation; pediatric AML peaks in infancy and early childhood
Median age ~45 years — AML affects middle-aged adults equally with pediatric populations
Median age ~55 years — adult AML has identical molecular landscape to pediatric AML differing only in age
Median age ~75 years — AML exclusively affects the elderly with no significant incidence before age 60
a: Median age ~68 years in adults — the incidence increases sharply with age due to accumulation of somatic mutations, clonal hematopoiesis, and age-related epigenetic dysregulation; pediatric AML peaks in infancy and early childhood

q: Clonal Hematopoiesis of Indeterminate Potential (CHIP) is an adult-specific pre-malignant state defined by which criteria?
o: Somatic mutations in hematopoietic driver genes (DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1) with variant allele frequency ≥2% in peripheral blood without cytopenia or hematological malignancy — prevalence increases dramatically with age
Clonal expansion of a single hematopoietic stem cell with normal karyotype and no somatic mutations detectable by NGS
Presence of any somatic mutation in peripheral blood leukocytes regardless of variant allele frequency or clinical context
Monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis with CD5+ B cells below 5 × 10⁹/L without lymphadenopathy or symptoms
a: Somatic mutations in hematopoietic driver genes (DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1) with variant allele frequency ≥2% in peripheral blood without cytopenia or hematological malignancy — prevalence increases dramatically with age

q: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) is the most common adult leukemia in Western countries. Which surface immunophenotype is characteristic of CLL?
o: CD5+, CD19+, CD23+, CD20dim, CD79bdim, with monoclonal surface immunoglobulin (SIg)dim — co-expression of CD5 (T-cell marker) on mature B cells is the hallmark of CLL
CD5−, CD19+, CD20bright, CD23−, FMC7+ — consistent with mantle cell lymphoma immunophenotype
CD5+, CD19+, CD23−, CD20bright, cyclin D1+ — indicating CLL transformation to large cell lymphoma
CD10+, CD19+, CD20+, BCL-2+, BCL-6+ — the immunophenotype of follicular lymphoma in leukemic phase
a: CD5+, CD19+, CD23+, CD20dim, CD79bdim, with monoclonal surface immunoglobulin (SIg)dim — co-expression of CD5 (T-cell marker) on mature B cells is the hallmark of CLL

q: The Rai and Binet staging systems for CLL both stratify patients based on which clinical parameters?
o: Lymphadenopathy, organomegaly (splenomegaly/hepatomegaly), and bone marrow failure (anemia Hgb <11 g/dL and/or thrombocytopenia platelets <100 × 10⁹/L) — reflecting tumor burden and marrow infiltration
Lymphocyte count, LDH level, and beta-2 microglobulin — serum markers of proliferative activity
IGHV mutation status, del(17p), and TP53 mutation — molecular prognostic factors
FISH cytogenetic findings (del(13q), del(11q), del(17p), trisomy 12) exclusively
a: Lymphadenopathy, organomegaly (splenomegaly/hepatomegaly), and bone marrow failure (anemia Hgb <11 g/dL and/or thrombocytopenia platelets <100 × 10⁹/L) — reflecting tumor burden and marrow infiltration

q: Ibrutinib, a first-generation BTK inhibitor approved for CLL, works by which mechanism and what is its most clinically significant adverse effect?
o: Irreversible covalent inhibition of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) blocking B-cell receptor signaling, proliferation, and survival; most significant adverse effects include atrial fibrillation, bleeding, and hypertension
Reversible competitive inhibition of BTK causing B-cell apoptosis through BCR pathway blockade; primary adverse effect is peripheral neuropathy
Inhibition of PI3K-delta downstream of BTK causing CLL cell redistribution from lymph nodes to blood; primary adverse effect is hepatotoxicity
Dual BTK and SYK inhibition causing superior B-cell receptor blockade; primary adverse effect is immune-mediated colitis
a: Irreversible covalent inhibition of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) blocking B-cell receptor signaling, proliferation, and survival; most significant adverse effects include atrial fibrillation, bleeding, and hypertension

q: Venetoclax, a BCL-2 inhibitor approved for CLL, works by which mechanism and requires which specific monitoring protocol at initiation?
o: Selectively inhibits BCL-2 anti-apoptotic protein restoring apoptosis in CLL cells that overexpress BCL-2; requires 5-week ramp-up dosing schedule to reduce tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) risk with mandatory hospitalization for highest-risk patients
Inhibits both BCL-2 and BCL-XL causing rapid CLL cell death without TLS risk at standard starting doses
Binds venetoclax to BCL-2 causing conformational change that activates pro-apoptotic BAX/BAK independent of BH3 mimicry; TLS monitoring only required in patients with bulky lymphadenopathy
Venetoclax inhibits BCL-2 phosphorylation preventing its degradation — TLS prophylaxis with allopurinol alone is sufficient for all CLL patients
a: Selectively inhibits BCL-2 anti-apoptotic protein restoring apoptosis in CLL cells that overexpress BCL-2; requires 5-week ramp-up dosing schedule to reduce tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) risk with mandatory hospitalization for highest-risk patients

q: Multiple Myeloma (MM) is an adult malignancy with median age of diagnosis of approximately 70 years. The diagnostic criteria for symptomatic myeloma (CRAB criteria) include:
o: HyperCalcemia (Ca >11 mg/dL), Renal insufficiency (Cr >2 mg/dL), Anemia (Hgb <10 g/dL or >2 g/dL below normal), Bone lesions (lytic lesions, osteoporosis with fractures) — plus ≥10% clonal plasma cells or biopsy-proven plasmacytoma
Only M-protein >3 g/dL in serum or >500 mg/24h in urine with ≥10% clonal plasma cells in bone marrow
Serum free light chain ratio >100 with involved chain >100 mg/L constituting sole diagnostic criterion without CRAB features
Any combination of anemia, bone pain, and elevated ESR in patients over 60 years of age
a: HyperCalcemia (Ca >11 mg/dL), Renal insufficiency (Cr >2 mg/dL), Anemia (Hgb <10 g/dL or >2 g/dL below normal), Bone lesions (lytic lesions, osteoporosis with fractures) — plus ≥10% clonal plasma cells or biopsy-proven plasmacytoma

q: The International Staging System (ISS) for Multiple Myeloma and its revised version (R-ISS) stratify patients using which laboratory parameters?
o: Serum beta-2 microglobulin and serum albumin (ISS); R-ISS adds LDH and high-risk cytogenetics (del(17p), t(4;14), t(14;16)) to ISS for refined prognostication
Serum M-protein level, urine Bence Jones protein, and bone marrow plasma cell percentage exclusively
Serum calcium, creatinine, hemoglobin, and number of lytic bone lesions on skeletal survey
Serum free light chain ratio, immunoparesis severity, and 24-hour urine total protein
a: Serum beta-2 microglobulin and serum albumin (ISS); R-ISS adds LDH and high-risk cytogenetics (del(17p), t(4;14), t(14;16)) to ISS for refined prognostication

q: Daratumumab, an anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody approved for multiple myeloma, exerts its anti-tumor effect through which mechanisms?
o: Complement-dependent cytotoxicity (CDC), antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC), antibody-dependent cellular phagocytosis (ADCP), and immunomodulation by depleting immunosuppressive CD38+ regulatory T and NK cells
Direct induction of apoptosis through CD38-mediated caspase 3/7 activation without immune effector function involvement
Blocking CD38 enzymatic function (cyclic ADP-ribose hydrolase) depleting NAD+ in myeloma cells causing metabolic crisis
Delivering a cytotoxic payload (calicheamicin) to CD38-expressing plasma cells via antibody-drug conjugate mechanism
a: Complement-dependent cytotoxicity (CDC), antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC), antibody-dependent cellular phagocytosis (ADCP), and immunomodulation by depleting immunosuppressive CD38+ regulatory T and NK cells

q: Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma (DLBCL) — the most common adult non-Hodgkin lymphoma — is classified by cell of origin (COO) into which two major molecular subtypes with different prognoses?
o: Germinal Center B-cell-like (GCB-DLBCL) with better prognosis; and Activated B-cell-like (ABC-DLBCL)/non-GCB with worse prognosis when treated with R-CHOP — determined by gene expression profiling or IHC algorithms (Hans, Choi)
Follicular DLBCL with excellent prognosis (>95% cure) and Diffuse DLBCL with poor prognosis (<30% cure)
CD20-positive DLBCL responding to rituximab and CD20-negative DLBCL requiring alternative antibody therapy
MYC-rearranged DLBCL with good prognosis and BCL-2-rearranged DLBCL with poor prognosis in all cases
a: Germinal Center B-cell-like (GCB-DLBCL) with better prognosis; and Activated B-cell-like (ABC-DLBCL)/non-GCB with worse prognosis when treated with R-CHOP — determined by gene expression profiling or IHC algorithms (Hans, Choi)

q: Double-hit lymphoma (DHL) is defined by concurrent rearrangements of which genes, and what is the significance of this diagnosis?
o: Concurrent MYC rearrangement PLUS BCL-2 and/or BCL-6 rearrangement — classified as High-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC and BCL-2/BCL-6 rearrangements; associated with very poor prognosis with standard R-CHOP requiring intensified regimens (DA-EPOCH-R or R-CODOX-M/IVAC)
Concurrent BCL-2 and BCL-6 rearrangements without MYC — causing dual anti-apoptotic and transcriptional dysregulation
Concurrent MYC overexpression and TP53 deletion causing combined proliferative and DNA repair defects
Double-hit refers to biallelic deletion of the CDKN2A tumor suppressor locus causing simultaneous p16 and ARF loss
a: Concurrent MYC rearrangement PLUS BCL-2 and/or BCL-6 rearrangement — classified as High-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC and BCL-2/BCL-6 rearrangements; associated with very poor prognosis with standard R-CHOP requiring intensified regimens (DA-EPOCH-R or R-CODOX-M/IVAC)

q: Follicular Lymphoma (FL) is the most common indolent adult lymphoma, characterized by t(14;18) in ~85% of cases causing overexpression of which anti-apoptotic protein?
o: BCL-2 — the t(14;18)(q32;q21) translocation juxtaposes BCL-2 to the IgH locus causing constitutive BCL-2 overexpression, preventing follicular B-cell apoptosis and enabling clonal accumulation
MYC — the t(14;18) translocation activates MYC driving rapid proliferation in follicular center B cells
BCL-6 — overexpression from t(14;18) blocks germinal center B-cell differentiation causing follicular architecture preservation
MCL-1 — an alternative BCL-2 family member overexpressed in follicular lymphoma through IgH translocation
a: BCL-2 — the t(14;18)(q32;q21) translocation juxtaposes BCL-2 to the IgH locus causing constitutive BCL-2 overexpression, preventing follicular B-cell apoptosis and enabling clonal accumulation

q: The International Prognostic Index (IPI) for aggressive lymphomas and FLIPI for follicular lymphoma share which common adverse prognostic variable?
o: Age >60 years — advanced age is an adverse prognostic factor in both IPI (DLBCL) and FLIPI (FL), reflecting reduced treatment tolerance, comorbidities, and biologically more aggressive disease in older patients
Bone marrow involvement — present in all IPI and FLIPI high-risk patients
Elevated beta-2 microglobulin — a shared adverse biomarker in both scoring systems
Prior treatment with anthracyclines — cumulative cardiac toxicity worsening outcomes in both lymphoma types
a: Age >60 years — advanced age is an adverse prognostic factor in both IPI (DLBCL) and FLIPI (FL), reflecting reduced treatment tolerance, comorbidities, and biologically more aggressive disease in older patients

q: Adult T-cell Leukemia/Lymphoma (ATLL) is caused by which retroviral infection and has a particularly poor prognosis in which clinical subtype?
o: Human T-cell Lymphotropic Virus type 1 (HTLV-1) — endemic in Japan, Caribbean, and parts of Africa; acute and lymphoma subtypes have median survival <1 year despite aggressive chemotherapy
Human Immunodeficiency Virus type 1 (HIV-1) — causing T-cell leukemia/lymphoma in immunocompromised adults
Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV) — causing HTLV-1-like T-cell lymphoma in EBV-seropositive adults over 60 years
Human Herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8) — causing T-cell lymphoma associated with Kaposi sarcoma in co-infected adults
a: Human T-cell Lymphotropic Virus type 1 (HTLV-1) — endemic in Japan, Caribbean, and parts of Africa; acute and lymphoma subtypes have median survival <1 year despite aggressive chemotherapy

q: Mantle Cell Lymphoma (MCL) is an aggressive adult B-cell lymphoma characterized by which pathognomonic molecular alteration?
o: t(11;14)(q13;q32) translocation juxtaposing CCND1 (cyclin D1) to the IgH locus — cyclin D1 overexpression drives uncontrolled G1-to-S phase cell cycle progression
t(14;18)(q32;q21) translocation causing BCL-2 overexpression identical to follicular lymphoma
t(8;14)(q24;q32) translocation causing MYC overexpression as seen in Burkitt lymphoma
t(2;5)(p23;q35) translocation causing NPM1-ALK fusion as seen in ALK-positive ALCL
a: t(11;14)(q13;q32) translocation juxtaposing CCND1 (cyclin D1) to the IgH locus — cyclin D1 overexpression drives uncontrolled G1-to-S phase cell cycle progression

q: Who is credited with first grouping Polycythemia Vera, Essential Thrombocythemia, and Myelofibrosis as 'Myeloproliferative Disorders' in 1951?
Which mutation is present in over 95% of adult Polycythemia Vera (PV) cases but found less frequently in pediatric erythrocytosis?
In pediatric Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which clinical finding is significantly more common at presentation than in adults?
o: Massive splenomegaly and high WBC counts
a: Massive splenomegaly and high WBC counts

q: Pediatric patients with Essential Thrombocythemia (ET) are more likely than adults to be 'Triple Negative,' meaning they lack mutations in JAK2, CALR, and which other gene?
What is a major long-term side effect of Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor (TKI) therapy specifically concerning to pediatric hematologists?
o: Growth plate inhibition and stunted height
a: Growth plate inhibition and stunted height

q: Adults with MPNs have a significantly higher baseline risk of which complication compared to pediatric MPN patients?
Which 'Philadelphia-like' MPN-related condition is more associated with pediatric populations than older adults?
In the 2022 WHO classification, which blast percentage in the blood or marrow defines the 'Accelerated Phase' of CML in both adults and children?
o: Term no longer formally recommended by WHO 5th Ed
a: Term no longer formally recommended by WHO 5th Ed

q: Which specific JAK2 mutation is more commonly found in adult PV patients who are V617F negative?
Which symptom, common in adult MPN patients, is measured using the MPN-SAF (Symptom Assessment Form)?
The 'Philadelphia Chromosome' represents a reciprocal translocation between which two chromosomes?
When treating pediatric ET, which agent is often preferred over Hydroxyurea to avoid potential long-term leukemogenic risk?
Which rare MPN is characterized by an increase in mature neutrophils and is associated with the CSF3R mutation?
True or False: Primary Myelofibrosis (PMF) is frequently diagnosed in the first decade of life.
In pediatric CML, what is the goal of therapy regarding the Philadelphia chromosome?

Warm Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (wAIHA) — the most common type of AIHA in both children and adults — is caused by IgG autoantibodies reactive at 37°C targeting which red cell antigen system, and what is the first-line treatment?
o: Pan-reactive IgG autoantibodies targeting Rh system antigens (most commonly) causing extravascular hemolysis by splenic macrophage Fc receptor-mediated destruction — first-line treatment is corticosteroids (prednisolone 1–2 mg/kg/day)
IgM complement-fixing autoantibodies reactive at cold temperatures (0–4°C) targeting I/i antigens causing intravascular hemolysis — first-line treatment is cold avoidance and rituximab
IgA autoantibodies targeting glycophorin A causing combined intravascular and extravascular hemolysis — first-line IVIG therapy
