IgG and IgM combined autoantibodies (mixed AIHA) targeting ABO antigens causing severe acute intravascular hemolysis — immediate plasma exchange
a: Pan-reactive IgG autoantibodies targeting Rh system antigens (most commonly) causing extravascular hemolysis by splenic macrophage Fc receptor-mediated destruction — first-line treatment is corticosteroids (prednisolone 1–2 mg/kg/day)

q: Cold Agglutinin Disease (CAD) in adults is caused by IgM autoantibodies reactive at cold temperatures targeting I antigen on red cells. It causes hemolysis through which complement-mediated mechanism?
o: IgM autoantibodies fix complement at cold acral temperatures activating the classical complement pathway depositing C3b on red cells causing extravascular hemolysis; complement amplification can cause intravascular hemolysis via terminal MAC complex
IgM autoantibodies directly lyse red cells by membrane insertion without complement activation in cold temperatures
Cold agglutinins cause red cell agglutination in peripheral vessels causing mechanical hemolysis from shear forces only
IgM cold agglutinins activate the alternative complement pathway by directly displacing factor H from red cell surfaces
a: IgM autoantibodies fix complement at cold acral temperatures activating the classical complement pathway depositing C3b on red cells causing extravascular hemolysis; complement amplification can cause intravascular hemolysis via terminal MAC complex

q: Paroxysmal Cold Hemoglobinuria (PCH) is the most common cause of acute hemolytic anemia in young children. It involves which unique pathological antibody?
o: The Donath-Landsteiner antibody — a biphasic IgG antibody that binds P antigen on red cells at cold temperatures then causes complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis at 37°C upon rewarming, typically following viral infections
A warm IgG antibody targeting I antigen in children under 5 years causing chronic extravascular hemolysis
A cold IgM antibody targeting i antigen in neonates causing complement-mediated hemolysis during cold exposure
A drug-dependent antibody triggered by common pediatric antibiotics causing immune complex-mediated red cell destruction
a: The Donath-Landsteiner antibody — a biphasic IgG antibody that binds P antigen on red cells at cold temperatures then causes complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis at 37°C upon rewarming, typically following viral infections

q: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH) is an acquired clonal stem cell disorder caused by somatic mutation in which gene, leading to deficiency of which proteins?
o: Somatic PIG-A mutation causing deficiency of GPI-anchored complement regulatory proteins CD55 (DAF) and CD59 (MIRL) on blood cell surfaces — leaving red cells, platelets, and white cells unprotected from complement-mediated destruction
Somatic DNMT3A mutation causing epigenetic dysregulation of complement gene expression in hematopoietic stem cells
Germline C3 mutation causing alternative pathway dysregulation selectively affecting PIG-A-negative clones
Somatic JAK2 V617F mutation causing constitutive complement activation in expanded hematopoietic clones
a: Somatic PIG-A mutation causing deficiency of GPI-anchored complement regulatory proteins CD55 (DAF) and CD59 (MIRL) on blood cell surfaces — leaving red cells, platelets, and white cells unprotected from complement-mediated destruction

q: Which of the following PNH complications is responsible for the majority of morbidity and mortality in untreated patients, and how does this risk differ in pediatric versus adult PNH?
o: Venous and arterial thrombosis (particularly hepatic vein — Budd-Chiari, cerebral venous sinus, and mesenteric vessels) — thrombosis is the leading cause of death in adult PNH; pediatric PNH is rarer but carries similar thrombotic risk in larger clones
Severe aplastic anemia progression — marrow failure is the universal outcome in all untreated PNH patients regardless of age
Transformation to acute myeloid leukemia — occurs in >30% of adult PNH patients within 5 years of diagnosis
Renal failure from chronic hemosiderinuria — the primary cause of death in both pediatric and adult PNH
a: Venous and arterial thrombosis (particularly hepatic vein — Budd-Chiari, cerebral venous sinus, and mesenteric vessels) — thrombosis is the leading cause of death in adult PNH; pediatric PNH is rarer but carries similar thrombotic risk in larger clones

q: Aplastic Anemia (AA) in children and adults shares which common immune-mediated pathogenesis, and how do treatment outcomes differ between the two age groups?
o: T-cell mediated destruction of hematopoietic stem cells — children have superior outcomes with matched sibling donor HSCT (>90% OS) as first-line therapy while adults respond well to immunosuppression (ATG + cyclosporine) but have inferior HSCT outcomes with age-related transplant toxicity
Viral bone marrow invasion causing permanent stem cell destruction — children recover spontaneously while adults require indefinite immunosuppression
Autoantibody-mediated stem cell destruction identical to ITP — both age groups respond equally to IVIG and corticosteroids
Clonal hematopoiesis causing progressive marrow failure — children transform to MDS faster than adults requiring earlier HSCT
a: T-cell mediated destruction of hematopoietic stem cells — children have superior outcomes with matched sibling donor HSCT (>90% OS) as first-line therapy while adults respond well to immunosuppression (ATG + cyclosporine) but have inferior HSCT outcomes with age-related transplant toxicity

q: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA) is characterized by which pathognomonic finding on peripheral blood smear caused by red cell fragmentation in the microvasculature?
o: Schistocytes (fragmented red cells/helmet cells) — caused by mechanical shearing of red cells on fibrin strands or damaged endothelium in conditions including TTP, HUS, DIC, malignant hypertension, and HELLP syndrome
Spherocytes with loss of central pallor — caused by complement-mediated membrane attack on red cell lipid bilayer
Target cells (codocytes) with central condensation — caused by relative membrane excess from fibrin deposition
Bite cells (degmacytes) with Heinz body pitting — caused by splenic macrophage removal of oxidized hemoglobin inclusions
a: Schistocytes (fragmented red cells/helmet cells) — caused by mechanical shearing of red cells on fibrin strands or damaged endothelium in conditions including TTP, HUS, DIC, malignant hypertension, and HELLP syndrome

q: HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is an obstetric emergency occurring in pregnant women. Which hematological finding distinguishes it from TTP and HUS in the differential diagnosis?
o: HELLP occurs exclusively in the third trimester or postpartum, resolves after delivery, and shows elevated liver enzymes (AST/ALT) with normal ADAMTS13 activity — unlike TTP (ADAMTS13 <10%) or HUS (Shiga toxin or complement mutation)
HELLP shows severe ADAMTS13 deficiency identical to TTP requiring plasma exchange as emergency treatment
HELLP causes direct complement-mediated hemolysis identical to aHUS requiring eculizumab therapy
HELLP is distinguished from TTP by the presence of thrombocytopenia — TTP characteristically spares platelet count in pregnancy-associated disease
a: HELLP occurs exclusively in the third trimester or postpartum, resolves after delivery, and shows elevated liver enzymes (AST/ALT) with normal ADAMTS13 activity — unlike TTP (ADAMTS13 <10%) or HUS (Shiga toxin or complement mutation)

q: Anemia in chronic kidney disease (CKD) — predominantly affecting adults — is caused by which primary mechanism, and what is the standard treatment?
o: Insufficient erythropoietin (EPO) production by damaged peritubular fibroblasts combined with iron deficiency from urinary losses and reduced GI absorption — treated with ESAs (darbepoetin alfa, epoetin alfa) plus intravenous iron supplementation targeting Hgb 10–11.5 g/dL
Uremic toxin-mediated bone marrow suppression requiring regular blood transfusions in all CKD stage 4–5 patients
Autoimmune hemolysis from uremia-induced anti-RBC autoantibody production treated with corticosteroids
Folate deficiency from dialysis-induced folate loss requiring oral supplementation exclusively without ESA therapy
a: Insufficient erythropoietin (EPO) production by damaged peritubular fibroblasts combined with iron deficiency from urinary losses and reduced GI absorption — treated with ESAs (darbepoetin alfa, epoetin alfa) plus intravenous iron supplementation targeting Hgb 10–11.5 g/dL

q: Hypersplenism causing anemia is a predominantly adult condition associated with portal hypertension and splenomegaly. It causes anemia through which mechanism?
o: Increased splenic sequestration and destruction of morphologically normal red cells — the enlarged congested spleen traps and prematurely destroys erythrocytes, leukocytes, and platelets causing pancytopenia with compensatory bone marrow hyperplasia
Splenic production of anti-erythrocyte IgG autoantibodies causing AIHA with positive DAT
Splenic secretion of inhibitory cytokines suppressing bone marrow erythropoiesis without mechanical sequestration
Splenic arteriovenous shunting causing hemodilution and pseudoanemia from expanded plasma volume without true red cell reduction
a: Increased splenic sequestration and destruction of morphologically normal red cells — the enlarged congested spleen traps and prematurely destroys erythrocytes, leukocytes, and platelets causing pancytopenia with compensatory bone marrow hyperplasia

q: Lead poisoning causing anemia in children presents with which combination of hematological and biochemical findings on laboratory evaluation?
o: Microcytic hypochromic anemia with basophilic stippling of red cells, elevated free erythrocyte protoporphyrin (FEP)/zinc protoporphyrin (ZPP), elevated blood lead level, and elevated urinary aminolevulinic acid (ALA) — lead inhibits multiple enzymes in the heme synthesis pathway
Macrocytic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils from lead-induced folate antagonism in erythroid precursors
Normocytic anemia with positive DAT from lead-induced autoantibody formation against red cell antigens
Hemolytic anemia with spherocytes from lead-induced membrane lipid peroxidation causing osmotic fragility
a: Microcytic hypochromic anemia with basophilic stippling of red cells, elevated free erythrocyte protoporphyrin (FEP)/zinc protoporphyrin (ZPP), elevated blood lead level, and elevated urinary aminolevulinic acid (ALA) — lead inhibits multiple enzymes in the heme synthesis pathway

q: Pure Red Cell Aplasia (PRCA) in adults is most commonly associated with which viral infection causing selective destruction of erythroid progenitor cells?
o: Parvovirus B19 — infects and destroys erythroid progenitors (BFU-E, CFU-E, pronormoblasts) via P antigen receptor binding; in immunocompetent adults causes transient aplastic crisis while chronic PRCA occurs in immunocompromised patients unable to clear the virus
Cytomegalovirus (CMV) — causing selective erythroid aplasia through IE protein-mediated BFU-E inhibition in adults
Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV) — causing pure red cell aplasia through anti-erythropoietin antibody induction
Human Herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6) — selectively targeting glycophorin A-expressing erythroid precursors causing PRCA
a: Parvovirus B19 — infects and destroys erythroid progenitors (BFU-E, CFU-E, pronormoblasts) via P antigen receptor binding; in immunocompetent adults causes transient aplastic crisis while chronic PRCA occurs in immunocompromised patients unable to clear the virus

q: Anemia of Prematurity (AOP) in preterm infants is treated with recombinant erythropoietin (EPO) combined with which essential cofactor supplementation to maximize erythropoietic response?
o: Enteral or parenteral iron supplementation (2–6 mg/kg/day elemental iron) — iron is the rate-limiting substrate for hemoglobin synthesis; without adequate iron, EPO-stimulated erythropoiesis is blunted and iron deficiency develops rapidly
Vitamin B12 and folate supplementation — the primary limiting nutrients for erythroid DNA synthesis in preterm infants
Vitamin C supplementation — enhancing iron absorption from enteral feeds and reducing oxidative damage to preterm red cells
Copper supplementation — the cofactor for ceruloplasmin which is required for iron incorporation into hemoglobin
a: Enteral or parenteral iron supplementation (2–6 mg/kg/day elemental iron) — iron is the rate-limiting substrate for hemoglobin synthesis; without adequate iron, EPO-stimulated erythropoiesis is blunted and iron deficiency develops rapidly

q: Immune-mediated hemolytic anemia secondary to drug exposure can occur through which three distinct mechanisms?
o: Drug adsorption (hapten) mechanism (penicillin binding to RBCs); immune complex (innocent bystander) mechanism (quinine/quinidine-drug-antibody complex activating complement); and autoantibody induction mechanism (methyldopa causing true warm AIHA with positive DAT)
Drug-induced oxidative hemolysis causing Heinz body formation in G6PD-deficient and normal individuals equally
Complement-mediated drug-induced hemolysis through alternative pathway activation by all antimicrobial agents
Drug-induced anti-spectrin antibodies causing intravascular hemolysis through membrane skeleton disruption
a: Drug adsorption (hapten) mechanism (penicillin binding to RBCs); immune complex (innocent bystander) mechanism (quinine/quinidine-drug-antibody complex activating complement); and autoantibody induction mechanism (methyldopa causing true warm AIHA with positive DAT)

q: The reticulocyte production index (RPI) is a calculated value that corrects the reticulocyte count for the degree of anemia and reticulocyte maturation time. An RPI <2 indicates which type of anemia?
o: Hypoproliferative anemia — inadequate bone marrow response to anemia, indicating iron deficiency, vitamin B12/folate deficiency, anemia of chronic disease, aplastic anemia, or marrow infiltration as the likely etiology
Hyperproliferative hemolytic anemia — appropriate bone marrow response to peripheral red cell destruction or blood loss
Hemolytic anemia specifically — RPI <2 exclusively identifies immune-mediated hemolysis from non-immune causes
Post-hemorrhagic anemia — RPI <2 occurs only in the immediate 24-hour period following acute blood loss before reticulocytosis develops
a: Hypoproliferative anemia — inadequate bone marrow response to anemia, indicating iron deficiency, vitamin B12/folate deficiency, anemia of chronic disease, aplastic anemia, or marrow infiltration as the likely etiology

q: Venous thromboembolism (VTE) incidence in children follows a bimodal age distribution. Which two age groups have the highest pediatric VTE risk?
o: Neonates (first month of life) and adolescents (15–18 years) — neonates have immature coagulation balance and central venous catheters while adolescents have adult-like VTE risk factors including oral contraceptives, obesity, and cancer
School-age children (5–10 years) and toddlers (1–3 years) — peak activity levels cause paradoxical venous stasis
Infants (1–6 months) and pre-adolescents (10–13 years) — rapid growth phases cause coagulation factor imbalance
Neonates and school-age children (6–9 years) — both groups have physiologically low protein C and protein S levels
a: Neonates (first month of life) and adolescents (15–18 years) — neonates have immature coagulation balance and central venous catheters while adolescents have adult-like VTE risk factors including oral contraceptives, obesity, and cancer

q: The most common cause of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) in hospitalized pediatric patients differs fundamentally from the adult VTE etiology. What is the most common precipitating factor in pediatric VTE?
o: Central venous catheter (CVC) — responsible for ~50–60% of all pediatric VTE events including DVT and pulmonary embolism, unlike adults where immobility, malignancy, and surgery predominate
Inherited thrombophilia (Factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A) — the primary cause of spontaneous VTE in all pediatric age groups
Dehydration from acute gastroenteritis causing hyperviscosity-driven venous thrombosis in infants and toddlers
Trauma and immobility — identical to adult VTE pathogenesis but at lower absolute incidence rates
a: Central venous catheter (CVC) — responsible for ~50–60% of all pediatric VTE events including DVT and pulmonary embolism, unlike adults where immobility, malignancy, and surgery predominate

q: Virchow's triad describes the three components predisposing to thrombosis. Which combination correctly identifies all three elements?
o: Endothelial injury/dysfunction, blood flow abnormalities (stasis or turbulence), and hypercoagulable state (altered blood constituents) — all three synergistically promote thrombus formation at venous and arterial sites
Platelet activation, coagulation factor deficiency, and fibrinolytic impairment — the hemostatic triad of thrombosis
Hyperviscosity, vasoconstriction, and platelet hyperreactivity — the rheological components of thrombosis
Inflammation, oxidative stress, and endothelial apoptosis — the molecular triad of atherothrombosis
a: Endothelial injury/dysfunction, blood flow abnormalities (stasis or turbulence), and hypercoagulable state (altered blood constituents) — all three synergistically promote thrombus formation at venous and arterial sites

q: Factor V Leiden mutation (FVL) is the most common inherited thrombophilia. It causes thrombosis through which molecular mechanism?
o: A point mutation (R506Q) in Factor Va renders it resistant to inactivation by Activated Protein C (APC resistance), prolonging thrombin generation by preventing Factor Va degradation in the prothrombinase complex
Factor V Leiden causes constitutive activation of Factor V without thrombin cleavage, driving continuous thrombin generation
FVL mutation creates a neo-epitope activating complement-mediated coagulation pathway amplification
FVL reduces Factor V production by 50% causing paradoxical thrombosis through feedback loop activation of alternative coagulation pathways
a: A point mutation (R506Q) in Factor Va renders it resistant to inactivation by Activated Protein C (APC resistance), prolonging thrombin generation by preventing Factor Va degradation in the prothrombinase complex

q: Prothrombin G20210A mutation causes thrombophilia through which mechanism, and what is its prevalence in the general Caucasian population?
o: A gain-of-function mutation in the prothrombin gene 3' UTR causing elevated plasma prothrombin levels (120–130% of normal) increasing thrombin generation potential — present in ~2–3% of the general Caucasian population
A loss-of-function mutation reducing prothrombin clearance causing 3-fold elevated prothrombin levels — present in ~10% of Caucasians
A missense mutation causing a hyperactive prothrombin molecule with increased thrombin conversion efficiency — present in ~0.1% of Caucasians
A promoter gain-of-function mutation increasing prothrombin gene transcription 5-fold — present in ~5% of Caucasian VTE patients exclusively
a: A gain-of-function mutation in the prothrombin gene 3' UTR causing elevated plasma prothrombin levels (120–130% of normal) increasing thrombin generation potential — present in ~2–3% of the general Caucasian population

q: Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS) is an acquired thrombophilia caused by autoantibodies against phospholipid-binding proteins. The three antiphospholipid antibodies tested in the diagnostic criteria are:
o: Lupus anticoagulant (LA), anticardiolipin antibodies (aCL IgG/IgM), and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein I antibodies (anti-β2GPI IgG/IgM) — all three must be tested and persistent on repeat testing ≥12 weeks apart for diagnosis
Anti-prothrombin antibodies, anti-protein C antibodies, and anti-protein S antibodies — the three pathogenic antibody classes in APS
Anti-factor Xa antibodies, anti-thrombin antibodies, and anti-fibrin antibodies — directly targeting coagulation cascade components
Anti-platelet antibodies (anti-GPIIb/IIIa), anti-VWF antibodies, and anti-fibrinogen antibodies — causing combined thrombotic and hemorrhagic manifestations
a: Lupus anticoagulant (LA), anticardiolipin antibodies (aCL IgG/IgM), and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein I antibodies (anti-β2GPI IgG/IgM) — all three must be tested and persistent on repeat testing ≥12 weeks apart for diagnosis

q: Catastrophic Antiphospholipid Syndrome (CAPS) is the most severe form of APS. It is defined by which criteria and carries what mortality rate?
o: Thrombosis in ≥3 organ systems within <1 week with histopathological evidence of small vessel occlusion and positive antiphospholipid antibodies — mortality rate ~30–50% despite aggressive treatment with anticoagulation, corticosteroids, plasma exchange, and IVIG
Thrombosis in ≥5 organ systems over >3 months with highly positive aPL antibodies — mortality rate <5% with anticoagulation alone
Recurrent DVT and PE in APS patients failing warfarin anticoagulation — mortality rate ~10% with DOAC rescue therapy
Severe thrombocytopenia (<20 × 10⁹/L) with positive aPL antibodies causing hemorrhagic manifestations — mortality rate ~15% with IVIG treatment
a: Thrombosis in ≥3 organ systems within <1 week with histopathological evidence of small vessel occlusion and positive antiphospholipid antibodies — mortality rate ~30–50% despite aggressive treatment with anticoagulation, corticosteroids, plasma exchange, and IVIG

q: Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT) is a life-threatening prothrombotic disorder caused by IgG antibodies against which antigen complex?
o: Platelet factor 4 (PF4)-heparin complex — anti-PF4/heparin IgG antibodies activate platelets via FcγRIIA receptor causing massive thrombin generation, thrombocytopenia, and paradoxical thrombosis despite heparin anticoagulation
Heparin-antithrombin complex — antibodies neutralize the therapeutic anticoagulant effect causing heparin resistance and rebound thrombosis
Thrombin-heparin complex — antibodies activate thrombin causing platelet consumption and thrombotic microangiopathy
Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa-heparin conjugate — antibodies cause platelet aggregation identical to TTP pathogenesis
a: Platelet factor 4 (PF4)-heparin complex — anti-PF4/heparin IgG antibodies activate platelets via FcγRIIA receptor causing massive thrombin generation, thrombocytopenia, and paradoxical thrombosis despite heparin anticoagulation

q: The 4T score is used to assess the pre-test probability of HIT. What do the four 'T's represent, and what score constitutes high probability?
o: Thrombocytopenia (degree and timing of platelet fall), Timing (platelet fall onset relative to heparin), Thrombosis (new clot or skin necrosis), and oTher causes (alternative explanation for thrombocytopenia) — score of 6–8 constitutes high probability requiring immediate heparin cessation and alternative anticoagulation
Thrombosis severity, Troponin elevation, Thrombocytopenia, and Treatment response — score >4 indicates HIT
Timing, Thrombocytopenia nadir, Temperature (fever), and Type of heparin — score >5 constitutes intermediate probability
Thrombin time, APTT ratio, Thrombocytopenia, and Test positivity (PF4 ELISA) — score >6 indicates definite HIT requiring fondaparinux treatment
a: Thrombocytopenia (degree and timing of platelet fall), Timing (platelet fall onset relative to heparin), Thrombosis (new clot or skin necrosis), and oTher causes (alternative explanation for thrombocytopenia) — score of 6–8 constitutes high probability requiring immediate heparin cessation and alternative anticoagulation

q: When HIT is clinically suspected or confirmed, heparin must be immediately discontinued and replaced with which non-heparin anticoagulants?
o: Direct thrombin inhibitors (argatroban — preferred in hepatic impairment; bivalirudin — preferred in renal impairment) or Factor Xa inhibitor fondaparinux — warfarin must NOT be initiated until platelet count recovers >150 × 10⁹/L
Immediate transition to warfarin with therapeutic INR within 48 hours of heparin cessation
Low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin) substitution — cross-reactivity with HIT antibodies is <10% making LMWH safe in confirmed HIT
Platelet transfusion followed by unfractionated heparin dose reduction to 50% to balance thrombotic and hemorrhagic risk
a: Direct thrombin inhibitors (argatroban — preferred in hepatic impairment; bivalirudin — preferred in renal impairment) or Factor Xa inhibitor fondaparinux — warfarin must NOT be initiated until platelet count recovers >150 × 10⁹/L

q: Neonatal cerebral sinovenous thrombosis (CSVT) is the most common arterial or venous stroke presentation in neonates. Which is the most common prothrombotic risk factor identified in neonatal CSVT?
o: Perinatal complications (birth asphyxia, dehydration, infection/sepsis) combined with physiologically low anticoagulant protein levels (Protein C, Protein S) — accounting for >80% of neonatal CSVT cases; inherited thrombophilia is a contributing but not primary factor
Inherited Factor V Leiden homozygosity — the single most common thrombophilia in neonatal CSVT
Maternal antiphospholipid syndrome with placental thrombosis causing fetal cerebrovascular events
Prematurity-associated intracranial hemorrhage converting to venous thrombosis in preterm neonates
a: Perinatal complications (birth asphyxia, dehydration, infection/sepsis) combined with physiologically low anticoagulant protein levels (Protein C, Protein S) — accounting for >80% of neonatal CSVT cases; inherited thrombophilia is a contributing but not primary factor

q: Protein C deficiency in its heterozygous form confers a 5–10-fold increased lifetime VTE risk. How is Protein C's natural anticoagulant function mediated?
o: Thrombomodulin-bound thrombin activates Protein C; Activated Protein C (APC) with Protein S as cofactor cleaves and inactivates Factor Va and Factor VIIIa, limiting thrombin generation on the platelet surface
Protein C directly inhibits Factor Xa by binding to its active site independent of thrombomodulin activation
Protein C activates Protein S which then directly inhibits the prothrombinase complex without requiring Protein C activation
Protein C binds to antithrombin potentiating AT-mediated inhibition of thrombin and Factor Xa in plasma
a: Thrombomodulin-bound thrombin activates Protein C; Activated Protein C (APC) with Protein S as cofactor cleaves and inactivates Factor Va and Factor VIIIa, limiting thrombin generation on the platelet surface

q: Antithrombin (AT) deficiency is the highest-risk inherited thrombophilia conferring a 25–50-fold increased VTE risk in symptomatic families. Antithrombin inhibits coagulation through which mechanism, and what potentiates its activity?
o: AT is a serine protease inhibitor (serpin) that irreversibly inhibits thrombin, Factor Xa, IXa, XIa, and XIIa by forming 1:1 stoichiometric complexes — heparin and heparan sulfate accelerate AT activity 1000-fold by inducing conformational change
AT blocks thrombomodulin-thrombin interaction preventing Protein C activation — heparin potentiates this by increasing AT-thrombomodulin binding affinity
AT directly degrades fibrinogen preventing fibrin clot formation — heparin enhances fibrinogenolysis 500-fold
AT inhibits only Factor Xa through TFPI-like mechanism — heparin increases Factor Xa-AT complex formation exclusively
a: AT is a serine protease inhibitor (serpin) that irreversibly inhibits thrombin, Factor Xa, IXa, XIa, and XIIa by forming 1:1 stoichiometric complexes — heparin and heparan sulfate accelerate AT activity 1000-fold by inducing conformational change

q: The Wells score is the most widely used clinical prediction rule for DVT in adults. It incorporates which clinical variables to stratify DVT pre-test probability?
o: Active cancer, paralysis/paresis/plaster immobilization, bedridden >3 days or surgery within 12 weeks, localized tenderness, entire leg swollen, calf swelling >3 cm, pitting edema, collateral superficial veins, previous DVT, and alternative diagnosis at least as likely — total score stratifies into low/moderate/high probability
Age, BMI, family history of DVT, D-dimer level, and blood group (non-O) — the five cardiovascular risk factors for DVT
Fever >38°C, leukocytosis, CRP >10 mg/L, elevated D-dimer, and positive Homans sign — the inflammatory DVT criteria
Immobility duration, oxygen saturation, heart rate, and respiratory rate — the modified Wells score for PE only
a: Active cancer, paralysis/paresis/plaster immobilization, bedridden >3 days or surgery within 12 weeks, localized tenderness, entire leg swollen, calf swelling >3 cm, pitting edema, collateral superficial veins, previous DVT, and alternative diagnosis at least as likely — total score stratifies into low/moderate/high probability

q: D-dimer has high sensitivity but low specificity for VTE. In which clinical context is D-dimer MOST useful diagnostically, and why is it less reliable in specific populations?
o: D-dimer most useful to EXCLUDE VTE in low-to-intermediate pre-test probability patients (high NPV >95%); less reliable in pregnancy, cancer, post-surgery, elderly (>80 years), and sepsis — all cause elevated D-dimer reducing specificity to near zero
D-dimer most useful to CONFIRM VTE in high-probability patients — a positive result is diagnostic obviating the need for imaging
D-dimer is equally reliable across all populations — age and comorbidity do not affect D-dimer sensitivity for VTE
D-dimer is only reliable for PE diagnosis — DVT requires duplex ultrasound regardless of D-dimer result
a: D-dimer most useful to EXCLUDE VTE in low-to-intermediate pre-test probability patients (high NPV >95%); less reliable in pregnancy, cancer, post-surgery, elderly (>80 years), and sepsis — all cause elevated D-dimer reducing specificity to near zero

q: Anemia is highly prevalent in older adults. According to WHO criteria, what hemoglobin threshold defines anemia in adults over 65 years, and what is the approximate prevalence of anemia in community-dwelling elderly?
o: Hgb <13 g/dL in men and <12 g/dL in women — anemia affects approximately 10–12% of community-dwelling elderly rising to >40% in hospitalized patients over 85 years
Hgb <11 g/dL in all elderly regardless of sex — prevalence is approximately 5% in all adults over 65 years
Hgb <14 g/dL in men and <13 g/dL in women over 65 — reflecting age-related decline in erythropoietic reserve
Hgb <10 g/dL in all elderly over 75 years — severe anemia exclusively defines geriatric anemia given physiological tolerance
a: Hgb <13 g/dL in men and <12 g/dL in women — anemia affects approximately 10–12% of community-dwelling elderly rising to >40% in hospitalized patients over 85 years

q: Unexplained anemia of the elderly (UAE) — also termed anemia of aging — accounts for approximately one-third of geriatric anemia cases after excluding nutritional deficiency and chronic disease. Which pathophysiological mechanism is primarily implicated?
o: Age-related decline in hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) reserve and clonal hematopoiesis combined with blunted erythropoietin (EPO) response to anemia, increased inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α), and progressive bone marrow adipose replacement reducing erythropoietic capacity
Complete EPO deficiency from age-related renal failure causing severe anemia identical to CKD anemia in all elderly patients
Autoimmune destruction of erythroid progenitors causing pure red cell aplasia as the universal mechanism of aging-related anemia
Dietary iron malabsorption from age-related gastric achlorhydria causing universal iron deficiency in all adults over 70 years
a: Age-related decline in hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) reserve and clonal hematopoiesis combined with blunted erythropoietin (EPO) response to anemia, increased inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α), and progressive bone marrow adipose replacement reducing erythropoietic capacity

q: Clonal Hematopoiesis of Indeterminate Potential (CHIP) prevalence increases dramatically with age. What is the estimated prevalence of CHIP in individuals over 70 years, and what are its primary clinical consequences?
o: CHIP present in ~10–20% of individuals over 70 years — associated with 0.5–1% annual risk of hematological malignancy, 2-fold increased cardiovascular disease risk from CHIP-driven macrophage inflammatory activation, and increased all-cause mortality
CHIP present in ~1–2% of individuals over 70 years — exclusively a hematological pre-malignant condition without cardiovascular consequences
CHIP present in >50% of individuals over 70 years — causes anemia in all affected elderly requiring routine EPO therapy
CHIP present in ~5% of individuals over 70 years — associated only with MDS transformation without cardiovascular implications
a: CHIP present in ~10–20% of individuals over 70 years — associated with 0.5–1% annual risk of hematological malignancy, 2-fold increased cardiovascular disease risk from CHIP-driven macrophage inflammatory activation, and increased all-cause mortality

q: Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment (CGA) in elderly patients with hematological malignancies evaluates which domains to guide treatment decisions beyond performance status alone?
o: Functional status (ADL/IADL), cognitive function (MMSE/MoCA), nutritional status (MNA), comorbidity burden (CIRS-G/Charlson), polypharmacy, social support, psychological status, and gait/fall risk — CGA identifies frailty distinguishing fit elderly who tolerate intensive therapy from unfit patients requiring dose-reduced regimens
Only ECOG performance status and serum albumin — these two parameters are sufficient to predict chemotherapy tolerance in all elderly patients
Cardiac ejection fraction, renal function (GFR), and hepatic function (Child-Pugh) exclusively — organ function determines treatment eligibility in geriatric hematology
Age alone — patients over 75 years automatically receive reduced-intensity therapy regardless of functional status or comorbidities
a: Functional status (ADL/IADL), cognitive function (MMSE/MoCA), nutritional status (MNA), comorbidity burden (CIRS-G/Charlson), polypharmacy, social support, psychological status, and gait/fall risk — CGA identifies frailty distinguishing fit elderly who tolerate intensive therapy from unfit patients requiring dose-reduced regimens

q: Monoclonal Gammopathy of Undetermined Significance (MGUS) is an age-related pre-malignant plasma cell disorder. What is its prevalence in adults over 70 years, and what is the annual rate of progression to multiple myeloma or related malignancy?
o: MGUS present in ~5–7% of adults over 70 years rising to ~10% over 85 years — approximately 1% per year progression risk to multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, or related lymphoplasmacytic malignancy (Mayo Clinic landmark study)
MGUS present in ~1% of adults over 70 years — 10% annual progression risk requiring immediate chemotherapy at diagnosis
MGUS present in ~20% of adults over 70 years — 0.1% annual progression risk making monitoring unnecessary in asymptomatic elderly
MGUS present in ~3% of adults over 70 years — 5% annual progression risk requiring bone marrow biopsy annually in all MGUS patients
a: MGUS present in ~5–7% of adults over 70 years rising to ~10% over 85 years — approximately 1% per year progression risk to multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, or related lymphoplasmacytic malignancy (Mayo Clinic landmark study)

q: Age is the single most important adverse prognostic factor in AML. What is the 5-year overall survival for elderly patients (>65 years) with AML compared to younger adults, and what biological factors explain this disparity?
o: 5-year OS ~10–15% in elderly AML vs. ~40–50% in younger adults — explained by higher frequency of adverse cytogenetics (monosomy 7, complex karyotype), secondary/therapy-related AML from prior MDS, TP53 mutations, MDR1 overexpression causing chemoresistance, and reduced tolerance to intensive induction chemotherapy
5-year OS ~30–35% in elderly AML — similar to younger adults when fitness-adjusted treatment is administered
5-year OS <5% universally in all AML patients over 70 years regardless of cytogenetics or treatment approach
5-year OS ~50% in elderly AML when treated with Venetoclax+Azacitidine — equivalent outcomes to intensive chemotherapy in younger adults
a: 5-year OS ~10–15% in elderly AML vs. ~40–50% in younger adults — explained by higher frequency of adverse cytogenetics (monosomy 7, complex karyotype), secondary/therapy-related AML from prior MDS, TP53 mutations, MDR1 overexpression causing chemoresistance, and reduced tolerance to intensive induction chemotherapy

q: The standard '7+3' induction chemotherapy for AML (cytarabine 7 days + anthracycline 3 days) is associated with significant toxicity in elderly patients. The mortality rate of elderly AML patients receiving intensive induction chemotherapy is approximately:
o: ~15–25% early treatment-related mortality (TRM) within 60 days of induction in patients over 75 years — driven by prolonged neutropenia, infections, mucositis, and cardiac toxicity from anthracyclines in elderly myocardium
~1–5% TRM identical to younger adults — anthracycline cardiotoxicity only affects patients with pre-existing cardiac disease
~50% TRM in all elderly AML patients receiving 7+3 — intensive chemotherapy should be universally avoided in patients over 65 years
~5–10% TRM in elderly AML — the same as standard-risk younger adults indicating age alone is not a contraindication to intensive therapy
a: ~15–25% early treatment-related mortality (TRM) within 60 days of induction in patients over 75 years — driven by prolonged neutropenia, infections, mucositis, and cardiac toxicity from anthracyclines in elderly myocardium

q: Reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) allogeneic HSCT has expanded transplant eligibility to elderly patients. What is the upper age limit currently accepted for RIC-HSCT in hematological malignancies at experienced centers?
o: Up to 75–80 years in carefully selected fit elderly patients (ECOG 0–1, no severe comorbidities) — RIC regimens using fludarabine-based conditioning reduce TRM compared to myeloablative conditioning while preserving graft-versus-leukemia effect
Up to 60 years — allogeneic HSCT is contraindicated beyond this age regardless of fitness or conditioning intensity
Up to 65 years — standard myeloablative conditioning is required for all HSCT regardless of patient age
Up to 70 years only when a matched sibling donor is available — unrelated donor HSCT is contraindicated over age 65
a: Up to 75–80 years in carefully selected fit elderly patients (ECOG 0–1, no severe comorbidities) — RIC regimens using fludarabine-based conditioning reduce TRM compared to myeloablative conditioning while preserving graft-versus-leukemia effect

q: Myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS) are predominantly diseases of the elderly with median age at diagnosis of approximately 70 years. What proportion of MDS patients are over 60 years at diagnosis, and how does age influence treatment decisions?
o: ~80–85% of MDS patients are over 60 years — age influences treatment by limiting HSCT eligibility to lower-risk MDS patients under 70 years while higher-risk elderly receive hypomethylating agents (azacitidine, decitabine); very elderly patients prioritize quality of life with supportive care
~50% of MDS patients are over 60 years — younger MDS patients with identical biology require identical treatment to elderly patients
~95% of MDS patients are over 75 years — MDS exclusively affects the very elderly and is always managed with transfusion support alone
~60% of MDS patients are over 60 years — age has no influence on MDS treatment decisions beyond HSCT eligibility assessment
a: ~80–85% of MDS patients are over 60 years — age influences treatment by limiting HSCT eligibility to lower-risk MDS patients under 70 years while higher-risk elderly receive hypomethylating agents (azacitidine, decitabine); very elderly patients prioritize quality of life with supportive care

q: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) has a median age at diagnosis of approximately 72 years. Which treatment regimen is preferred for elderly/unfit CLL patients with del(17p) or TP53 mutation who cannot tolerate chemoimmunotherapy?
o: Ibrutinib (BTK inhibitor) or acalabrutinib monotherapy — BTK inhibitors achieve high response rates independent of TP53 mutation status and are well-tolerated in elderly patients; venetoclax-obinutuzumab is an alternative fixed-duration option for del(17p) CLL
Fludarabine-cyclophosphamide-rituximab (FCR) — the gold standard regardless of TP53 status or age in all CLL patients
Chlorambucil-obinutuzumab — effective for del(17p) CLL due to obinutuzumab's superior complement-mediated cytotoxicity overcoming TP53 pathway defects
Alemtuzumab monotherapy — anti-CD52 antibody targets all lymphocytes regardless of TP53 mutation as the standard del(17p) CLL treatment
a: Ibrutinib (BTK inhibitor) or acalabrutinib monotherapy — BTK inhibitors achieve high response rates independent of TP53 mutation status and are well-tolerated in elderly patients; venetoclax-obinutuzumab is an alternative fixed-duration option for del(17p) CLL

q: Polypharmacy in elderly hematology patients creates significant risks of drug-drug interactions (DDIs). Which DOAC interaction is most clinically significant in elderly patients commonly prescribed multiple medications?
o: Strong CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein inhibitors (azole antifungals, clarithromycin, amiodarone) increase DOAC plasma levels causing bleeding risk; strong inducers (rifampicin, phenytoin, carbamazepine) reduce DOAC levels causing treatment failure — DDIs are more dangerous in elderly due to reduced drug clearance from age-related CYP3A4 decline and renal impairment
DOACs have no clinically significant drug interactions — their predictable pharmacokinetics eliminate DDI concerns in all elderly patients
Only warfarin has clinically significant drug interactions — DOACs are safe to combine with all medications including antifungals and antibiotics
P-glycoprotein interactions only affect dabigatran while CYP3A4 interactions exclusively affect apixaban — these do not overlap in clinical practice
a: Strong CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein inhibitors (azole antifungals, clarithromycin, amiodarone) increase DOAC plasma levels causing bleeding risk; strong inducers (rifampicin, phenytoin, carbamazepine) reduce DOAC levels causing treatment failure — DDIs are more dangerous in elderly due to reduced drug clearance from age-related CYP3A4 decline and renal impairment

q: Falls risk assessment is a critical component of geriatric hematology because anticoagulation for AF or VTE in elderly patients who fall frequently creates which therapeutic dilemma?
o: A fall causing intracranial hemorrhage (ICH) in an anticoagulated elderly patient carries ~50% mortality — risk stratification using HAS-BLED and CHA₂DS₂-VASc scores guides individualized anticoagulation decisions; most elderly patients with high AF stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2) have net clinical benefit from anticoagulation even with moderate fall risk
All elderly patients who fall more than once per year should have anticoagulation permanently discontinued regardless of AF stroke risk
Falls risk is irrelevant in anticoagulation decision-making — stroke prevention always outweighs ICH risk from falls in elderly AF patients
Aspirin monotherapy should replace anticoagulation in all elderly fallers with AF as it provides equivalent stroke protection with lower ICH risk
a: A fall causing intracranial hemorrhage (ICH) in an anticoagulated elderly patient carries ~50% mortality — risk stratification using HAS-BLED and CHA₂DS₂-VASc scores guides individualized anticoagulation decisions; most elderly patients with high AF stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2) have net clinical benefit from anticoagulation even with moderate fall risk

q: Age-related thrombocytopenia in elderly patients has multiple causes. Which condition specifically causes isolated thrombocytopenia in elderly adults through a clonal megakaryocytic process driven by mutations in the calreticulin (CALR) or JAK2 gene?
o: Essential Thrombocythemia (ET) — paradoxically, ET can occasionally present with thrombocytopenia in elderly patients from platelet consumption (acquired von Willebrand syndrome) when platelet counts are extremely high; more commonly, MDS with isolated del(5q) causes thrombocytosis not thrombocytopenia
Primary Myelofibrosis — causing progressive thrombocytopenia from marrow fibrosis replacing megakaryocytes in elderly patients
MDS with single lineage dysplasia affecting the megakaryocytic lineage — the most common clonal cause of isolated thrombocytopenia in elderly patients
Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML) in accelerated phase — causing thrombocytopenia from abnormal megakaryopoiesis in elderly adults
a: Primary Myelofibrosis — causing progressive thrombocytopenia from marrow fibrosis replacing megakaryocytes in elderly patients

q: The ELN (European LeukemiaNet) 2022 risk stratification for AML in older adults emphasizes which molecular markers as particularly important adverse prognostic factors unique to elderly AML?
o: TP53 mutations (biallelic or monoallelic with del(17p)) — associated with complex karyotype, chemoresistance, and dismal prognosis (median OS <6 months); ASXL1, RUNX1, and BCOR mutations — defining secondary-type AML with poor outcomes in elderly patients regardless of therapy intensity
FLT3-ITD mutations exclusively — the only ELN adverse marker specific to elderly AML distinguishing it from younger adult AML
NPM1 mutations with normal karyotype — paradoxically conferring adverse prognosis in elderly AML despite favorable prognosis in younger adults
IDH1 and IDH2 mutations — the primary adverse markers in elderly AML uniquely targetable by ivosidenib and enasidenib in all elderly patients
a: TP53 mutations (biallelic or monoallelic with del(17p)) — associated with complex karyotype, chemoresistance, and dismal prognosis (median OS <6 months); ASXL1, RUNX1, and BCOR mutations — defining secondary-type AML with poor outcomes in elderly patients regardless of therapy intensity

q: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) in elderly adults differs from childhood ITP in which clinical and management aspect?
o: Elderly ITP is predominantly chronic, rarely spontaneously remits, frequently harbors underlying secondary causes (CLL, MDS, medications, autoimmune disease), and carries higher bleeding risk from concurrent anticoagulation/antiplatelet use and comorbidities — requiring more aggressive treatment thresholds than childhood ITP
Elderly ITP responds better to corticosteroids than childhood ITP due to age-related immune senescence reducing autoantibody production
Elderly ITP and childhood ITP have identical response rates to IVIG and splenectomy with no age-specific management differences
Elderly ITP is exclusively caused by drug reactions while childhood ITP is exclusively post-viral — the etiology determines identical treatment in both groups
a: Elderly ITP is predominantly chronic, rarely spontaneously remits, frequently harbors underlying secondary causes (CLL, MDS, medications, autoimmune disease), and carries higher bleeding risk from concurrent anticoagulation/antiplatelet use and comorbidities — requiring more aggressive treatment thresholds than childhood ITP

q: A 3-year-old boy presents with pallor, irritability, and a hemoglobin of 7.2 g/dL. His MCV is 62 fL, RDW is 18%, serum ferritin is 4 ng/mL, and TIBC is 520 µg/dL. He drinks approximately 900 mL of cow's milk daily. What is the most appropriate next step?
o: Oral ferrous sulfate 3–6 mg/kg/day of elemental iron and reduce cow's milk intake to <500 mL/day
Intravenous iron infusion as oral iron is inadequate for severe deficiency in toddlers
Red blood cell transfusion given hemoglobin below 8 g/dL in a symptomatic child
Bone marrow biopsy to exclude aplastic anemia before initiating iron therapy
a: Oral ferrous sulfate 3–6 mg/kg/day of elemental iron and reduce cow's milk intake to <500 mL/day
ex: This toddler has classic iron deficiency anemia (IDA): microcytic hypochromic anemia (MCV 62, high RDW), very low ferritin (4 ng/mL), and elevated TIBC (520 µg/dL). Excessive cow's milk consumption (>500 mL/day) displaces iron-rich foods and causes occult GI blood loss from cow's milk protein enteropathy. First-line treatment is oral ferrous sulfate 3–6 mg/kg/day elemental iron between meals. Dietary counseling to limit milk and introduce iron-rich foods is essential. IV iron is reserved for malabsorption or non-compliance. Transfusion is only for hemodynamically unstable patients or Hgb <5 g/dL. Reticulocytosis occurs within 72 hours confirming the diagnosis.

q: A 28-year-old woman presents at 20 weeks gestation with fatigue, glossitis, and macrocytic anemia (Hgb 9.1 g/dL, MCV 108 fL). She is a strict vegan and has not been taking supplements. Serum B12 is 89 pg/mL (normal >200) and folate is normal. What is the most appropriate management?
o: Intramuscular hydroxocobalamin 1 mg daily for 7 days then weekly for 4 weeks, followed by daily oral B12 supplementation for the remainder of pregnancy
High-dose oral folic acid 5 mg/day — folate deficiency is the primary cause of macrocytic anemia in pregnancy regardless of B12 level
Red blood cell transfusion to immediately correct anemia before initiating B12 therapy given gestational age
Delay B12 treatment until after delivery as supplementation may mask underlying pernicious anemia diagnosis
a: Intramuscular hydroxocobalamin 1 mg daily for 7 days then weekly for 4 weeks, followed by daily oral B12 supplementation for the remainder of pregnancy
ex: This pregnant vegan has B12 deficiency megaloblastic anemia. B12 is exclusively found in animal products; vegans require supplementation. In pregnancy, B12 deficiency is particularly dangerous as it causes neural tube defects, maternal macrocytic anemia, and neonatal neurological damage from B12-deficient breast milk. IM hydroxocobalamin ensures reliable absorption bypassing any absorption issues. High-dose folate WITHOUT B12 correction can mask hematological features of B12 deficiency while allowing subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord to progress — never give folate alone when B12 deficiency is present. Reticulocytosis and improving Hgb are expected within 7–10 days.

q: A 45-year-old male with known alcoholic cirrhosis presents with hematemesis and is found to have Hgb 7.8 g/dL, MCV 104 fL, platelets 58 × 10⁹/L, INR 2.1, and low fibrinogen (1.1 g/L). Peripheral smear shows target cells and macrocytes. Which single underlying mechanism best explains his complex coagulopathy?
o: Liver failure causing simultaneous reduction in both procoagulant (II, V, VII, IX, X) and anticoagulant (Protein C, Protein S, antithrombin) factor synthesis creating a rebalanced but precarious hemostatic state prone to both bleeding and thrombosis
Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) from portal hypertension consuming all coagulation factors equally
Vitamin K deficiency exclusively — alcoholics have poor dietary intake causing isolated reduction in Vitamin K-dependent factors
Hypersplenism alone causing isolated thrombocytopenia and secondary coagulation factor consumption
a: Liver failure causing simultaneous reduction in both procoagulant (II, V, VII, IX, X) and anticoagulant (Protein C, Protein S, antithrombin) factor synthesis creating a rebalanced but precarious hemostatic state prone to both bleeding and thrombosis
ex: Liver disease causes a complex coagulopathy because the liver synthesizes virtually all coagulation factors AND natural anticoagulants. The INR is prolonged from reduced procoagulants but does not reflect the true hemostatic state because Protein C and antithrombin are equally reduced. This creates a 'rebalanced hemostasis' that is actually quite fragile. Additional contributors in this patient include: thrombocytopenia from hypersplenism and thrombopoietin deficiency; macrocytosis from alcohol and folate deficiency; and target cells from altered cholesterol:phospholipid ratio in membranes. Standard coagulation tests (INR/APTT) overestimate bleeding risk. Thromboelastography (TEG/ROTEM) provides better hemostatic assessment before procedures.

q: A 6-year-old girl with known HbSS disease presents in January with temperature 38.8°C, WBC 18 × 10⁹/L, and no focal infection source. She is on penicillin prophylaxis and is up-to-date on vaccines. What is the most critical immediate management step?
o: Blood cultures followed by immediate IV ceftriaxone 50–80 mg/kg within 60 minutes of triage — empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics are mandatory for all febrile SCD children regardless of clinical appearance due to life-threatening risk of overwhelming post-splenectomy sepsis
Discharge with oral amoxicillin and review in 24 hours if penicillin-resistant Streptococcus pneumoniae is unlikely given vaccination status
Reassurance — fever is expected from vaso-occlusive crisis in SCD and antibiotics are only indicated if blood culture returns positive
IV hydration and morphine for presumed vaso-occlusive crisis causing physiological fever with CBC monitoring every 4 hours
a: Blood cultures followed by immediate IV ceftriaxone 50–80 mg/kg within 60 minutes of triage — empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics are mandatory for all febrile SCD children regardless of clinical appearance due to life-threatening risk of overwhelming post-splenectomy sepsis
ex: Children with HbSS have functional asplenia from repeated splenic infarction, making them exquisitely susceptible to overwhelming sepsis from encapsulated organisms — particularly Streptococcus pneumoniae — with mortality rates up to 25% if treatment is delayed. The 'well-appearing' febrile SCD child is a medical emergency. Current guidelines (ASH, BSH) mandate blood cultures and empirical IV ceftriaxone within 60 minutes of presentation, regardless of vaccination status or clinical appearance. Penicillin prophylaxis does not eliminate risk from antibiotic-resistant strains. The child must be admitted for observation. Rapid clinical deterioration can occur within hours. This scenario is a time-critical emergency analogous to sepsis protocol activation.

q: A 72-year-old man presents with 3 months of fatigue, back pain, and confusion. Laboratory findings: Hgb 9.2 g/dL, calcium 12.8 mg/dL, creatinine 2.4 mg/dL, total protein 10.8 g/dL, albumin 3.2 g/dL. Serum protein electrophoresis shows an M-spike of 4.2 g/dL (IgG kappa). Skeletal survey shows multiple lytic lesions. What is the diagnosis and the immediate treatment priority?
o: Multiple Myeloma with hypercalcemic crisis — immediate IV fluid resuscitation (0.9% NaCl), zoledronic acid (bisphosphonate), calcitonin for rapid calcium lowering, and initiation of dexamethasone while arranging urgent hematology consultation
MGUS with coincidental hypercalcemia from primary hyperparathyroidism — parathyroidectomy is the priority before myeloma treatment
Waldenstrom Macroglobulinemia with hyperviscosity syndrome — plasmapheresis is the immediate priority over calcium correction
Metastatic solid tumor with lytic bone lesions mimicking myeloma — CT-PET scan before initiating any myeloma-specific treatment
a: Multiple Myeloma with hypercalcemic crisis — immediate IV fluid resuscitation (0.9% NaCl), zoledronic acid (bisphosphonate), calcitonin for rapid calcium owering, and initiation of dexamethasone while arranging urgent hematology consultation
ex: This patient has symptomatic multiple myeloma meeting CRAB criteria: hyperCalcemia (12.8 mg/dL), Renal insufficiency (Cr 2.4), Anemia (Hgb 9.2), and lytic Bone lesions. IgG kappa M-spike at 4.2 g/dL is diagnostic. Hypercalcemia of malignancy (calcium >12 mg/dL with symptoms of confusion) is a medical emergency requiring: (1) IV saline hydration to expand intravascular volume and promote calciuric diuresis; (2) Zoledronic acid to inhibit osteoclast-mediated bone resorption (mechanism of myeloma hypercalcemia); (3) Calcitonin for acute rapid reduction (takes effect in 4–6 hours); (4) Dexamethasone — directly cytotoxic to myeloma cells and reduces hypercalcemia. After stabilization, definitive myeloma treatment (VRd: bortezomib-lenalidomide-dexamethasone) is initiated.

q: A 55-year-old woman undergoing cardiac surgery receives unfractionated heparin on day 1. By day 8, her platelet count has fallen from 220 × 10⁹/L to 68 × 10⁹/L. She develops right leg swelling and ultrasound confirms DVT. Her 4T score is 7 (high probability). What is the correct immediate management?
o: Immediately stop ALL heparin (including flushes and heparin-coated catheters), send anti-PF4/heparin antibody (ELISA), and initiate argatroban (direct thrombin inhibitor) — warfarin must NOT be started until platelets recover above 150 × 10⁹/L
Continue heparin and add warfarin immediately — the DVT requires therapeutic anticoagulation
Switch to low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin) which has less HIT cross-reactivity and initiate warfarin
Transfuse platelets to >100 × 10⁹/L before anticoagulation initiation to minimize bleeding during DVT treatment
a: Immediately stop ALL heparin (including flushes and heparin-coated catheters), send anti-PF4/heparin antibody (ELISA), and initiate argatroban (direct thrombin inhibitor) — warfarin must NOT be started until platelets recover above 150 × 10⁹/L
ex: HIT type II is a life-threatening immune-mediated prothrombotic disorder. The 4T score of 7 indicates high pre-test probability. Key management principles: (1) STOP ALL heparin immediately — including line flushes, heparin-bonded catheters, and LMWH (>90% HIT antibody cross-reactivity with LMWH); (2) Send serotonin release assay (SRA) as gold standard and PF4/heparin ELISA; (3) Start non-heparin anticoagulation immediately — argatroban (preferred if normal hepatic function) or bivalirudin; (4) NEVER start warfarin acutely — warfarin depletes Protein C before other factors creating a transient hypercoagulable state causing warfarin-induced venous limb gangrene; wait until platelets >150 × 10⁹/L with 5+ days of argatroban overlap. Platelet transfusion is contraindicated — it fuels the thrombotic process ('fuel on the fire').

q: A 16-year-old boy presents with 3 days of fever, jaundice, dark urine, and severe anemia (Hgb 6.1 g/dL). He recently returned from a holiday in Nigeria and was prescribed primaquine for malaria prophylaxis. Peripheral smear shows bite cells and Heinz bodies. Reticulocyte count is 12%. DAT is negative. What is the diagnosis?
o: G6PD deficiency with oxidative hemolytic crisis triggered by primaquine — the diagnosis is confirmed by G6PD enzyme assay (best performed 2–3 months after acute episode to avoid false-normal from high reticulocyte G6PD activity)
Autoimmune hemolytic anemia — negative DAT makes this diagnosis unlikely but does not exclude IgA-mediated AIHA
Hereditary spherocytosis presenting at adolescence with hemolytic crisis triggered by infection
Falciparum malaria causing direct red cell invasion and hemolysis — blood film for malaria parasites is the urgent investigation
a: G6PD deficiency with oxidative hemolytic crisis triggered by primaquine — the diagnosis is confirmed by G6PD enzyme assay (best performed 2–3 months after acute episode to avoid false-normal from high reticulocyte G6PD activity)
ex: G6PD deficiency is X-linked (affects males primarily) and is particularly prevalent in sub-Saharan African populations. G6PD protects red cells from oxidative stress by maintaining NADPH and glutathione levels. Primaquine (8-aminoquinoline antimalarial) is a potent oxidant triggering acute intravascular hemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients. Classic features: sudden onset hemolytic anemia, jaundice, dark urine (hemoglobinuria), bite cells (degmacytes — from splenic removal of Heinz body-containing RBCs), and Heinz bodies (precipitated oxidized hemoglobin) on supravital stain. DAT is negative (non-immune hemolysis). G6PD assay is best done during recovery — reticulocytes have higher G6PD activity causing false-normal results during acute crisis. Management: stop primaquine, supportive care, transfusion if severe. Use artemisinin-based therapy for malaria in G6PD-deficient patients.

q: A 35-year-old woman with SLE presents with Hgb 8.8 g/dL, platelets 42 × 10⁹/L, LDH 890 IU/L, schistocytes on blood film, creatinine 1.8 mg/dL, and neurological symptoms (confusion, headache). Her ADAMTS13 activity is <5% with positive inhibitor. What is the most urgent intervention?
o: Urgent therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) — 1.5 plasma volume daily using fresh frozen plasma as replacement fluid, combined with corticosteroids and rituximab for refractory acquired TTP
IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days for SLE flare causing thrombocytopenia and hemolytic anemia without plasma exchange
Platelet transfusion to >50 × 10⁹/L before initiating plasma exchange to prevent procedure-related hemorrhage
Eculizumab (anti-C5) as first-line treatment for complement-mediated TMA in SLE patients
a: Urgent therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) — 1.5 plasma volume daily using fresh frozen plasma as replacement fluid, combined with corticosteroids and rituximab for refractory acquired TTP
ex: This patient has acquired TTP: the classic pentad (MAHA + thrombocytopenia + neurological symptoms + renal impairment + fever) with ADAMTS13 <5% and inhibitor confirming immune-mediated TTP (iTTP). SLE is a known trigger for acquired TTP. Plasma exchange is the cornerstone of treatment — it simultaneously removes the anti-ADAMTS13 inhibitory antibody AND replenishes ADAMTS13 enzyme. Without plasma exchange, iTTP mortality is >90%. Key principles: (1) Do NOT give platelet transfusions — this is contraindicated in TTP ('fuel on the fire') unless life-threatening hemorrhage; (2) FFP is the replacement fluid of choice (contains ADAMTS13); (3) Corticosteroids suppress autoantibody production; (4) Rituximab added for SLE-associated or refractory TTP; (5) Caplacizumab can be added to prevent early relapses. ADAMTS13 distinguishes TTP from aHUS (complement-mediated, normal ADAMTS13) and STEC-HUS.

q: A 58-year-old male presents with splenomegaly, WBC 145 × 10⁹/L, differential showing the full spectrum of myeloid maturation with <2% blasts, basophilia, Hgb 11.2 g/dL, and platelets 890 × 10⁹/L. BCR-ABL1 PCR is positive at 98% IS. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment, and what monitoring interval is recommended?
o: Imatinib 400 mg daily (or second-generation TKI dasatinib/nilotinib as alternatives) with BCR-ABL1 PCR monitoring by IS every 3 months — target is BCR-ABL1 ≤10% IS at 3 months (early molecular response), ≤1% IS at 6 months, and ≤0.1% IS (MMR) by 12 months
Hydroxyurea to reduce WBC below 10 × 10⁹/L followed by allogeneic HSCT as definitive first-line therapy for all CML patients
Intensive induction chemotherapy (7+3 cytarabine + daunorubicin) for leukostasis prevention given WBC >100 × 10⁹/L
Ponatinib as first-line therapy — the most potent TKI should be used upfront to achieve the fastest molecular response in all newly diagnosed CML
a: Imatinib 400 mg daily (or second-generation TKI dasatinib/nilotinib as alternatives) with BCR-ABL1 PCR monitoring by IS every 3 months — target is BCR-ABL1 ≤10% IS at 3 months (early molecular response), ≤1% IS at 6 months, and ≤0.1% IS (MMR) by 12 months
ex: This is CML in chronic phase: leukocytosis with full myeloid maturation spectrum, basophilia, thrombocytosis, splenomegaly, and positive BCR-ABL1. TKIs have transformed CML from a fatal disease to one with near-normal life expectancy. Imatinib remains an acceptable first-line option (generic, cost-effective) though second-generation TKIs (dasatinib, nilotinib, bosutinib) achieve faster and deeper molecular responses. ELN 2020 response milestones guide treatment: failure to achieve BCR-ABL1 ≤10% IS at 3 months should trigger switch to 2G TKI or mutation analysis. T315I gatekeeper mutation is resistant to all except ponatinib and asciminib. Allogeneic HSCT is reserved for TKI-resistant/intolerant patients. Treatment-free remission (TFR) is achievable in ~40% of patients achieving deep molecular response (MR4.5).

q: A 4-year-old girl is brought in with 2-day history of epistaxis and gum bleeding following a viral upper respiratory infection. Platelet count is 8 × 10⁹/L, Hgb 12.1 g/dL, WBC 7.8 × 10⁹/L (normal differential). Bone marrow biopsy is not performed. No lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. What is the diagnosis and management approach?
o: Acute childhood ITP — observation alone is appropriate per ASH 2019 guidelines as the majority of children with non-severe bleeding resolve spontaneously within 6 months without pharmacological treatment; IVIG or corticosteroids reserved for severe/wet bleeding
Acute leukemia — the platelet count of 8 × 10⁹/L mandates urgent bone marrow biopsy before any management decision
Aplastic anemia — post-viral bone marrow failure causing isolated thrombocytopenia requiring ATG and cyclosporine
Immediate platelet transfusion to raise count above 20 × 10⁹/L to prevent intracranial hemorrhage in all thrombocytopenic children
a: Acute childhood ITP — observation alone is appropriate per ASH 2019 guidelines as the majority of children with non-severe bleeding resolve spontaneously within 6 months without pharmacological treatment; IVIG or corticosteroids reserved for severe/wet bleeding
ex: Classic acute post-viral ITP in a toddler: isolated thrombocytopenia (Hgb and WBC are normal — ruling out leukemia), no organomegaly, preceded by viral illness, minor mucocutaneous bleeding only. ASH 2019 guidelines recommend observation for children with non-severe ITP (skin purpura/minor mucous membrane bleeding) regardless of platelet count, because: (1) spontaneous resolution occurs in >80% within 6 months; (2) ICH risk is only 0.1–0.5%; (3) treatment side effects may exceed disease risk. IVIG 0.8–1 g/kg or short-course prednisolone is used for wet purpura, significant mucosal bleeding, or if treatment is required before procedures. The key distinction from leukemia is isolated thrombocytopenia with normal other cell lines and no blast cells on smear.

q: A 67-year-old woman presents with 6-month progressive fatigue, weight loss, drenching night sweats, and massive splenomegaly. CBC: WBC 3.2 × 10⁹/L, Hgb 8.8 g/dL, platelets 88 × 10⁹/L. Peripheral smear shows leukoerythroblastic picture with numerous teardrop cells (dacryocytes). Bone marrow biopsy shows MF-3 reticulin fibrosis. JAK2 V617F positive. What is the diagnosis and which scoring system guides prognosis?
o: Primary Myelofibrosis (PMF) — Dynamic International Prognostic Scoring System (DIPSS) uses age >65, Hgb <10 g/dL, WBC >25 × 10⁹/L, blasts ≥1% in blood, and constitutional symptoms to stratify risk; high-risk PMF may be considered for allogeneic HSCT while ruxolitinib reduces splenomegaly and symptoms in intermediate/high-risk patients
Myelodysplastic syndrome with myelofibrosis — IPSS-R is the appropriate scoring system for MDS with secondary fibrosis
Hairy Cell Leukemia with bone marrow fibrosis — TRAP staining and CD103 flow cytometry confirm the diagnosis requiring cladribine
Chronic Myeloid Leukemia in accelerated phase — BCR-ABL1 PCR must be performed before attributing fibrosis to JAK2-positive MPN
a: Primary Myelofibrosis (PMF) — Dynamic International Prognostic Scoring System (DIPSS) uses age >65, Hgb <10 g/dL, WBC >25 × 10⁹/L, blasts ≥1% in blood, and constitutional symptoms to stratify risk; high-risk PMF may be considered for allogeneic HSCT while ruxolitinib reduces splenomegaly and symptoms in intermediate/high-risk patients
ex: The leukoerythroblastic blood picture (nucleated RBCs + immature granulocytes) with dacryocytes (teardrop cells squeezed through fibrotic marrow), massive splenomegaly from extramedullary hematopoiesis, and MF-3 fibrosis on biopsy are pathognomonic of PMF. JAK2 V617F confirms the MPN diagnosis (~60% of PMF). DIPSS-plus adds karyotype, transfusion dependence, and platelets <100 × 10⁹/L. This patient has multiple DIPSS adverse factors (age >65, Hgb <10, constitutional symptoms) placing her in intermediate-2/high-risk. Ruxolitinib (JAK1/2 inhibitor) reduces spleen volume and constitutional symptoms but does not alter the natural history. Allogeneic HSCT is the only curative option — this patient should be evaluated for transplant eligibility. Fedratinib and pacritinib are alternative JAK inhibitors for ruxolitinib-resistant/intolerant patients.

q: A 52-year-old male with no prior medical history presents with a left-sided unprovoked DVT confirmed on Doppler ultrasound. D-dimer is markedly elevated. He is otherwise well. Thrombophilia screen including Factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A, protein C, protein S, antithrombin, lupus anticoagulant, and anticardiolipin antibodies are all normal. How long should anticoagulation be continued?
o: Extended (indefinite) anticoagulation should be strongly considered for all unprovoked proximal DVT — the annual recurrence risk without anticoagulation is ~10% per year and the benefit of extended treatment generally outweighs bleeding risk in patients with no contraindications; formal shared decision-making using HER-DOO2 or HERDOO-2 tools assists decision-making
3 months only — the absence of thrombophilia confirms a low recurrence risk making short-duration treatment sufficient for unprovoked DVT
6 months followed by low-dose aspirin 100 mg daily as indefinite VTE secondary prevention in thrombophilia-negative patients
12 months as the standard duration for all unprovoked DVT regardless of patient factors or shared decision-making
a: Extended (indefinite) anticoagulation should be strongly considered for all unprovoked proximal DVT — the annual recurrence risk without anticoagulation is ~10% per year and the benefit of extended treatment generally outweighs bleeding risk in patients with no contraindications; formal shared decision-making using HER-DOO2 or HERDOO-2 tools assists decision-making
ex: Unprovoked VTE (no identifiable reversible provoking factor) carries a high recurrence risk: ~10% in year 1, ~30% at 5 years, ~50% at 10 years after stopping anticoagulation. The negative thrombophilia screen does NOT lower recurrence risk sufficiently to recommend stopping anticoagulation — isolated heterozygous FVL/PT G20210A are weak risk factors. Current ASH/ACCP guidelines recommend extended anticoagulation after first unprovoked proximal DVT unless high bleeding risk. Low-dose apixaban (2.5 mg BID) or rivaroxaban (10 mg OD) are safe extended-treatment options. Males with unprovoked DVT have consistently higher recurrence risk than females — an additional factor supporting extended treatment. The decision must be individualized through shared decision-making considering bleeding risk, patient preference, and quality of life.

q: A 25-year-old woman presents with episodic abdominal pain, headaches, and fatigue for 2 years. She has had two unexplained episodes of DVT and one portal vein thrombosis. CBC shows Hgb 9.8 g/dL, WBC 3.1 × 10⁹/L, platelets 92 × 10⁹/L. LDH is markedly elevated. Urinalysis shows hemosiderinuria. Flow cytometry of peripheral blood shows a GPI-deficient clone (CD55/CD59 negative) of 45%. What is the diagnosis and first-line targeted therapy?
o: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH) — eculizumab (anti-C5 complement inhibitor) 600 mg IV weekly for 4 doses then 900 mg every 2 weeks is the first-line therapy for hemolytic/thrombotic PNH; meningococcal vaccination is mandatory before eculizumab initiation
Cold Agglutinin Disease — sutimlimab (anti-C1s) is first-line therapy for complement-mediated hemolysis
Autoimmune hemolytic anemia with secondary hypercoagulability — corticosteroids are first-line before complement pathway-targeted therapy
Aplastic Anemia with secondary PNH clone — ATG and cyclosporine should precede complement inhibitor therapy in all PNH patients with cytopenias
a: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH) — eculizumab (anti-C5 complement inhibitor) 600 mg IV weekly for 4 doses then 900 mg every 2 weeks is the first-line therapy for hemolytic/thrombotic PNH; meningococcal vaccination is mandatory before eculizumab initiation
ex: PNH is an acquired clonal stem cell disorder caused by somatic PIG-A mutation causing GPI-anchor protein deficiency (CD55/CD59). Classic features include: episodic hemolysis (darker morning urine from nocturnal complement activation), pancytopenia, thrombosis at unusual sites (hepatic vein — Budd-Chiari, portal vein, cerebral sinovenous), and hemosiderinuria from chronic intravascular hemolysis. A GPI-negative clone >10% with hemolysis or thrombosis is an indication for complement inhibitor therapy. Eculizumab (C5 inhibitor) prevents MAC formation, eliminating intravascular hemolysis and drastically reducing thrombotic risk. Ravulizumab (longer-acting C5 inhibitor, every 8 weeks) and iptacopan (oral factor B inhibitor addressing residual extravascular hemolysis) are newer options. Critically, eculizumab blocks terminal complement leaving patients susceptible to Neisseria meningitidis — vaccination ≥2 weeks before initiation is mandatory, plus prophylactic penicillin.

q: A 7-year-old boy with HbSS disease is found to have bilateral TCD velocities of 210 cm/s. He has had no prior overt stroke. His parents are concerned about the need for regular blood transfusions and ask about alternatives. What is the evidence-based counseling you would provide?
o: Chronic transfusion therapy (maintaining HbS <30%) is first-line per STOP trial (90% stroke risk reduction vs. observation); the TWiTCH trial showed hydroxyurea is non-inferior to transfusion for maintaining TCD velocity in children without severe vasculopathy on MRA — transition to hydroxyurea is a validated option after initial transfusion normalization of TCD
Hydroxyurea can be initiated immediately without any transfusion period — TWiTCH trial supports hydroxyurea as first-line for all TCD-abnormal patients
Aspirin antiplatelet therapy reduces cerebrovascular risk in TCD-abnormal HbSS children as effectively as transfusion therapy
Repeat TCD in 6 months — a single abnormal reading is insufficient to initiate prophylactic transfusion therapy in asymptomatic children
a: Chronic transfusion therapy (maintaining HbS <30%) is first-line per STOP trial (90% stroke risk reduction vs. observation); the TWiTCH trial showed hydroxyurea is non-inferior to transfusion for maintaining TCD velocity in children without severe vasculopathy on MRA — transition to hydroxyurea is a validated option after initial transfusion normalization of TCD
ex: The STOP trial (1998) was a landmark RCT demonstrating chronic transfusion therapy reduced first stroke risk by 90% in HbSS children with TCD ≥200 cm/s. An isolated TCD ≥200 cm/s is an absolute indication to initiate transfusion. The TWiTCH trial (2016) demonstrated that after ≥1 year of transfusion with normalized TCD and no severe vasculopathy on MRA, children could safely transition to hydroxyurea. Key caveats for transition: MRA brain imaging must exclude severe intracranial stenosis; HbF must reach ≥20% before discontinuing transfusions; and TCD must be maintained <170 cm/s. Families can be counseled that transfusion is the evidence-based bridge and hydroxyurea transition is a validated long-term strategy. Iron chelation is also required during transfusion therapy.

q: A 78-year-old woman on rivaroxaban for AF presents to the emergency department with sudden onset severe headache and CT scan showing acute subarachnoid hemorrhage. She took her last rivaroxaban dose 4 hours ago. Her creatinine is 1.1 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate immediate hematological intervention?
o: Andexanet alfa (anti-Xa reversal agent) IV bolus + infusion — specifically reverses factor Xa inhibitors (rivaroxaban, apixaban) restoring hemostasis within minutes; andexanet alfa is FDA-approved for life-threatening bleeding on anti-Xa inhibitors
Idarucizumab — the appropriate reversal agent for all DOAC-related life-threatening bleeding

4-factor Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (4F-PCC) — acceptable alternative for anti-Xa reversal when andexanet alfa is unavailable, given its procoagulant activity replenishing Factors II, VII, IX, and X
Fresh Frozen Plasma (FFP) 15 mL/kg — sufficient to reverse rivaroxaban anticoagulation in the setting of intracranial hemorrhage
a: Andexanet alfa (anti-Xa reversal agent) IV bolus + infusion — specifically reverses factor Xa inhibitors (rivaroxaban, apixaban) restoring hemostasis within minutes; andexanet alfa is FDA-approved for life-threatening bleeding on anti-Xa inhibitors
ex: Life-threatening DOAC-related bleeding requires specific reversal. Andexanet alfa is a modified recombinant Factor Xa decoy molecule that binds and sequesters rivaroxaban and apixaban, immediately reversing anti-Xa activity. Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran (direct thrombin inhibitor) — NOT Factor Xa inhibitors. Where andexanet alfa is unavailable, 4F-PCC (25–50 IU/kg) is an acceptable alternative — it bypasses the anticoagulant effect by providing procoagulant factors. FFP requires large volumes (15 mL/kg = ~1,000 mL in this patient) and takes time for thawing/infusion — insufficient for acute ICH reversal. Additionally: neurosurgical consultation, BP management (target systolic <140 mmHg), and urgent coagulation assessment should occur simultaneously. The decision to restart anticoagulation after ICH requires careful individualized risk-benefit analysis.

q: A 42-year-old woman presents with 6 months of progressive breathlessness, fatigue, and pallor. Hgb is 6.2 g/dL, MCV 78 fL (low normal), reticulocyte count 0.5%, LDH 320 IU/L (normal), WBC 1.8 × 10⁹/L, neutrophils 0.6 × 10⁹/L, platelets 28 × 10⁹/L. Bone marrow trephine shows <5% cellularity with fat replacement and no dysplasia. What is the diagnosis and first-line treatment?
o: Severe Aplastic Anemia (SAA) — first-line treatment for patients without an HLA-matched sibling donor is horse-ATG (hATG) + cyclosporine + eltrombopag; patients with matched sibling donor under age 40 proceed directly to allogeneic HSCT as first-line therapy
Hypoplastic MDS — azacitidine (hypomethylating agent) is the appropriate treatment without HSCT consideration
Myelofibrosis with aplastic presentation — JAK inhibitor therapy (ruxolitinib) addresses the underlying fibrosis causing pancytopenia
Acute leukemia with bone marrow failure — lumbar puncture and urgent 7+3 induction chemotherapy without delay
a: Severe Aplastic Anemia (SAA) — first-line treatment for patients without an HLA-matched sibling donor is horse-ATG (hATG) + cyclosporine + eltrombopag; patients with matched sibling donor under age 40 proceed directly to allogeneic HSCT as first-line therapy
ex: SAA diagnosis requires ≥2 of: neutrophils <0.5 × 10⁹/L, platelets <20 × 10⁹/L, reticulocytes <20 × 10⁹/L — plus hypocellular marrow (<25%). This patient meets all criteria. Treatment algorithm: (1) Patients ≤35–40 years with matched sibling donor → allogeneic HSCT (curative, >90% OS); (2) Patients without MSD or over 40 → hATG (Atgam) + cyclosporine + eltrombopag (triple immunosuppression — NEJM 2017 landmark trial showing eltrombopag improves response rates from ~41% to ~68%); rabbit-ATG (thymoglobulin) is inferior to hATG for first-line SAA. Response is assessed at 3–6 months. Non-responders receive alternative immunosuppression or allo-HSCT with unrelated donor. MDS and PNH must be excluded before treatment as they can present with hypocellular marrow.
