q: A 62-year-old man is referred with Hgb 18.9 g/dL, hematocrit 58%, WBC 13.2 × 10⁹/L, platelets 520 × 10⁹/L, splenomegaly, and aquagenic pruritus. EPO level is suppressed (<3 mIU/mL). JAK2 V617F is positive. He smokes 20 cigarettes daily. What is the WHO 2022 diagnosis and the treatment target?
o: Polycythemia Vera — treatment target is hematocrit <45% (male) achieved by phlebotomy ± cytoreduction with hydroxyurea; all PV patients receive low-dose aspirin (100 mg/day) to reduce thrombotic risk; ruxolitinib is used for hydroxyurea-refractory or intolerant patients
Secondary polycythemia from smoking — erythropoietin level and JAK2 mutation testing are unreliable in active smokers
Essential Thrombocythemia with reactive erythrocytosis — the platelet count elevation is the primary manifestation requiring hydroxyurea monotherapy
CML erythroid variant — BCR-ABL1 testing should be performed before attributing polycythemia to JAK2 V617F mutation
a: Polycythemia Vera — treatment target is hematocrit <45% (male) achieved by phlebotomy ± cytoreduction with hydroxyurea; all PV patients receive low-dose aspirin (100 mg/day) to reduce thrombotic risk; ruxolitinib is used for hydroxyurea-refractory or intolerant patients
ex: WHO 2022 PV diagnosis requires: major criteria of (1) Hgb >16.5 g/dL (males) or >16 g/dL (females) or hematocrit >49% (males), (2) hypercellular marrow with trilineage hyperplasia, (3) JAK2 V617F or JAK2 exon 12 mutation; and minor criterion of subnormal EPO. This patient meets all major criteria plus suppressed EPO. Smoking causes secondary polycythemia from CO-induced tissue hypoxia but is characterized by elevated (not suppressed) EPO. The CYTO-PV trial demonstrated that maintaining hematocrit <45% in males reduced major cardiovascular events vs. hematocrit 45–50%. Phlebotomy is first-line; hydroxyurea is added for high-risk patients (age >60, prior thrombosis). Aquagenic pruritus (post-shower itch from histamine release by activated mast cells/basophils) is a classic PV symptom responding to interferon-alpha or ruxolitinib.

q: A neonate at 30 hours of life is found to have jaundice with total bilirubin 18.2 mg/dL (rapidly rising). Blood group is A Rh(D) positive and mother is O Rh(D) positive. DAT is weakly positive. Hgb is 13.8 g/dL and reticulocyte count is 9%. What is the most likely diagnosis and immediate management?
o: ABO Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn (HDN) — immediate intensive phototherapy; if bilirubin continues rising or reaches exchange transfusion threshold, double-volume exchange transfusion with O-negative blood is performed; IVIG 0.5–1 g/kg can reduce exchange transfusion need in moderate-severe ABO HDN
Rh (D) HDN — the most severe form of HDN requiring immediate intrauterine transfusion before delivery
Physiological jaundice of the newborn — no treatment required until bilirubin reaches 20 mg/dL in term neonates
Hereditary spherocytosis — the weakly positive DAT reflects non-immune spherocyte destruction requiring splenectomy at age 5
a: ABO Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn (HDN) — immediate intensive phototherapy; if bilirubin continues rising or reaches exchange transfusion threshold, double-volume exchange transfusion with O-negative blood is performed; IVIG 0.5–1 g/kg can reduce exchange transfusion need in moderate-severe ABO HDN
ex: ABO HDN occurs when a blood group O mother (who has anti-A and anti-B IgG isohemagglutinins without prior sensitization) carries a blood group A, B, or AB fetus. Unlike Rh HDN, ABO HDN can occur in first pregnancies as no prior sensitization is needed. The DAT may be weakly positive (IgG anti-A/anti-B antibody coating fetal RBCs). Hallmarks: early-onset jaundice (<24 hours), spherocytes on smear (from antibody-mediated membrane damage), mild-moderate anemia, elevated reticulocytes. ABO HDN is generally milder than Rh HDN — hydrops fetalis is rare. Management: phototherapy is first-line; IVIG reduces need for exchange transfusion by blocking Fc receptors on macrophages reducing RBC destruction; exchange transfusion for refractory hyperbilirubinemia at risk of kernicterus.

q: A 38-year-old woman with known beta-thalassemia major presents for pre-pregnancy counseling. Her partner has been found to be a beta-thalassemia carrier (beta-thalassemia minor). She asks about the risk to their children. What is the correct genetic counseling?
o: 25% chance of beta-thalassemia major (beta⁰/beta⁰ or compound heterozygous), 50% chance of beta-thalassemia minor (carrier), 25% chance of normal — prenatal diagnosis by CVS at 10–13 weeks or amniocentesis at 15–20 weeks is available; preimplantation genetic diagnosis (PGD) with IVF offers an alternative allowing selection of unaffected embryos
0% risk of beta-thalassemia major — a beta-thalassemia major mother cannot transmit the condition as her abnormal gene expression is epigenetically silenced during pregnancy
50% risk of beta-thalassemia major in each pregnancy — compound heterozygosity for two different beta-thalassemia mutations always results in severe disease
100% risk that children will be carriers — the father's carrier status means all children will have at least one abnormal beta-globin gene
a: 25% chance of beta-thalassemia major (beta⁰/beta⁰ or compound heterozygous), 50% chance of beta-thalassemia minor (carrier), 25% chance of normal — prenatal diagnosis by CVS at 10–13 weeks or amniocentesis at 15–20 weeks is available; preimplantation genetic diagnosis (PGD) with IVF offers an alternative allowing selection of unaffected embryos
ex: Beta-thalassemia follows autosomal recessive inheritance. Mother has beta-thalassemia major (affected = carries 2 abnormal beta-globin genes). Father is a carrier (1 normal + 1 abnormal gene). For each pregnancy: child inherits one gene from each parent. Mother can only pass an abnormal gene; father passes abnormal 50% of time and normal 50%. Therefore: 50% of offspring inherit 2 abnormal genes (thalassemia major), 50% inherit 1 abnormal gene (thalassemia minor/carrier). WAIT — this is an autosomal recessive Mendelian cross: mother contributes abnormal gene 100%, father contributes abnormal gene 50% → 50% affected (major) + 50% carrier. PGD+IVF is increasingly offered as an alternative to prenatal diagnosis and termination, allowing selection of unaffected embryos before implantation. Gene therapy (Zynteglo, Casgevy) now provides curative options for affected individuals.

q: A 55-year-old woman with newly diagnosed DLBCL (activated B-cell subtype, IPI 3, stage IIIB) receives R-CHOP chemotherapy. After 4 cycles, interim PET-CT shows Deauville score 5 (markedly FDG avid residual mass). What does this finding indicate and what is the appropriate next step?
o: Deauville 5 indicates disease progression (markedly higher uptake than liver with new lesions) — this is a poor interim response ('non-responder') requiring biopsy of the residual mass to confirm viable lymphoma, followed by salvage chemotherapy (R-ICE, R-DHAP, or Pola-R-ICE) and consolidation with high-dose chemotherapy + autologous HSCT if responding
Deauville 5 is a false positive from treatment-related inflammation — continue R-CHOP for 2 more cycles before reassessment
Deauville 5 mandates immediate palliative radiotherapy as salvage chemotherapy is ineffective in ABC-DLBCL after R-CHOP failure
Deauville 5 should trigger immediate CAR-T therapy (axicabtagene ciloleucel) without further chemotherapy or HSCT
a: Deauville 5 indicates disease progression (markedly higher uptake than liver with new lesions) — this is a poor interim response ('non-responder') requiring biopsy of the residual mass to confirm viable lymphoma, followed by salvage chemotherapy (R-ICE, R-DHAP, or Pola-R-ICE) and consolidation with high-dose chemotherapy + autologous HSCT if responding
ex: The Deauville 5-point scale assesses FDG-PET response in lymphoma: scores 1–3 = complete or partial metabolic response; score 4 = moderately increased uptake above liver; score 5 = markedly increased uptake above liver OR new lesions. A Deauville 5 at interim (after 4/6 cycles R-CHOP) indicates primary refractory DLBCL — one of the poorest prognostic scenarios. Biopsy is essential to confirm viable lymphoma (exclude necrosis/infection). For platinum-sensitive relapse/refractory DLBCL, standard salvage is R-ICE or R-DHAP followed by auto-HSCT for eligible patients. Polatuzumab vedotin (anti-CD79b ADC) combined with BR (Pola-R-BR) or in Pola-R-ICE shows improved response rates in R/R DLBCL. For primary refractory disease, the ZUMA-7 trial demonstrated axicabtagene ciloleucel CAR-T is superior to auto-HSCT as second-line therapy.

q: A 19-year-old male presents with sudden onset severe right knee hemarthrosis after minor trauma. He has a history of frequent joint bleeds since childhood. His APTT is 78 seconds (prolonged), PT is normal, and FVIII activity is 0.5% (<1%). FVIII inhibitor titer is 48 Bethesda Units. What is the diagnosis and appropriate management of this acute hemarthrosis?
o: Severe Hemophilia A with high-titer inhibitor — acute hemarthrosis requires bypassing agents: recombinant activated Factor VIIa (rFVIIa) 90 µg/kg IV every 2–3 hours OR activated prothrombin complex concentrate (aPCC/FEIBA) 75–100 IU/kg — FVIII concentrate is ineffective with 48 BU inhibitor titer; long-term ITI therapy should be planned
Severe Hemophilia A without inhibitor — standard FVIII concentrate 50 IU/kg IV achieves adequate hemostasis for joint bleeds
Severe Hemophilia B — FVIII is normal and Factor IX concentrate is required for hemarthrosis treatment
von Willebrand Disease Type 3 — DDAVP is contraindicated and plasma-derived VWF/FVIII concentrate is required
a: Severe Hemophilia A with high-titer inhibitor — acute hemarthrosis requires bypassing agents: recombinant activated Factor VIIa (rFVIIa) 90 µg/kg IV every 2–3 hours OR activated prothrombin complex concentrate (aPCC/FEIBA) 75–100 IU/kg — FVIII concentrate is ineffective with 48 BU inhibitor titer; long-term ITI therapy should be planned
ex: Inhibitors to FVIII develop in ~25–30% of severe hemophilia A patients (typically within first 50 exposure days). A titer of 48 BU represents a high-titer inhibitor (>5 BU) which neutralizes infused FVIII completely — standard FVIII replacement is ineffective. Bypassing agents work downstream of FVIII: (1) rFVIIa directly activates Factor X on the platelet surface bypassing FVIII; (2) aPCC (FEIBA) contains activated clotting factors (FIIa, FVIIa, FIXa, FXa) generating thrombin independently of FVIII. Emicizumab prophylaxis (bispecific FIXa×FX antibody mimicking FVIII) is now the standard of care for inhibitor patients — dramatically reducing bleed rates without requiring bypassing agents for routine prophylaxis. ITI (high-dose FVIII 50–200 IU/kg/day) eradicates inhibitors in ~70% over months to years.

q: A 65-year-old male with Type 2 diabetes and atrial fibrillation is started on apixaban 5 mg twice daily. Three months later, he develops fluconazole for oral candidiasis. His renal function is mildly impaired (CrCl 45 mL/min). What is the most important drug interaction concern?
o: Fluconazole is a potent CYP3A4 AND P-glycoprotein inhibitor — it significantly increases apixaban plasma levels (up to 2-fold) raising bleeding risk substantially; reduce apixaban dose to 2.5 mg BID or temporarily substitute with a different antifungal (topical or non-CYP3A4-inhibiting agent) or use a shorter course with bleeding vigilance
Fluconazole has no interaction with apixaban as DOACs do not undergo hepatic metabolism
Reduce apixaban dose only if creatinine rises above 2.5 mg/dL — mild renal impairment does not require dose adjustment with concurrent CYP3A4 inhibitors
Switch to warfarin — DOACs are contraindicated with all antifungal agents due to universal hepatotoxicity risk
a: Fluconazole is a potent CYP3A4 AND P-glycoprotein inhibitor — it significantly increases apixaban plasma levels (up to 2-fold) raising bleeding risk substantially; reduce apixaban dose to 2.5 mg BID or temporarily substitute with a different antifungal (topical or non-CYP3A4-inhibiting agent) or use a shorter course with bleeding vigilance
ex: Apixaban and rivaroxaban are metabolized by CYP3A4 and transported by P-glycoprotein. Dual CYP3A4 + P-gp inhibitors (fluconazole, ketoconazole, clarithromycin, ritonavir) significantly increase DOAC plasma concentrations. Fluconazole is one of the most potent CYP3A4 inhibitors in clinical use. Combined with mild CKD (which already slightly impairs DOAC clearance), this interaction substantially increases bleeding risk. Options: (1) Topical antifungal (nystatin) if feasible; (2) Short course fluconazole (single dose) with vigilance; (3) Dose reduction of apixaban to 2.5 mg BID during fluconazole course; (4) Switch to anidulafungin (IV, no CYP interaction). Dabigatran is primarily renally cleared (80%) and P-gp substrate but minimal CYP3A4 — less affected by azoles but MORE affected by renal impairment. These DDIs are particularly dangerous in elderly patients with multiple comorbidities.

q: A 48-year-old woman is found to have an M-protein of 1.8 g/dL (IgG kappa) on serum protein electrophoresis requested for fatigue workup. Bone marrow biopsy shows 6% clonal plasma cells. Serum calcium, creatinine, Hgb, and skeletal survey are all normal. Serum free light chain ratio is 4.2 (mildly abnormal). What is the diagnosis and management?
o: MGUS (Monoclonal Gammopathy of Undetermined Significance) — no treatment required; risk stratification using Mayo MGUS risk model (M-protein size, immunoglobulin isotype, serum FLC ratio); low-risk MGUS followed clinically every 6 months for first year then annually; no indication for chemotherapy or biopsy-guided treatment
Smoldering Multiple Myeloma — requires immediate treatment with lenalidomide or daratumumab-based therapy to prevent progression
Symptomatic Multiple Myeloma — meets CRAB criteria and requires immediate VRd induction followed by autologous HSCT
Waldenström Macroglobulinemia — IgG kappa M-protein with plasma cell infiltration requires rituximab-based therapy
a: MGUS (Monoclonal Gammopathy of Undetermined Significance) — no treatment required; risk stratification using Mayo MGUS risk model (M-protein size, immunoglobulin isotype, serum FLC ratio); low-risk MGUS followed clinically every 6 months for first year then annually; no indication for chemotherapy or biopsy-guided treatment
ex: MGUS is defined by: M-protein <3 g/dL + bone marrow clonal plasma cells <10% + absence of CRAB end-organ damage. This patient has M-protein 1.8 g/dL + 6% plasma cells + no CRAB — classic MGUS. SMM requires ≥10% BMPC OR M-protein ≥3 g/dL without end-organ damage. Symptomatic myeloma requires CRAB or SLiM criteria. MGUS requires no treatment — only monitoring. Mayo Clinic risk model: (1) non-IgG isotype, (2) M-protein >1.5 g/dL, (3) abnormal FLC ratio (<0.26 or >1.65) — 0 risk factors = low risk (5% progression at 20 years); 3 risk factors = high risk (58% at 20 years). This patient has 1 risk factor (mildly abnormal FLC ratio) — low-intermediate risk. Annual monitoring with CBC, creatinine, calcium, M-protein, and FLC ratio is appropriate.

q: A 9-month-old girl of West African descent is brought with fever, extreme pallor, and abdominal distension. Spleen is palpable 8 cm below the costal margin. Hgb has acutely dropped to 4.8 g/dL from a known baseline of 8.5 g/dL with reticulocytosis (15%). She is tachycardic with a capillary refill of 4 seconds. What is the diagnosis and immediate management?
o: Acute splenic sequestration crisis in HbSS disease — immediate IV fluid resuscitation to correct hypovolemia, urgent red blood cell transfusion (5 mL/kg packed RBCs to initial Hgb target of 8–9 g/dL — AVOID overtransfusion as auto-transfusion occurs when spleen releases sequestered cells), and parental education on splenic palpation for secondary prevention
Aplastic crisis from Parvovirus B19 — absence of reticulocytes confirms marrow suppression requiring platelet transfusion and EPO therapy
Malaria-associated severe anemia requiring IV artesunate before blood transfusion to prevent parasitic disease exacerbation
Hypersplenism from portal hypertension — splenectomy is the immediate intervention for acute hypersplenism-related anemia
a: Acute splenic sequestration crisis in HbSS disease — immediate IV fluid resuscitation to correct hypovolemia, urgent red blood cell transfusion (5 mL/kg packed RBCs to initial Hgb target of 8–9 g/dL — AVOID overtransfusion as auto-transfusion occurs when spleen releases sequestered cells), and parental education on splenic palpation for secondary prevention
ex: Splenic sequestration crisis is a life-threatening emergency in young children with HbSS (most common 6 months to 5 years when spleen is still present before auto-infarction). Sickled RBCs obstruct splenic outflow causing massive sequestration of blood volume in the spleen — causing acute hypovolemic shock (tachycardia, prolonged CRT) with rapid Hgb drop ≥2 g/dL from baseline AND splenomegaly with high reticulocyte count. The high reticulocyte count distinguishes this from aplastic crisis (reticulocytopenia). Immediate management: IV fluid bolus (10 mL/kg NS) for shock + small-volume RBC transfusion (5 mL/kg) targeting Hgb 8–9 g/dL — CRITICAL: do NOT transfuse to normal Hgb because when the spleen releases sequestered cells ('auto-transfusion'), the Hgb will overshoot causing hyperviscosity. Splenectomy after age 2 for recurrent episodes.

q: A 70-year-old man presents with Hgb 10.2 g/dL, MCV 88 fL, platelets 98 × 10⁹/L, WBC 2.9 × 10⁹/L. Bone marrow shows 7% blasts, multilineage dysplasia, and del(7) on cytogenetics. IPSS-R score is calculated at 6.5. His ECOG performance status is 2. What treatment approach is most appropriate?
o: High-risk MDS (IPSS-R >3.5) — azacitidine 75 mg/m² SC/IV days 1–7 of a 28-day cycle is the approved first-line therapy for high-risk MDS improving overall survival vs. conventional care; allogeneic HSCT should be evaluated if the patient becomes a candidate after improving performance status
Low-risk MDS — ESA (erythropoietin) therapy is appropriate for this patient with EPO level testing before initiation
Lenalidomide — the standard treatment for all MDS regardless of cytogenetic profile given its broad activity across MDS subtypes
Immediate 7+3 induction chemotherapy — blasts >5% mandate AML-like induction treatment in all MDS patients
a: High-risk MDS (IPSS-R >3.5) — azacitidine 75 mg/m² SC/IV days 1–7 of a 28-day cycle is the approved first-line therapy for high-risk MDS improving overall survival vs. conventional care; allogeneic HSCT should be evaluated if the patient becomes a candidate after improving performance status
ex: IPSS-R score 6.5 places this patient in the 'Very High' risk category (>4.5) — median OS without treatment is <1 year. Del(7) is a very poor prognosis cytogenetic abnormality. Azacitidine (5-azacytidine) is an epigenetic hypomethylating agent (HMA) FDA-approved for high-risk MDS after the AZA-001 trial demonstrated 24.5-month median OS vs. 15 months for conventional care, including superior AML transformation-free survival. Decitabine is an alternative HMA. Oral azacitidine (CC-486) is used for maintenance post-HSCT. For this 70-year-old with ECOG 2 and del(7), allo-HSCT eligibility is questionable — geriatric assessment (CGA), comorbidity scoring (HCT-CI), and performance status improvement with azacitidine are considered. Lenalidomide is specifically indicated for del(5q) MDS — del(7) is an adverse factor. AML induction chemotherapy is generally not indicated for MDS with <10% blasts.

q: A 33-year-old woman presents with 2 weeks of progressive fatigue and jaundice. CBC: Hgb 7.1 g/dL, reticulocytes 18%, WBC 6.2 × 10⁹/L, platelets 195 × 10⁹/L. LDH 1,890 IU/L, indirect bilirubin 4.8 mg/dL, haptoglobin undetectable. DAT is strongly positive (IgG+C3d). ADAMTS13 is normal. She is on no medications. What is the diagnosis, and what is the first-line treatment?
o: Warm Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (wAIHA) — first-line treatment is prednisolone 1–1.5 mg/kg/day (up to 80 mg/day) achieving response in ~70–80% of patients; rituximab is added early for severe disease or as second-line for steroid-refractory/dependent cases; folic acid 5 mg/day supplementation for compensatory erythropoiesis
TTP — plasma exchange is required given LDH elevation and hemolytic anemia despite normal ADAMTS13
G6PD deficiency hemolytic crisis — glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase assay confirms diagnosis; corticosteroids are contraindicated
Paroxysmal Cold Hemoglobinuria — Donath-Landsteiner test should replace DAT in all young women with hemolytic anemia
a: Warm Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (wAIHA) — first-line treatment is prednisolone 1–1.5 mg/kg/day (up to 80 mg/day) achieving response in ~70–80% of patients; rituximab is added early for severe disease or as second-line for steroid-refractory/dependent cases; folic acid 5 mg/day supplementation for compensatory erythropoiesis
ex: Classic wAIHA: positive DAT (IgG + C3d from warm IgG fixing complement), elevated indirect bilirubin and LDH (extravascular hemolysis), undetectable haptoglobin (consumed by hemoglobin-haptoglobin clearance), absent thrombocytopenia/schistocytes (ruling out TTP), normal ADAMTS13, and high reticulocyte count. Unlike TTP, normal platelets and normal ADAMTS13 exclude TTP; unlike G6PD deficiency, DAT is positive. Corticosteroids stabilize the RBC membrane and suppress autoantibody production — 70–80% response rate but frequent relapse on dose reduction. Rituximab (anti-CD20, 375 mg/m² weekly × 4 doses) depletes autoantibody-producing B cells achieving sustained remission in ~75% of steroid-dependent patients. Third-line: splenectomy, mycophenolate mofetil, or fostamatinib. Folic acid 5 mg/day compensates for increased folate consumption from accelerated erythropoiesis.

q: A 45-year-old man presents to ED with acute onset crushing chest pain and dyspnea. He is tachycardic (HR 115), hypotensive (BP 88/60), and has oxygen saturation of 88%. ECG shows right heart strain. CT pulmonary angiogram confirms massive bilateral pulmonary emboli with RV:LV ratio >0.9. His serum troponin is elevated. He has no contraindications to thrombolysis. What is the definitive treatment?
o: Systemic thrombolysis with alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hours — followed by heparin infusion once APTT <80 seconds; surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed thrombolysis reserved for failed systemic thrombolysis; concurrent resuscitation with IV fluids (cautious) and vasopressors (norepinephrine) for hemodynamic support
Immediate anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin infusion as sole therapy — thrombolysis is always contraindicated in elderly patients with PE
Subcutaneous enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg once daily — LMWH is the preferred initial anticoagulation for massive PE over UFH infusion
Inferior vena cava filter placement as the primary intervention for massive PE with hemodynamic compromise
a: Systemic thrombolysis with alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hours — followed by heparin infusion once APTT <80 seconds; surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed thrombolysis reserved for failed systemic thrombolysis; concurrent resuscitation with IV fluids (cautious) and vasopressors (norepinephrine) for hemodynamic support
ex: High-risk (massive) PE is defined by hemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg, HR >100, hypoxemia) — this patient has systolic BP 88, tachycardia, and SpO2 88% with confirmed bilateral massive PE and RV strain. Without reperfusion, mortality approaches 30–60%. Systemic thrombolysis with alteplase is the standard of care for massive PE with no contraindications. Alteplase 100 mg/2h achieves rapid pulmonary artery recanalization, reducing RV afterload and restoring cardiac output. Heparin is withheld during thrombolysis (ICH risk) and restarted without bolus when APTT <80 sec. The PEITHO trial evaluated intermediate-high risk PE — thrombolysis is reserved for TRUE hemodynamic instability. Surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed techniques (EKOS, rheolytic thrombectomy) are reserved for thrombolysis contraindication or failure. IVC filters are only for anticoagulation contraindication, not as primary PE treatment.

q: A 14-year-old girl presents with menorrhagia since menarche, easy bruising, and frequent epistaxis. Her Hgb is 10.8 g/dL (microcytic, MCV 72 fL), platelets 245 × 10⁹/L, PT and APTT are both normal. VWF antigen is 42% (low), VWF activity (VWF:RCo) is 38%, VWF multimer pattern is normal, and FVIII is 48%. What is the diagnosis and first-line treatment for heavy menstrual bleeding?
o: Von Willebrand Disease Type 1 — first-line treatment for heavy menstrual bleeding is DDAVP (desmopressin) intranasally or IV combined with tranexamic acid; oral contraceptives (combined pill) are highly effective for menorrhagia in VWD; VWF/FVIII concentrate is reserved for DDAVP-non-responders or surgical procedures
Von Willebrand Disease Type 3 — complete absence of VWF requiring immediate plasma-derived VWF/FVIII concentrate regardless of bleeding severity
Platelet function defect (Glanzmann Thrombasthenia) — normal VWF and PT/APTT with mucocutaneous bleeding indicates platelet receptor deficiency
Hemophilia A — FVIII 48% in the moderate range causing menorrhagia and bruising; FVIII concentrate is first-line treatment
a: Von Willebrand Disease Type 1 — first-line treatment for heavy menstrual bleeding is DDAVP (desmopressin) intranasally or IV combined with tranexamic acid; oral contraceptives (combined pill) are highly effective for menorrhagia in VWD; VWF/FVIII concentrate is reserved for DDAVP-non-responders or surgical procedures
ex: VWD Type 1 is the most common inherited bleeding disorder: proportional reduction in VWF antigen AND activity with normal multimer pattern — reflects quantitative partial deficiency of structurally normal VWF. Reduced FVIII (48%) reflects decreased VWF carrier function (VWF stabilizes FVIII in plasma). Presentation: mucocutaneous bleeding (epistaxis, easy bruising), menorrhagia (most common presentation in women), postoperative bleeding. Microcytic anemia from chronic iron deficiency from menorrhagia. DDAVP stimulates Weibel-Palade body release of VWF 3–5 fold — effective in ~80% of Type 1 VWD. Tranexamic acid (antifibrinolytic) reduces menstrual blood loss by preventing clot dissolution. Combined oral contraceptives upregulate hepatic VWF production and reduce menstrual flow. VWF/FVIII concentrate for DDAVP failures, Type 2/3 VWD, or major procedures. DDAVP is contraindicated in Type 2B (worsens thrombocytopenia) and Type 3.

q: A 68-year-old woman with RA on long-term methotrexate presents with fatigue and macrocytic anemia (Hgb 9.8 g/dL, MCV 112 fL). Serum B12 is 280 pg/mL (normal) and serum folate is 3.2 ng/mL (low normal). RBC folate is 95 ng/mL (low). Homocysteine is elevated at 28 µmol/L. Bone marrow shows megaloblastic changes with giant metamyelocytes. What is the mechanism and management?
o: Methotrexate inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) — depleting tetrahydrofolate (THF) required for thymidylate and purine synthesis causing functional folate deficiency with megaloblastic anemia despite normal serum folate; treatment is folic acid 5 mg/day (given on non-methotrexate days) — folinic acid (leucovorin) is used for MTX toxicity rescue
Methotrexate directly inhibits B12 absorption in the terminal ileum causing B12 deficiency despite normal serum B12 levels
Folate supplementation is contraindicated in RA — folinic acid (leucovorin) is the only safe folate source in methotrexate-treated patients regardless of dose or indication
Methotrexate causes autoimmune gastritis reducing intrinsic factor production — B12 IM replacement is required
a: Methotrexate inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) — depleting tetrahydrofolate (THF) required for thymidylate and purine synthesis causing functional folate deficiency with megaloblastic anemia despite normal serum folate; treatment is folic acid 5 mg/day (given on non-methotrexate days) — folinic acid (leucovorin) is used for MTX toxicity rescue
ex: Methotrexate competitively inhibits DHFR, blocking conversion of dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate. THF is essential for one-carbon transfer reactions in purine and thymidylate synthesis needed for DNA replication in rapidly dividing cells (especially erythroid precursors). This creates FUNCTIONAL folate deficiency even when serum folate appears normal — RBC folate (reflects tissue stores over 3 months) is a better marker. Elevated homocysteine confirms impaired folate-dependent methionine synthesis. Megaloblastic changes on BM reflect impaired DNA synthesis. Management: folic acid 1–5 mg/day on non-methotrexate days is standard practice to prevent MTX-related mucositis, cytopenias, and liver toxicity WITHOUT compromising MTX therapeutic efficacy (folic acid does not circumvent MTX anti-inflammatory mechanism). Folinic acid (leucovorin) bypasses DHFR and is used for MTX overdose/toxicity rescue.

q: A 22-year-old man from Southeast Asia presents with Hgb 8.2 g/dL, MCV 62 fL, elevated reticulocytes (8%), splenomegaly, and a hemoglobin electrophoresis showing: HbH 30%, HbBarts 5%, HbA absent, and elevated HbA2 2.1% (normal). What is the diagnosis?
o: Hemoglobin H (HbH) disease — deletion of 3 out of 4 alpha-globin genes (--/-α genotype most commonly in Southeast Asians); HbH (β4 tetramers) and residual HbBarts (γ4 from slow HbF-to-HbA switch) form due to massive alpha chain deficiency; management: folic acid supplementation, avoid oxidant drugs and infections, monitor for hydrops in future pregnancies
Beta-thalassemia major — absent HbA with severe microcytic anemia requiring regular transfusion program
HbE/beta-thalassemia — compound heterozygosity causing moderate-severe thalassemia syndrome in Southeast Asian populations
Alpha-thalassemia trait (carrier) — deletion of 2 alpha genes causing mild microcytic anemia without HbH bands on electrophoresis
a: Hemoglobin H (HbH) disease — deletion of 3 out of 4 alpha-globin genes (--/-α genotype most commonly in Southeast Asians); HbH (β4 tetramers) and residual HbBarts (γ4 from slow HbF-to-HbA switch) form due to massive alpha chain deficiency; management: folic acid supplementation, avoid oxidant drugs and infections, monitor for hydrops in future pregnancies
ex: HbH disease results from deletion/dysfunction of 3 of 4 alpha-globin genes. Severely reduced alpha chain production causes massive excess of beta chains (and gamma chains in infants) which form unstable tetramers: HbH (β4) and HbBarts (γ4). Both have extremely high O2 affinity and are useless for tissue O2 delivery. HbH is also unstable and precipitates as Heinz body-like inclusions causing chronic hemolytic anemia. HbA is absent or severely reduced (no alpha chains available). Characteristic: moderate chronic hemolytic anemia (Hgb 7–10 g/dL), splenomegaly, microcytosis. Most patients do NOT require regular transfusions (unlike beta-thal major) — management is supportive. Key complications: hemolytic crises with infections/oxidants, splenomegaly, and risk of Hb Barts hydrops fetalis (all 4 deletions) in offspring if partner is also a carrier. Genetic counseling and partner testing are essential.

q: An 80-year-old male is found incidentally to have a lymphocyte count of 22 × 10⁹/L. He is asymptomatic with no lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, or organomegaly. Hgb is 13.8 g/dL, platelets 189 × 10⁹/L. Flow cytometry confirms CD5+, CD19+, CD23+, CD20dim, CD38−, ZAP-70−, and IGHV mutated status. His Rai stage is 0 and Binet stage A. What is the appropriate management?
o: Active surveillance (watchful waiting) — Rai stage 0/Binet A CLL with favorable biology (IGHV mutated, CD38−, ZAP-70−) has excellent prognosis with median survival >10 years; treatment is only initiated for Rai stage III–IV, symptomatic disease, or meeting iwCLL treatment criteria (doubling time <6 months, constitutional symptoms, cytopenias)
Immediate FCR (fludarabine-cyclophosphamide-rituximab) chemoimmunotherapy — early treatment prevents progression to symptomatic CLL
Ibrutinib monotherapy — BTK inhibitors should be started immediately in all newly diagnosed CLL regardless of stage to prevent Richter transformation
Obinutuzumab-chlorambucil — the standard first-line treatment for all CLL patients regardless of stage or biology
a: Active surveillance (watchful waiting) — Rai stage 0/Binet A CLL with favorable biology (IGHV mutated, CD38−, ZAP-70−) has excellent prognosis with median survival >10 years; treatment is only initiated for Rai stage III–IV, symptomatic disease, or meeting iwCLL treatment criteria (doubling time <6 months, constitutional symptoms, cytopenias)
ex: Early-stage asymptomatic CLL with favorable biomarkers does not benefit from early treatment — multiple randomized trials (CLL01, CLL06) demonstrated no survival advantage to early chemotherapy in Binet A CLL. iwCLL 2018 treatment criteria specify initiation for: progressive marrow failure (Hgb <10 g/dL or platelets <100 × 10⁹/L), symptomatic massive splenomegaly (>6 cm below costal margin), massive lymphadenopathy (>10 cm), progressive lymphocytosis doubling in <6 months, autoimmune cytopenias refractory to steroids, or constitutional B symptoms. Favorable prognostic markers in this patient: IGHV mutated (best predictor — median time to treatment >10 years), CD38 negative, ZAP-70 negative, Rai 0/Binet A. No del(17p) or TP53 mutation mentioned. Annual CBC and clinical review is appropriate.

q: A 55-year-old man presents with bone pain, pathological fracture of L2, Hgb 9.1 g/dL, calcium 11.8 mg/dL, creatinine 2.9 mg/dL, and M-protein 5.2 g/dL (IgG lambda). Bone marrow shows 45% clonal plasma cells. He is fit (ECOG 1) and has no significant cardiac comorbidity. What is the optimal treatment strategy?
o: VRd induction (bortezomib-lenalidomide-dexamethasone) × 4 cycles followed by autologous HSCT after stem cell harvest, then VRd or lenalidomide maintenance — FORTE and GRIFFIN trials support VRd + auto-HSCT as standard first-line for transplant-eligible myeloma; daratumumab-VRd (Dara-VRd) is emerging as enhanced induction
VMP (bortezomib-melphalan-prednisolone) — the regimen of choice for all myeloma patients regardless of transplant eligibility
Melphalan-prednisolone alone — traditional treatment for all myeloma since the 1960s with proven durability over novel agents
Lenalidomide monotherapy maintenance without induction — anti-myeloma activity is sufficient to achieve remission without combination chemotherapy or HSCT
a: VRd induction (bortezomib-lenalidomide-dexamethasone) × 4 cycles followed by autologous HSCT after stem cell harvest, then VRd or lenalidomide maintenance — FORTE and GRIFFIN trials support VRd + auto-HSCT as standard first-line for transplant-eligible myeloma; daratumumab-VRd (Dara-VRd) is emerging as enhanced induction
ex: This fit 55-year-old with newly diagnosed symptomatic MM (CRAB criteria: hypercalcemia, renal, anemia, bone disease) and 45% BMPC is transplant-eligible. The IFM 2009 and EMN02 trials established that VRd + auto-HSCT achieves superior PFS compared to VRd alone. Standard algorithm: (1) VRd induction ×4–6 cycles; (2) PBSC mobilization with G-CSF ± plerixafor; (3) High-dose melphalan 200 mg/m² conditioning; (4) Auto-HSCT; (5) Lenalidomide maintenance (CALGB 100104/IFM 2005-02 — improves PFS and OS). Daratumumab-VRd (Dara-VRd) is now preferred at many centers based on GRIFFIN trial showing superior MRD negativity rates. Tandem HSCT for high-risk cytogenetics (del17p, t(4;14), t(14;16)). Zoledronic acid for bone disease. Rehabilitation and vertebroplasty for L2 fracture.

q: A 5-year-old child is referred with a 2-week history of pallor, petechiae, and fatigue. CBC: WBC 78 × 10⁹/L, Hgb 6.8 g/dL, platelets 18 × 10⁹/L. Peripheral smear shows 85% blasts with lymphoblast morphology. Bone marrow confirms B-ALL with 92% blasts. FISH shows ETV6-RUNX1 (TEL-AML1) fusion. MRD at end of induction is <0.01%. What is the prognostic significance and expected outcome?
o: ETV6-RUNX1 (TEL-AML1) is the most favorable cytogenetic finding in pediatric B-ALL — present in ~25% of childhood B-ALL; combined with MRD negativity (<0.01% = MRD-negative) at end of induction, this child is in the lowest risk group with predicted overall survival >95% with standard risk-stratified chemotherapy without HSCT
ETV6-RUNX1 carries intermediate prognosis similar to Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL — allogeneic HSCT is recommended in first remission
MRD <0.01% at end of induction is an adverse marker indicating occult blast persistence requiring therapy intensification
ETV6-RUNX1 is associated with late relapses (>5 years) occurring in >50% of patients necessitating indefinite surveillance beyond standard 2-year follow-up
a: ETV6-RUNX1 (TEL-AML1) is the most favorable cytogenetic finding in pediatric B-ALL — present in ~25% of childhood B-ALL; combined with MRD negativity (<0.01% = MRD-negative) at end of induction, this child is in the lowest risk group with predicted overall survival >95% with standard risk-stratified chemotherapy without HSCT
ex: ETV6-RUNX1 fusion results from t(12;21)(p13;q22) cryptic translocation (not visible on conventional karyotype — FISH required). It confers the most favorable prognosis in childhood B-ALL: ~95% event-free survival with contemporary therapy. The ETV6-RUNX1 protein acts as a dominant-negative transcriptional repressor disrupting CBF-mediated differentiation in a different, more favorable way than other AML-associated CBF translocations. MRD negativity (<0.01% by flow cytometry or PCR) at end of induction is the most powerful prognostic marker in pediatric ALL — indicating excellent chemotherapy sensitivity. MRD-negative patients may qualify for treatment de-escalation (e.g., protocol AALL0434 de-escalation arms). Late relapses CAN occur in ETV6-RUNX1 ALL (even 5+ years) but at low rates — surveillance is continued per protocol. HSCT is NOT indicated in MRD-negative standard-risk ALL.

q: A 64-year-old male smoker with COPD presents with Hgb 19.8 g/dL, hematocrit 61%, MCV 72 fL (microcytic). Serum erythropoietin (EPO) level is markedly elevated at 68 mIU/mL (normal 4–26). Arterial blood gas shows PaO2 58 mmHg. Bone marrow shows erythroid hyperplasia. JAK2 V617F is negative. What is the diagnosis and management?
o: Secondary polycythemia from chronic hypoxia (COPD) — the elevated EPO is the physiological response to hypoxemia driving compensatory erythrocytosis; management is optimization of COPD (bronchodilators, O2 therapy targeting SpO2 >90%) and phlebotomy only for severe hyperviscosity symptoms (hematocrit >55–60%)
Polycythemia Vera — JAK2 negativity does not exclude PV as 2% of cases are JAK2-exon-12 positive; bone marrow biopsy and EPO level confirm diagnosis
Testosterone-secreting adrenal adenoma causing androgen-driven erythrocytosis — adrenal CT scan is the priority investigation
Renal cell carcinoma with EPO-secreting paraneoplastic syndrome — CT scan abdomen is the urgent investigation
a: Secondary polycythemia from chronic hypoxia (COPD) — the elevated EPO is the physiological response to hypoxemia driving compensatory erythrocytosis; management is optimization of COPD (bronchodilators, O2 therapy targeting SpO2 >90%) and phlebotomy only for severe hyperviscosity symptoms (hematocrit >55–60%)
ex: This distinguishes PRIMARY (PV) from SECONDARY polycythemia. Key differentiating features: (1) Elevated EPO — in PV, EPO is SUPPRESSED (clonal erythropoiesis is EPO-independent); secondary polycythemia has ELEVATED EPO (appropriate response to hypoxia or inappropriate from tumor); (2) JAK2 V617F negative — effectively excludes PV in most cases; (3) PaO2 58 mmHg — significant hypoxemia driving EPO production; (4) Microcytic RBCs — iron deficiency from erythroid demand depletion (also distinguishes from PV where RBCs are macronormocytic). COPD-related hypoxic secondary polycythemia: treat the underlying cause (LTOT when PaO2 <55 mmHg), stop smoking. Phlebotomy to hematocrit <55% reserved for symptomatic hyperviscosity. Unlike PV, low-dose aspirin and cytoreductive therapy are NOT routinely indicated. Exclude other causes of elevated EPO: renal cysts, RCC, hepatocellular carcinoma, cerebellar hemangioblastoma.

q: A 30-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation is found to have platelets of 88 × 10⁹/L at routine antenatal blood test. Her BP is 148/94 mmHg and she has 2+ proteinuria on dipstick. LDH is 580 IU/L, AST 82 IU/L, and peripheral smear shows schistocytes. What is the diagnosis and management priority?
o: HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) complicating pre-eclampsia — immediate hospitalization, IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis, antihypertensives (labetalol/hydralazine) for BP control, corticosteroids for fetal lung maturation, and urgent delivery planning (definitive treatment) — neonatology involvement for preterm neonate at 34 weeks
TTP complicating pregnancy — immediate plasma exchange is required before any delivery planning
Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) of pregnancy — reassure the patient that platelet counts of 88 × 10⁹/L are safe in pregnancy; no intervention required until delivery
DIC from placental abruption — urgent FFP, cryoprecipitate, and platelet transfusion before ADAMTS13 testing
a: HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) complicating pre-eclampsia — immediate hospitalization, IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis, antihypertensives (labetalol/hydralazine) for BP control, corticosteroids for fetal lung maturation, and urgent delivery planning (definitive treatment) — neonatology involvement for preterm neonate at 34 weeks
ex: HELLP syndrome is a severe variant of pre-eclampsia requiring its own diagnostic and management framework. Tennessee classification: Class 1 (platelets <50 × 10⁹/L), Class 2 (50–100 × 10⁹/L), Class 3 (100–150 × 10⁹/L). This patient has Class 2 HELLP with hypertension + proteinuria = severe pre-eclampsia. MAHA (schistocytes) differentiates from ITP (no hemolysis). ADAMTS13 is NORMAL in HELLP (distinguishes from TTP). The ONLY curative treatment is delivery — at 34 weeks, benefits of delivery outweigh prematurity risks. Maternal indications for immediate delivery: severe hypertension, eclampsia, placental abruption, DIC, maternal distress. Corticosteroids (betamethasone) accelerate fetal lung maturity. Magnesium sulfate prevents eclamptic seizures. Platelet threshold for cesarean: >50 × 10⁹/L for regional anesthesia typically >80 × 10⁹/L is preferred.

q: A 50-year-old man presents with gradual onset fever, hepatosplenomegaly, and pancytopenia (Hgb 8.1 g/dL, WBC 1.8 × 10⁹/L, platelets 42 × 10⁹/L). He has a history of Crohn's disease on adalimumab. Bone marrow trephine shows hemophagocytosis with activated macrophages engulfing RBCs, platelets, and leukocytes. Ferritin is 42,000 ng/mL. Soluble CD25 (sIL-2R) is markedly elevated. What is the diagnosis and treatment?
o: Secondary Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) — likely triggered by EBV reactivation from adalimumab-induced immunosuppression; treatment per HLH-2004 protocol: dexamethasone + etoposide ± cyclosporine; urgent investigation for underlying trigger (EBV, CMV, malignancy); adalimumab must be discontinued; HSCT for refractory cases
Visceral leishmaniasis (Kala-azar) — the combination of hepatosplenomegaly and pancytopenia in an immunosuppressed patient requires liposomal amphotericin B treatment
Felty's syndrome — the combination of RA (similar to Crohn's) and splenomegaly with pancytopenia requires splenectomy
Acute leukemia with hemophagocytic features — bone marrow transplant is indicated before etoposide therapy
a: Secondary Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) — likely triggered by EBV reactivation from adalimumab-induced immunosuppression; treatment per HLH-2004 protocol: dexamethasone + etoposide ± cyclosporine; urgent investigation for underlying trigger (EBV, CMV, malignancy); adalimumab must be discontinued; HSCT for refractory cases
ex: HLH is a life-threatening hyperinflammatory syndrome caused by dysregulated macrophage and T-cell activation. HScore or HLH-2004 diagnostic criteria: fever, splenomegaly, cytopenias (≥2 lineages), hypertriglyceridemia or hypofibrinogenemia, hemophagocytosis on marrow biopsy, low/absent NK cell activity, ferritin ≥500 ng/mL (typically >10,000 in HLH), and elevated sCD25 (sIL-2R). Secondary HLH triggers in adults: infections (EBV most common — especially in immunosuppressed), malignancy (T-cell lymphoma most dangerous), and autoimmune disease. Adalimumab (anti-TNF) causes profound immunosuppression predisposing to EBV/CMV reactivation triggering HLH. Treatment: stop immunosuppressant, HLH-2004 protocol (dexamethasone 10 mg/m² + etoposide 150 mg/m²), emapalumab (anti-IFN-γ) for refractory primary HLH, HSCT for primary/refractory HLH. Ferritin >10,000 ng/mL has ~90% sensitivity for HLH.

q: A 17-year-old male presents with 3 weeks of fatigue, cervical lymphadenopathy, and a mediastinal mass on chest X-ray measuring 12 cm (massive mediastinal involvement, MTR >0.35). Biopsy shows nodular sclerosis Hodgkin Lymphoma. PET-CT staging: Stage IIA bulky (mediastinal mass). What is the standard first-line treatment approach?
o: ABVD chemotherapy (adriamycin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) × 4–6 cycles with response-adapted consolidation radiotherapy to the mediastinum; interim PET after 2 cycles guides escalation (Deauville ≥3) or de-escalation (Deauville 1–2 — RATHL trial avoidance of bleomycin ± radiotherapy omission)
BEACOPP escalated (bleomycin, etoposide, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, vincristine, procarbazine, prednisolone) × 8 cycles — mandatory for all bulky mediastinal Hodgkin Lymphoma regardless of age
Radiotherapy alone (involved field, 30 Gy) — sufficient for all early-stage Hodgkin Lymphoma with mediastinal involvement
Anti-CD30 brentuximab vedotin monotherapy — targeted therapy replaces cytotoxic chemotherapy in all pediatric HL patients to avoid late effects
a: ABVD chemotherapy (adriamycin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) × 4–6 cycles with response-adapted consolidation radiotherapy to the mediastinum; interim PET after 2 cycles guides escalation (Deauville ≥3) or de-escalation (Deauville 1–2 — RATHL trial avoidance of bleomycin ± radiotherapy omission)
ex: Stage IIA bulky HL (mediastinal mass >1/3 thoracic diameter = 'massive mediastinal HL') requires combined modality treatment. ABVD remains the global standard for newly diagnosed HL — adriamycin (anthracycline) + bleomycin (pulmonary toxicity risk) + vinblastine + dacarbazine. Response-adapted PET-CT: (1) RAPID trial and RATHL trial support PET-guided de-escalation — PET-negative after 2 ABVD cycles → omit bleomycin (AVD) reducing pulmonary toxicity; (2) PET-positive → escalate to BEACOPPesc or add brentuximab vedotin (A+AVD from ECHELON-1 trial). Bulky mediastinal disease typically requires consolidation IFRT (20–30 Gy) to residual PET-positive sites. Long-term late effects in young patients: bleomycin pulmonary fibrosis, anthracycline cardiomyopathy, secondary malignancies from radiotherapy — pediatric protocols minimize these aggressively.

q: A 40-year-old woman is referred after two unexplained second-trimester miscarriages and one episode of DVT. Her APTT is persistently prolonged at 52 seconds (not correcting on mixing study). Anticardiolipin IgG is elevated at 68 GPL units on two occasions 14 weeks apart. Lupus anticoagulant is positive on both occasions. Anti-β2GPI IgG is also positive. What is the diagnosis and long-term management?
o: Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS) — triple-positive aPL with obstetric and thrombotic events; long-term warfarin (target INR 2–3) for thrombotic APS; hydroxychloroquine for primary prophylaxis; low-dose aspirin + LMWH (therapeutic dose) throughout future pregnancies to prevent recurrent obstetric APS; DOACs are AVOIDED in APS due to inferior outcomes (TRAPS trial — rivaroxaban inferior to warfarin)
Hereditary thrombophilia — Factor V Leiden testing is more important than aPL antibodies in recurrent miscarriage
Lupus anticoagulant from SLE requiring hydroxychloroquine monotherapy without anticoagulation
APTT prolongation from Factor VIII inhibitor — Bethesda assay is required before diagnosing APS
a: Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS) — triple-positive aPL with obstetric and thrombotic events; long-term warfarin (target INR 2–3) for thrombotic APS; hydroxychloroquine for primary prophylaxis; low-dose aspirin + LMWH (therapeutic dose) throughout future pregnancies to prevent recurrent obstetric APS; DOACs are AVOIDED in APS due to inferior outcomes (TRAPS trial — rivaroxaban inferior to warfarin)
ex: APS classification requires ≥1 clinical criterion (vascular thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity) + ≥1 laboratory criterion persistent ≥12 weeks: LA, aCL IgG/IgM >40 GPL/MPL, or anti-β2GPI IgG/IgM >99th percentile. This patient is TRIPLE POSITIVE (LA + aCL + anti-β2GPI) — the highest thrombotic risk category. Two second-trimester losses + DVT confirm both obstetric and thrombotic APS. Critical management points: (1) DOACs are INFERIOR to warfarin in APS — TRAPS trial and multiple observational studies show higher thrombotic recurrence with rivaroxaban, especially in triple-positive patients; warfarin INR 2–3 is standard; (2) Future pregnancies: aspirin 75 mg + therapeutic-dose LMWH throughout gestation and 6 weeks postpartum — reduces recurrent pregnancy loss by ~75%; (3) Hydroxychloroquine for all APS patients; (4) Evaluate for underlying SLE (ANA, complement).

q: A 58-year-old man is admitted with septic shock from E. coli bacteremia following urological surgery. CBC on admission: Hgb 11.2 g/dL, WBC 28 × 10⁹/L, platelets 52 × 10⁹/L (from baseline 220 × 10⁹/L). Coagulation: PT 19 seconds (INR 1.8), APTT 48 seconds, fibrinogen 0.9 g/L, D-dimer >20 µg/mL (markedly elevated). Peripheral smear shows schistocytes. What is the diagnosis and treatment hierarchy?
o: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) from sepsis — treatment hierarchy: (1) treat the underlying cause (IV antibiotics, source control); (2) transfusion support: platelet transfusion if <50 × 10⁹/L with active bleeding, FFP 15–20 mL/kg for prolonged PT/APTT with bleeding, cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <1.5 g/L; (3) consider rFVIIa or tranexamic acid only in refractory life-threatening hemorrhage
TTP from sepsis — plasma exchange is the priority before antibiotic therapy given consumptive thrombocytopenia and schistocytes
HIT from perioperative heparin — anti-PF4 antibody testing and immediate heparin cessation with argatroban
Liver failure from sepsis-induced hepatic dysfunction — fresh frozen plasma replacement for isolated coagulation factor deficiency
a: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) from sepsis — treatment hierarchy: (1) treat the underlying cause (IV antibiotics, source control); (2) transfusion support: platelet transfusion if <50 × 10⁹/L with active bleeding, FFP 15–20 mL/kg for prolonged PT/APTT with bleeding, cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <1.5 g/L; (3) consider rFVIIa or tranexamic acid only in refractory life-threatening hemorrhage
ex: Sepsis is the most common cause of DIC. ISTH DIC score: platelet count ≤50 × 10⁹/L (2 points) + PT >6s prolonged (2 points) + fibrinogen <1 g/L (1 point) + elevated D-dimer/FDP (3 points) = overt DIC (score ≥5). This patient scores >5. Pathophysiology: overwhelming TF expression from endotoxin triggers massive thrombin generation → consuming all coagulation factors and platelets → simultaneous thrombosis (microvascular) and hemorrhage (factor depletion). Treatment principle: TREAT THE CAUSE — all other interventions are supportive. Cryoprecipitate is the most concentrated fibrinogen source (1 pool raises fibrinogen by ~0.5 g/L). Heparin for DIC is controversial — considered only for thrombosis-dominant DIC (purpura fulminans, acral ischemia). Antifibrinolytics (TXA) are CONTRAINDICATED in DIC unless fibrinolysis-dominant confirmed by TEG. TTP has normal PT/APTT and markedly reduced ADAMTS13.

q: A 75-year-old woman with newly diagnosed diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), ECOG performance status 3, and ejection fraction of 35% is referred for treatment. Standard R-CHOP is not feasible given severe cardiac dysfunction. What is the most appropriate treatment modification?
o: R-CDOP (cyclophosphamide-liposomal doxorubicin-vincristine-prednisolone + rituximab) — liposomal doxorubicin (Caelyx) has significantly reduced cardiotoxicity compared to conventional doxorubicin while maintaining anti-lymphoma activity in cardiac-compromised patients; alternatively, R-CVP (omitting anthracycline) or R-GEMOX for very frail patients
Full-dose R-CHOP must be administered in all DLBCL patients — cardiac status is a relative contraindication that should not modify chemotherapy intensity
Palliative corticosteroids (dexamethasone) alone — all DLBCL in patients over 70 years with cardiac disease should receive palliative intent treatment without cytotoxic agents
Lenalidomide monotherapy — single-agent non-anthracycline treatment achieves equivalent outcomes to R-CHOP in cardiac-compromised elderly DLBCL
a: R-CDOP (cyclophosphamide-liposomal doxorubicin-vincristine-prednisolone + rituximab) — liposomal doxorubicin (Caelyx) has significantly reduced cardiotoxicity compared to conventional doxorubicin while maintaining anti-lymphoma activity in cardiac-compromised patients; alternatively, R-CVP (omitting anthracycline) or R-GEMOX for very frail patients
ex: Anthracycline (doxorubicin) cardiotoxicity is the primary dose-limiting concern in elderly DLBCL. Conventional doxorubicin causes cumulative cardiomyopathy through free radical generation — particularly dangerous in patients with pre-existing LV dysfunction (EF 35%). Liposomal doxorubicin (Caelyx/Doxil) is encapsulated in PEGylated liposomes: (1) reduced peak plasma concentration reducing myocardial exposure; (2) preferential accumulation in tumor tissue; (3) equivalent anti-lymphoma activity at equivalent doses; (4) significantly reduced cardiotoxicity (CAEBL-2 trial). Other options for EF<45%: R-CVP (omit anthracycline — less effective but safer); R-GemOx (gemcitabine-oxaliplatin-rituximab); R-bendamustine. For ECOG 3 patients, R-miniCHOP (50% dose reduction of all cytotoxic agents) is validated in the phase II GELA trial. Cardiac optimization (carvedilol, ACE inhibitors) before treatment is also essential.

q: A 32-year-old woman is 28 weeks pregnant and is found to have a platelet count of 82 × 10⁹/L. She has no symptoms, no hypertension, no proteinuria, LDH is normal, and liver function tests are normal. Her platelet count was normal in the first trimester. What is the most likely diagnosis and management?
o: Gestational thrombocytopenia (GT) — the most common cause of thrombocytopenia in pregnancy (~75% of cases); typically mild (platelets 70–150 × 10⁹/L), develops in late second/third trimester, no maternal or neonatal complications, and resolves spontaneously postpartum; no treatment required; monitor platelets monthly; no need for epidural threshold modification at this platelet count
ITP — requires immediate corticosteroid treatment to prevent neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia
Pre-eclampsia with thrombocytopenia — elevated blood pressure and proteinuria are always present by 28 weeks in pre-eclamptic thrombocytopenia
NAIT — fetal thrombocytopenia from maternal anti-HPA-1a antibodies causing isolated maternal thrombocytopenia without neonatal concern
a: Gestational thrombocytopenia (GT) — the most common cause of thrombocytopenia in pregnancy (~75% of cases); typically mild (platelets 70–150 × 10⁹/L), develops in late second/third trimester, no maternal or neonatal complications, and resolves spontaneously postpartum; no treatment required; monitor platelets monthly; no need for epidural threshold modification at this platelet count
ex: GT is the most common cause of pregnancy-associated thrombocytopenia, accounting for ~75% of cases. It is a diagnosis of exclusion characterized by: mild thrombocytopenia (rarely <70 × 10⁹/L), no pre-pregnancy thrombocytopenia history, third trimester onset, absence of hypertension/proteinuria/elevated LDH/liver enzymes (distinguishing from pre-eclampsia/HELLP), and spontaneous resolution within 1–2 months postpartum. Importantly, GT causes no neonatal thrombocytopenia (distinguishing from ITP where maternal IgG can cross placenta) and no maternal complications. Differential diagnosis algorithm: pre-eclampsia/HELLP (hypertension + elevated LFTs/LDH), ITP (antecedent thrombocytopenia, antiplatelet antibodies, neonatal risk), TTP (MAHA + schistocytes + neurological), AFLP. Epidural anesthesia threshold: platelets >80 × 10⁹/L is generally acceptable — this patient at 82 × 10⁹/L is borderline but manageable.

q: An 8-year-old boy presents with a 6-month history of recurrent pneumonias, oral ulcers, and perianal abscesses. Absolute neutrophil count (ANC) on three separate occasions: 0.2, 0.3, and 0.1 × 10⁹/L. Bone marrow shows maturation arrest at the promyelocyte stage. ELANE gene sequencing reveals a novel heterozygous missense mutation. His father has a similar history of infections. What is the diagnosis and treatment?
o: Severe Congenital Neutropenia (SCN) from ELANE mutation — G-CSF (filgrastim) 5–10 µg/kg/day SC to maintain ANC >1.0 × 10⁹/L; antibiotic prophylaxis; annual bone marrow biopsy and cytogenetics for MDS/AML surveillance (15–20% cumulative risk at 10 years); allogeneic HSCT for G-CSF non-responders or MDS/AML transformation
Cyclic Neutropenia from ELANE mutation — 21-day neutrophil cycling pattern is characteristic and G-CSF is not first-line therapy
Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID) — neutropenia is secondary to recurrent infections depleting neutrophil reserves; IVIG is the primary treatment
Autoimmune Neutropenia of Childhood — resolves spontaneously by age 4 without treatment in the majority of cases
a: Severe Congenital Neutropenia (SCN) from ELANE mutation — G-CSF (filgrastim) 5–10 µg/kg/day SC to maintain ANC >1.0 × 10⁹/L; antibiotic prophylaxis; annual bone marrow biopsy and cytogenetics for MDS/AML surveillance (15–20% cumulative risk at 10 years); allogeneic HSCT for G-CSF non-responders or MDS/AML transformation
ex: SCN (Kostmann syndrome) is defined by ANC <0.5 × 10⁹/L from birth causing severe recurrent bacterial infections. ELANE mutations (encoding neutrophil elastase) account for ~60% of autosomal dominant SCN — misfolded NE protein triggers unfolded protein response causing promyelocyte apoptosis (maturation arrest at promyelocyte-myelocyte stage on marrow biopsy). The familial pattern (affected father) confirms autosomal dominant inheritance. SCNIR (Severe Chronic Neutropenia International Registry) data: G-CSF >90% response rate (ANC target ≥1.0 × 10⁹/L); long-term G-CSF required indefinitely. Critical concern: ~15–20% cumulative MDS/AML risk at 10 years — driven by acquired CSF3R (G-CSF receptor) truncation mutations. Annual marrow surveillance mandatory. High G-CSF doses (>8 µg/kg/day) and acquired RUNX1 mutations are additional AML risk factors — HSCT should be considered when CSF3R mutations are detected.

q: A 66-year-old woman presents with fatigue, early satiety, and 8 kg weight loss over 3 months. Examination reveals a 14 cm spleen. CBC: Hgb 9.8 g/dL, WBC 24 × 10⁹/L (predominantly mature lymphocytes), platelets 98 × 10⁹/L. Peripheral blood flow cytometry: CD19+, CD20bright, CD22+, CD11c+, CD103+, CD25+, and TRAP positive. Bone marrow biopsy is an aspirate dry tap; trephine shows diffuse infiltration with mononuclear cells with 'fried egg' cytoplasm and characteristic bone marrow fibrosis. BRAF V600E mutation is confirmed. What is the treatment?
o: Hairy Cell Leukemia (HCL) — first-line treatment is cladribine (2-CdA) 0.1 mg/kg/day IV continuous infusion × 7 days OR 0.14 mg/kg/day IV over 2 hours × 5 days — achieving complete remission in >85–90% of patients with durable responses lasting >10 years; rituximab may be added for incomplete responses
CLL with atypical immunophenotype — FCR chemoimmunotherapy is the standard first-line treatment
Splenic Marginal Zone Lymphoma (SMZL) — rituximab monotherapy or splenectomy is the preferred initial approach
Prolymphocytic leukemia — alemtuzumab (anti-CD52) is the appropriate treatment for this aggressive mature B-cell disorder
a: Hairy Cell Leukemia (HCL) — first-line treatment is cladribine (2-CdA) 0.1 mg/kg/day IV continuous infusion × 7 days OR 0.14 mg/kg/day IV over 2 hours × 5 days — achieving complete remission in >85–90% of patients with durable responses lasting >10 years; rituximab may be added for incomplete responses
ex: HCL is a unique indolent B-cell LPD with highly characteristic features: (1) Immunophenotype: CD11c+, CD103+, CD25+, CD123+, annexin A1+ — this 'pentad' (especially CD103+CD25+CD11c+) is pathognomonic; (2) TRAP (tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase) positivity — resistant to L-tartrate; (3) Dry tap from hairy cell-induced marrow fibrosis; (4) 'Fried egg' appearance on trephine; (5) BRAF V600E mutation in >95% of classic HCL (absent in HCL variant and SMZL). Cladribine (2-chlorodeoxyadenosine) is a purine analog causing DNA damage in quiescent lymphoid cells — >90% complete remission, 10+ year DFS. Pentostatin (DCF) is an alternative purine analog. Relapsed/refractory HCL: vemurafenib (BRAF inhibitor) ± rituximab for BRAF V600E-positive relapsed disease — moxetumomab pasudotox (anti-CD22 immunotoxin) for multi-relapsed cases.

q: A 50-year-old man presents with acute onset severe headache ('thunderclap'), neck stiffness, and photophobia. LP shows xanthochromic CSF. CT head is normal. He is on warfarin (INR 4.8) for prosthetic heart valve. His hemostasis must be urgently reversed. What is the most appropriate reversal strategy?
o: 4-factor Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (4F-PCC, Beriplex/Octaplex) 25–50 IU/kg IV — immediately reverses warfarin by replacing Factors II, VII, IX, and X; vitamin K 10 mg IV is administered concurrently for sustained reversal; 4F-PCC works within minutes unlike FFP (hours) and avoids volume overload; bridge back to warfarin/LMWH post-neurosurgical assessment
FFP 15–20 mL/kg — the standard warfarin reversal agent in all emergency settings including intracranial hemorrhage
Stop warfarin and observe — spontaneous INR correction will occur within 24–48 hours without need for active reversal in subarachnoid hemorrhage
Idarucizumab IV — the appropriate reversal agent for supratherapeutic anticoagulation from warfarin causing CNS hemorrhage
a: 4-factor Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (4F-PCC, Beriplex/Octaplex) 25–50 IU/kg IV — immediately reverses warfarin by replacing Factors II, VII, IX, and X; vitamin K 10 mg IV is administered concurrently for sustained reversal; 4F-PCC works within minutes unlike FFP (hours) and avoids volume overload; bridge back to warfarin/LMWH post-neurosurgical assessment
ex: Supratherapeutic warfarin (INR 4.8) with acute intracranial hemorrhage is a true hematological emergency requiring immediate reversal. 4F-PCC advantages over FFP: (1) Immediate reversal (<15 minutes) vs. FFP (1–2 hours for thawing + infusion); (2) Small concentrated volume (20–40 mL vs. 1000+ mL FFP) — critical in patients prone to volume overload or with prosthetic valve requiring careful fluid management; (3) Contains all four vitamin K-dependent procoagulant factors plus Protein C and S; (4) No ABO typing or thawing required. IV vitamin K (10 mg) sustains reversal over 6–12 hours (time for new factor synthesis). FFP alone is inadequate for urgent warfarin reversal in life-threatening hemorrhage. Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran (NOT warfarin). Recombinant VIIa is an option for refractory hemorrhage but lacks Factors IX and X replacement. Post-event: mechanical valve patients typically require LMWH bridging after 48–72 hours if hemostasis confirmed.

q: A 29-year-old male with known Fanconi Anemia (FANCA genotype) presents with progressive pancytopenia: Hgb 7.8 g/dL, WBC 1.9 × 10⁹/L, platelets 38 × 10⁹/L. Bone marrow biopsy shows severe hypocellularity (5% cellularity). He has a matched sibling donor (MSD). Which HSCT conditioning regimen is critical to use, and why does it differ from standard aplastic anemia conditioning?
o: Low-dose/modified conditioning regimen (Fludarabine 30 mg/m²/day × 5 days + low-dose cyclophosphamide 10–15 mg/kg/day × 4 days ± low-dose radiation 300–450 cGy TBI) — standard-dose alkylating agents (cyclophosphamide 200 mg/kg) and radiation cause fatal regimen-related toxicity in FA patients due to their defective DNA interstrand crosslink repair pathway
Standard myeloablative conditioning (cyclophosphamide 200 mg/kg over 4 days) — identical to non-FA severe aplastic anemia conditioning protocol
Reduced-intensity conditioning with fludarabine + busulfan — standard AML conditioning modified for bone marrow failure in FA
No conditioning is required for FA-HSCT — the immune deficiency in FA allows spontaneous donor engraftment without cytoreductive preparation
a: Low-dose/modified conditioning regimen (Fludarabine 30 mg/m²/day × 5 days + low-dose cyclophosphamide 10–15 mg/kg/day × 4 days ± low-dose radiation 300–450 cGy TBI) — standard-dose alkylating agents (cyclophosphamide 200 mg/kg) and radiation cause fatal regimen-related toxicity in FA patients due to their defective DNA interstrand crosslink repair pathway
ex: Fanconi Anemia is caused by defective DNA interstrand crosslink (ICL) repair — the FA/BRCA pathway. ICL-inducing agents (alkylating agents like cyclophosphamide, nitrogen mustard, busulfan) and radiation are the very agents that CAUSE chromosomal breaks in FA cells (demonstrated by DEB/MMC testing). Standard SAA conditioning uses cyclophosphamide 200 mg/kg — in FA patients this would cause catastrophic, often fatal multi-organ toxicity from uncorrected DNA damage. Modified low-dose conditioning (Flu + low-CY ± low TBI) achieves adequate immunosuppression for donor engraftment while minimizing DNA damage. Results: OS >80% with MSD using modified conditioning. FA patients also have increased cancer risk post-HSCT (squamous cell carcinoma of head/neck/GYN tract) requiring lifelong oncological surveillance. ALL FA patients with aplastic anemia/MDS transformation should be referred for early HSCT evaluation before second malignancy or clonal evolution.

q: A 62-year-old woman presents with progressive weakness, bilateral lower limb numbness, and impaired proprioception over 3 months. Neurological examination reveals subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (posterior and lateral column signs). Hgb 9.8 g/dL, MCV 118 fL. She had a total gastrectomy for gastric cancer 4 years ago. Serum B12 is 48 pg/mL (severely low). What is the urgency and route of B12 replacement?
o: Urgent IM hydroxocobalamin (1 mg daily for 7 days, then weekly for 4 weeks, then monthly for life) — neurological manifestations of B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration) require urgent treatment as irreversibility increases with duration; after total gastrectomy, oral B12 is ineffective (no intrinsic factor); monthly maintenance injections are required lifelong
High-dose oral cyanocobalamin 1000 µg/day — passive diffusion allows adequate absorption even without intrinsic factor after total gastrectomy
Weekly IM B12 for 2 years then reassess — neurological manifestations can be safely observed before initiating definitive therapy
Folate supplementation first — folic acid corrects hematological B12 deficiency before neurological assessment begins
a: Urgent IM hydroxocobalamin (1 mg daily for 7 days, then weekly for 4 weeks, then monthly for life) — neurological manifestations of B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration) require urgent treatment as irreversibility increases with duration; after total gastrectomy, oral B12 is ineffective (no intrinsic factor); monthly maintenance injections are required lifelong
ex: Total gastrectomy removes ALL gastric parietal cells — eliminating intrinsic factor (IF) production permanently. Without IF, vitamin B12 cannot be absorbed at the ileal cubilin receptors (99% of B12 absorption requires IF). B12 deficiency after gastrectomy is UNIVERSAL if not supplemented and develops insidiously over years as hepatic stores deplete (~2–4 years). Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (SACD) involves demyelination of posterior columns (vibration/proprioception loss) and lateral columns (corticospinal tract — upper motor neuron signs). SACD is a neurological emergency — delay in treatment leads to irreversible demyelination and permanent disability. IM hydroxocobalamin bypasses the absorption defect entirely. High-dose oral B12 (1000–2000 µg/day) exploits passive diffusion (1–2% absorbed without IF) and is an acceptable alternative in patients refusing injections, but IM is the gold standard post-gastrectomy. NEVER give folic acid alone when B12 deficiency is suspected — it corrects anemia but accelerates neurological deterioration.

q: A 48-year-old female nurse presents with ecchymoses, epistaxis, and gum bleeding of 3-week duration with no prior bleeding history. Hgb 11.2 g/dL, platelets 9 × 10⁹/L, PT/APTT/fibrinogen all normal. Bone marrow biopsy shows abundant megakaryocytes. Anti-platelet IgG antibodies are detected. She is not on any medications. What is the diagnosis and first-line treatment?
o: Primary Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP) in adults — first-line treatment is prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day (up to 80 mg/day) for 2–4 weeks with gradual taper; IVIG 1 g/kg × 1–2 doses for rapid platelet elevation in severe bleeding or before urgent procedures; anti-D immunoglobulin for Rh(D)-positive non-splenectomized patients as an alternative first-line option
Aplastic anemia — hypocellular marrow with thrombocytopenia requires ATG + cyclosporine immunosuppression
Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura — isolated severe thrombocytopenia with anti-platelet antibodies indicates TTP requiring plasma exchange
Drug-induced ITP — all adult ITP is secondary to unrecognized medication exposure; full drug history excludes this before ITP diagnosis
a: Primary Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP) in adults — first-line treatment is prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day (up to 80 mg/day) for 2–4 weeks with gradual taper; IVIG 1 g/kg × 1–2 doses for rapid platelet elevation in severe bleeding or before urgent procedures; anti-D immunoglobulin for Rh(D)-positive non-splenectomized patients as an alternative first-line option
ex: Adult ITP is predominantly chronic unlike childhood ITP (acute/self-limiting). Diagnosis of exclusion: isolated thrombocytopenia + abundant megakaryocytes on marrow (ruling out production failure) + anti-platelet IgG + absence of secondary causes (SLE, CLL, medications, HIV, HCV). Normal PT/APTT/fibrinogen excludes DIC/TTP/TMA. Adult ITP: ~70–80% respond to corticosteroids initially but majority relapse on taper — unlike children where 80% remit spontaneously. Mechanism: anti-GPIIb/IIIa or anti-GPIb IgG antibodies → splenic macrophage Fc receptor-mediated platelet destruction + megakaryocyte thrombopoiesis inhibition. IVIG 1 g/kg transiently raises platelets within 24–48 hours (Fc receptor blockade mechanism). Second-line options: rituximab (~60% response), TPO-receptor agonists (eltrombopag, romiplostim) for chronic ITP, splenectomy (60–70% durable remission). Fostamatinib (SYK inhibitor) and avatrombopag are newer third-line options.

q: A 25-year-old woman presents with recurrent painful vaso-occlusive crises (6 hospitalizations in the past year) despite being on hydroxyurea 35 mg/kg/day for 2 years. Her HbF is 12%, ANC is 2.1 × 10⁹/L, and baseline Hgb is 8.1 g/dL. She asks about newer treatment options. What is the most appropriate next therapy to add or substitute?
o: Crizanlizumab (anti-P-selectin monoclonal antibody, SUSTAIN trial) reduces VOC frequency by 45% compared to placebo as add-on therapy; voxelotor (allosteric Hgb modifier increasing O2 affinity) improves Hgb but does not reduce VOC — both can be used with hydroxyurea; gene therapy (betibeglogene autotemcel/Zynteglo or exagamglogene autotemcel/Casgevy) offers potential cure and should be discussed
Double the hydroxyurea dose to 70 mg/kg/day — insufficient HbF elevation indicates subtherapeutic dosing
Immediate allogeneic HSCT from matched sibling donor — the only curative option that should be offered to all young SCD patients refractory to hydroxyurea
Chronic transfusion therapy to maintain HbS <30% — indiscriminate chronic transfusion is the second-line for all hydroxyurea-refractory VOC patients
a: Crizanlizumab (anti-P-selectin monoclonal antibody, SUSTAIN trial) reduces VOC frequency by 45% compared to placebo as add-on therapy; voxelotor (allosteric Hgb modifier increasing O2 affinity) improves Hgb but does not reduce VOC — both can be used with hydroxyurea; gene therapy (betibeglogene autotemcel/Zynteglo or exagamglogene autotemcel/Casgevy) offers potential cure and should be discussed
ex: This patient is at maximum tolerated hydroxyurea dose (dose-limited by ANC 2.1 × 10⁹/L approaching the 2.0 × 10⁹/L threshold) with persistent high VOC rate — hydroxyurea partial responder. Novel FDA-approved SCD therapies: (1) Crizanlizumab (Adakveo) — anti-P-selectin antibody preventing P-selectin-mediated vaso-occlusion; SUSTAIN trial showed 45% reduction in VOC rate; can be combined with HU; IV infusion every 4 weeks; (2) Voxelotor (Oxbryta) — allosterically stabilizes HbS in oxyhemoglobin state preventing deoxygenation-induced polymerization; improves Hgb ~1 g/dL but modest VOC reduction; (3) Gene therapy: Zynteglo (lentiviral βA-T87Q-globin insertion) and Casgevy (CRISPR BCL11A enhancer disruption reactivating HbF) are potentially curative — Casgevy is the first CRISPR therapy approved by FDA/EMA. Allo-HSCT remains curative when a matched sibling donor is available, especially in young patients.

q: A 71-year-old male is referred following an incidental finding of paraprotein (IgA lambda 0.8 g/dL) on a protein electrophoresis done during a workup for peripheral neuropathy. Bone marrow biopsy shows 3% clonal plasma cells. Free light chain ratio is 0.3 (mildly abnormal, below 0.26). His neuropathy is progressive with nerve conduction studies showing predominantly demyelinating peripheral neuropathy. VEGF levels are markedly elevated. CT shows osteosclerotic bone lesions. What is the diagnosis beyond MGUS?
o: POEMS syndrome (Polyneuropathy, Organomegaly, Endocrinopathy, M-protein, Skin changes) — IgA or IgG lambda M-protein with osteosclerotic lesions, demyelinating neuropathy, elevated VEGF, and possible endocrinopathy/organomegaly; treatment: radiation for solitary/limited osteosclerotic lesions; autologous HSCT for disseminated disease; lenalidomide-dexamethasone for transplant-ineligible patients
Smoldering multiple myeloma with paraneoplastic neuropathy — VRd induction is required before addressing the neuropathy
AL amyloidosis — lambda light chains deposit in peripheral nerves causing amyloid neuropathy; fat pad biopsy and Congo red staining confirm amyloid deposits
Waldenström Macroglobulinemia with IgM-mediated demyelinating neuropathy — IgA paraprotein excludes this diagnosis but anti-MAG antibody testing should be performed
a: POEMS syndrome (Polyneuropathy, Organomegaly, Endocrinopathy, M-protein, Skin changes) — IgA or IgG lambda M-protein with osteosclerotic lesions, demyelinating neuropathy, elevated VEGF, and possible endocrinopathy/organomegaly; treatment: radiation for solitary/limited osteosclerotic lesions; autologous HSCT for disseminated disease; lenalidomide-dexamethasone for transplant-ineligible patients
ex: POEMS syndrome is a paraneoplastic disorder from an underlying plasma cell neoplasm. Diagnostic criteria (Dispenzieri): mandatory — polyneuropathy + clonal plasma cell disorder; plus at least 1 major (osteosclerotic lesion, Castleman disease, elevated VEGF) and 1 minor criterion (organomegaly, endocrinopathy, skin changes, papilledema, extravascular volume overload, thrombocytosis). Key features: lambda light chain restriction (>95%), osteosclerotic NOT osteolytic lesions (unlike MM), markedly elevated VEGF (reflects disease activity), demyelinating polyneuropathy (vs. MM — axonal neuropathy from amyloid or bortezomib). Very low plasma cell burden (3% BMPC, 0.8 g/dL M-protein) does not exclude POEMS — the diagnosis is clinical. VEGF elevation is a sensitive biomarker. Treatment: limited osteosclerotic disease → radiotherapy achieves high response rates; disseminated disease → auto-HSCT is preferred curative approach; non-transplant eligible → lenalidomide-dexamethasone.

q: A 55-year-old male with a history of treated DLBCL in complete remission for 3 years develops a rising LDH and rapidly enlarging cervical lymphadenopathy over 3 weeks. Biopsy of the lymph node shows diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with MYC rearrangement confirmed on FISH (no BCL-2 or BCL-6 rearrangement). PET-CT shows stage IV disease. He is fit (ECOG 1). This is identified as a late relapse. What is the optimal treatment approach?
o: Salvage chemotherapy (R-ICE or R-DHAP or Pola-R-ICE) to establish chemosensitivity, followed by high-dose chemotherapy and autologous HSCT for chemo-sensitive relapsed DLBCL; if chemo-refractory, CAR-T therapy (axicabtagene ciloleucel or lisocabtagene maraleucel) is indicated based on ZUMA-7 and TRANSFORM trials showing superiority/non-inferiority to auto-HSCT in second-line setting
Rituximab monotherapy — CD20-targeted therapy alone is sufficient for late DLBCL relapse
Palliative bendamustine-rituximab — aggressive treatment is not appropriate for relapsed DLBCL regardless of patient fitness
Ibrutinib (BTK inhibitor) — MYC rearrangement indicates B-cell receptor pathway dependence amenable to BTK inhibition
a: Salvage chemotherapy (R-ICE or R-DHAP or Pola-R-ICE) to establish chemosensitivity, followed by high-dose chemotherapy and autologous HSCT for chemo-sensitive relapsed DLBCL; if chemo-refractory, CAR-T therapy (axicabtagene ciloleucel or lisocabtagene maraleucel) is indicated based on ZUMA-7 and TRANSFORM trials showing superiority/non-inferiority to auto-HSCT in second-line setting
ex: Relapsed DLBCL (>12 months from complete remission = 'late relapse') in a fit patient (ECOG 1) requires curative-intent salvage strategy. MYC rearrangement as a SINGLE hit (without concurrent BCL-2/BCL-6) = 'single-hit MYC' — not classified as double/triple hit lymphoma; prognosis is intermediate. Treatment algorithm: (1) Platinum-based salvage (R-ICE: rituximab-ifosfamide-carboplatin-etoposide, or R-DHAP: rituximab-dexamethasone-cytarabine-cisplatin, or newer Pola-R-ICE) × 2–3 cycles; (2) If chemo-sensitive (CR/PR): stem cell mobilization → high-dose chemotherapy (BEAM: BCNU-etoposide-cytarabine-melphalan) + auto-HSCT; (3) If primary refractory or early relapse (<12 months): CAR-T cell therapy (ZUMA-7 trial: axi-cel superior to auto-HSCT in 2nd line; TRANSFORM: liso-cel non-inferior); (4) Polatuzumab vedotin (anti-CD79b ADC) in Pola-R-BR for 3rd line+.

q: Who is credited as the 'Father of Taxonomy' and introduced the binomial nomenclature system still used in bacterial classification today?
What is the correct descending hierarchical order of taxonomic ranks used in bacteriology?
o: Domain → Phylum → Class → Order → Family → Genus → Species
Kingdom → Class → Phylum → Order → Family → Genus → Species
Domain → Class → Phylum → Family → Order → Genus → Species
Kingdom → Phylum → Order → Class → Family → Genus → Species
a: Domain → Phylum → Class → Order → Family → Genus → Species
