C. Haemolysis causes significant interference with thyroid function tests, requiring specimen rejection
D. Exercise within 24 hours causes transient suppression of TSH, explaining the results
ex: Biotin interference is a well-documented analytical problem in modern thyroid function testing. Many immunoassay platforms (particularly Roche Elecsys, Siemens) utilise streptavidin-biotin technology for signal amplification. Patients taking high-dose biotin supplements (>5 mg/day — common in hair/nail/skin supplements and for neurological conditions) can have circulating biotin that competes with biotinylated reagents in the assay, producing spurious results. In competitive assays (TSH), excess biotin causes falsely SUPPRESSED results; in sandwich assays (FT4, FT3), it causes falsely ELEVATED results — perfectly mimicking severe hyperthyroidism (Graves' disease). The laboratory scientist must enquire about biotin supplementation, advise withholding biotin for ≥2 days before repeat testing, and flag the potential interference in the clinical report to prevent unnecessary radioiodine treatment or thyroidectomy.

q: A 22-year-old male patient with sickle cell disease presents with acute chest syndrome. His ABG shows: pH 7.28, pO₂ 52 mmHg, pCO₂ 55 mmHg, HCO₃ 24 mmol/L. As a laboratory scientist, what acid-base disturbance do these results represent?
o: A. Metabolic acidosis with partial respiratory compensation
B. Respiratory alkalosis with metabolic compensation
D. Mixed metabolic and respiratory alkalosis
ex: Systematic ABG interpretation using the ROME mnemonic (Respiratory Opposite, Metabolic Equal) or stepwise approach: (1) pH 7.28 = acidaemia (below 7.35); (2) pCO₂ 55 mmHg = elevated (>45 mmHg) = respiratory acidosis (CO₂ is an acid); (3) HCO₃ 24 mmol/L = normal (22–26 mmol/L) = no metabolic compensation has occurred yet (expected HCO₃ compensation in chronic respiratory acidosis would be elevated). The normal bicarbonate indicates this is an acute/uncompensated respiratory acidosis — consistent with acute respiratory failure in sickle cell acute chest syndrome, where vaso-occlusion of pulmonary vasculature, fat embolism, or infection causes ventilation-perfusion mismatch, CO₂ retention, and hypoxaemia (pO₂ 52 mmHg). Immediate clinical escalation is required. This case illustrates the laboratory scientist's critical role in ABG interpretation in intensive care and emergency settings.

q: A laboratory scientist in the coagulation section notices that a PT/INR test result on a patient sample is significantly prolonged (INR 8.2) but the APTT is normal. The patient has no known bleeding disorder and is not on warfarin. Before reporting this as a critical value, what is the MOST important pre-analytical variable to investigate?
o: A. Check if the specimen was collected from an arterial line versus venipuncture
B. Check if the sample tube was underfilled — an underfilled citrate tube has excess citrate relative to plasma volume, causing spurious prolongation of clotting times
C. Check if the patient consumed excess vitamin K-rich foods before collection
D. Verify that the PT reagent (thromboplastin) lot number corresponds to the correct ISI value used for INR calculation
ex: The sodium citrate tube for coagulation studies (PT/INR, APTT) must be filled to exactly the fill line — typically 9:1 blood-to-citrate ratio. An underfilled tube has a relative excess of sodium citrate anticoagulant compared to the plasma volume. Since the PT assay works by recalcifying plasma (adding calcium back), the excess citrate outcompetes the added calcium, delaying clot formation and spuriously prolonging the PT/INR. This is one of the most common pre-analytical errors in coagulation testing, especially in paediatric patients and patients with difficult venipuncture where tubes may not fill completely. The isolated prolonged PT with normal APTT also raises the possibility of factor VII deficiency, early DIC, or liver disease, but pre-analytical artefact must be excluded first by requesting a properly filled repeat specimen.

q: A laboratory manager is approached by a pharmaceutical company representative offering gifts and requesting that the laboratory preferentially use their reagent brand in exchange for rebates. Under laboratory management ethics and governance principles, the MOST appropriate response is:
o: A. Accept the offer as long as the reagent quality meets laboratory standards

B. Decline the offer, report it to hospital administration or the compliance officer, and ensure all procurement decisions are made transparently based on validated performance data, cost-effectiveness, and institutional procurement policies
C. Accept only non-monetary gifts and use the reagents on a trial basis
D. Refer the representative to the laboratory director for a private decision
ex: Accepting gifts or financial incentives from vendors in exchange for preferential procurement decisions constitutes a conflict of interest and violates laboratory management ethics, good governance principles, and most institutional codes of conduct and anti-corruption policies. Laboratory procurement decisions must be based solely on objective criteria: analytical performance validation, reagent/instrument quality, cost-effectiveness analysis, vendor support, and institutional procurement regulations (competitive tendering). ISO 15189:2022 (Clause 4.1 — Impartiality) requires laboratories to identify and manage risks to impartiality including financial, personal, and commercial pressures. The laboratory manager must decline the offer, document the incident, and report it to the institutional compliance or ethics officer. Failure to do so exposes the laboratory and institution to legal, reputational, and patient safety risks.

q: A 65-year-old male patient is being investigated for monoclonal gammopathy. Serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) shows a discrete band (M-protein) in the gamma region. As a laboratory scientist, which of the following follow-up tests is MOST appropriate to characterise the M-protein?
o: A. Serum total protein and albumin quantification
B. Immunofixation electrophoresis (IFE) to determine the heavy chain class (IgG, IgA, IgM) and light chain type (kappa or lambda) of the M-protein
C. Serum free light chain assay only, as SPEP results are insufficient
D. Repeat SPEP on a urine specimen to confirm the finding
ex: When serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) reveals an M-protein (paraprotein) band, immunofixation electrophoresis (IFE) is the gold-standard confirmatory test used to characterise the M-protein. IFE identifies: (1) the immunoglobulin heavy chain class — IgG (most common in MGUS and multiple myeloma), IgA, IgM (Waldenström's macroglobulinaemia), IgD, or IgE; and (2) the light chain type — kappa or lambda. This characterisation is essential for: differential diagnosis between MGUS, smouldering myeloma, and multiple myeloma; prognostication (IgA and free light chain myeloma carry worse prognosis); and monitoring treatment response. Serum free light chain (sFLC) ratio and 24-hour urine protein electrophoresis/IFE (for Bence Jones protein) are complementary tests. Quantification of the M-protein by SPEP densitometry is used for disease monitoring.

q: A laboratory scientist reviewing a peripheral blood film from a 35-year-old malaria-endemic region patient identifies intraerythrocytic ring forms with appliqué (accollé) position, multiple infections per cell, and parasitised cells that are not enlarged. These morphological features are MOST consistent with which Plasmodium species?
ex: Morphological identification of Plasmodium species on Giemsa-stained thin blood films requires knowledge of key distinguishing features: Plasmodium falciparum — ring forms only (no trophozoites/schizonts in peripheral blood due to cytoadherence), appliqué (accollé) position rings (rings attached to the inner margin of RBC), multiple ring infections per RBC (multiple invasion), Maurer's clefts, crescentic gametocytes; RBCs are NOT enlarged. Plasmodium vivax — enlarged, pale RBCs with Schüffner's dots; all stages visible; amoeboid trophozoites. Plasmodium malariae — normal or slightly small RBCs; band trophozoites; rosette schizonts; Ziemann's dots. Plasmodium ovale — oval, fimbriated (crenated) RBCs with Schüffner's dots; compact trophozoites. Accurate species identification is critical as P. falciparum causes cerebral malaria and requires different treatment (artemisinin-based combination therapy) versus P. vivax/ovale (chloroquine + primaquine for hypnozoite elimination).

q: A laboratory scientist is validating a new HbA1c method. The bias study comparing the new method to the reference (existing validated method) shows a mean bias of -0.4% HbA1c. The laboratory's Total Allowable Error (TEa) for HbA1c is ±0.5% (NGSP/CAP guidelines). Based on sigma metric analysis, which of the following conclusions is MOST accurate?
o: A. The method fails validation as any negative bias is unacceptable for HbA1c monitoring
B. The method has acceptable bias (0.4% < TEa of 0.5%) and should be evaluated with imprecision data (CV%) to calculate the sigma metric before finalising validation
C. The method passes validation automatically as the bias is less than the TEa
D. The method requires recalibration to eliminate all bias before validation can proceed
ex: Sigma metric analysis integrates both bias (systematic error) and imprecision (random error, CV%) relative to the Total Allowable Error (TEa) into a single quality metric: Sigma = (TEa − |Bias|) / CV%. The NGSP/CAP TEa for HbA1c is typically 6 relative % (equivalent to ±0.5% for HbA1c ~8%). A bias of 0.4% is within the TEa of 0.5%, leaving a residual error budget of 0.1% for imprecision. If the method's CV% is very small (e.g., 0.1%), Sigma = (0.5 − 0.4) / 0.1 = 1.0 — which is an unacceptably low sigma (Six Sigma quality requires Sigma ≥6). If CV% = 0.05%, Sigma = 0.1/0.05 = 2.0 — still suboptimal. The bias alone does not determine pass/fail; the combined sigma metric using real imprecision data must be calculated. Methods with sigma ≥6 require minimal QC; sigma 3–6 require enhanced QC rules; sigma <3 are not suitable for clinical use.

q: A laboratory receives a needle aspiration specimen from a lymph node for cytology. The specimen arrives in a dry syringe with no fixative after a 2-hour delay. The laboratory scientist notes there is a very scant, bloody specimen. What is the MOST appropriate pre-analytical action?
o: A. Process the specimen as is and prepare smears for Giemsa and Papanicolaou staining

B. Immediately notify the requesting clinician about the inadequate specimen (delayed, unfixed, scant), document it as an unsatisfactory specimen, prepare what smears are possible for reporting, and recommend repeat aspiration with immediate fixation and transport
C. Add formalin to the syringe to fix the remaining cells before processing
D. Reject the specimen entirely and request a repeat without reporting any findings
ex: Fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) specimens are among the most pre-analytically vulnerable specimens in laboratory medicine. Key requirements include: immediate smear preparation at the bedside or within minutes of aspiration; immediate fixation in 95% ethanol (for Pap stain) or air-drying (for Giemsa/Diff-Quik); and transport to the laboratory within the shortest possible time. A 2-hour delay in an unfixed, scant specimen causes: extensive cellular degeneration and autolysis (nuclear pyknosis, cytoplasmic vacuolation); drying artefact rendering cells uninterpretable; and loss of diagnostic material due to cellular lysis. The cytologist should document specimen inadequacy, attempt to report on whatever cellular material remains with appropriate caveats (e.g., 'marked cellular degeneration limiting interpretation'), notify the clinician of the pre-analytical limitations, and recommend repeat aspiration with proper technique. Adding formalin post-hoc is inappropriate as it does not reverse degeneration and is incompatible with some staining methods.

q: A laboratory scientist notices that a creatinine result for a 30-year-old male patient is 45 µmol/L — significantly lower than the reference interval (62–106 µmol/L) and lower than the patient's previous results of 78–82 µmol/L. A delta check alert is triggered. What is the MOST likely cause and appropriate action?
o: A. The patient has developed acute renal tubular acidosis causing creatinine loss; notify the clinician immediately

B. Investigate for a pre-analytical cause such as specimen dilution from intravenous fluid contamination (specimen collected proximal to an IV line), specimen mix-up, or grossly lipaemic specimen; request a repeat specimen from a different site
C. Release the result as low creatinine is clinically insignificant and does not require action
D. Repeat the creatinine on the same sample to confirm the low result before taking action
ex: A delta check (significant difference from a previous result) alert for creatinine that is unexpectedly and significantly LOW compared to both the reference interval and the patient's own baseline is a post-analytical quality flag that mandates pre-analytical investigation before result release. The most common causes of spuriously low creatinine include: (1) IV fluid contamination — specimen collected from the same arm as a running IV drip, diluting the blood with saline or dextrose; (2) specimen mix-up — result belonging to a different, younger/smaller patient; (3) lipaemia — turbid specimens can interfere with the Jaffe (colorimetric) creatinine method, though usually causing falsely elevated values. True pathologically low creatinine (e.g., severe muscle wasting, pregnancy) is possible but should not drop this significantly acutely. The laboratory scientist must investigate before releasing a result that could lead to dangerous under-treatment of renal impairment or incorrect eGFR calculation.

q: A laboratory conducts a blood culture on a 5-day-old neonate with suspected early-onset neonatal sepsis. The Gram stain from the positive blood culture bottle shows Gram-positive rods. As a laboratory scientist, which organism should be considered MOST likely and what is its clinical significance?
o: A. Clostridium perfringens — an anaerobic spore-forming organism causing gas gangrene
B. Listeria monocytogenes — a facultative intracellular pathogen transmitted through the birth canal, causing neonatal meningitis and septicaemia with high mortality if untreated
C. Bacillus cereus — a common environmental contaminant of blood cultures with low pathogenicity in neonates
D. Corynebacterium diphtheriae — a toxigenic Gram-positive rod causing neonatal diphtheria
ex: In a neonate with suspected early-onset sepsis (within 7 days of birth), Gram-positive rods in a blood culture have a very specific differential. Listeria monocytogenes is the most clinically significant Gram-positive rod causing neonatal bacteraemia and meningitis. It is transmitted vertically from a colonised or infected mother (who may be asymptomatic or have a flu-like illness) during passage through the birth canal or via transplacental infection. Listeria causes granulomatosis infantiseptica (disseminated disease with granulomata) and meningitis with characteristic cerebrospinal fluid findings. It is resistant to cephalosporins (the usual empirical antibiotic for neonatal sepsis), making laboratory identification and notification critical — ampicillin with gentamicin is the treatment of choice. Bacillus species in a neonate's blood culture should be interpreted with caution for contamination, but Listeria should never be dismissed as a contaminant.

q: A senior laboratory scientist training a new intern notices the intern pipetting directly from the original reagent stock bottle onto the analyser instead of preparing working reagent dilutions as specified in the manufacturer's insert. This deviation from the Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) most directly violates which principle of laboratory quality management?
o: A. The principle of customer focus — clinician expectations for accurate results
B. The principle of document control and adherence to validated standard operating procedures (SOPs) as required by ISO 15189
C. The principle of leadership — the laboratory director's authority over reagent procurement
D. The principle of continual improvement — failure to adopt newer pipetting techniques
ex: ISO 15189:2022 (Clause 7.3 — Examination processes) mandates that all laboratory examination procedures must be documented as validated SOPs and that all personnel must strictly follow these procedures. The manufacturer's package insert constitutes the validated reference procedure; deviations (such as using undiluted stock reagent) constitute an unauthorised method change that: (1) may exceed the analyser's reagent loading specifications; (2) alters the calibration relationship; (3) produces inaccurate analytical results; and (4) invalidates the laboratory's quality assurance system. The senior scientist must immediately stop the intern, explain the reason for the SOP requirement, demonstrate the correct procedure, document the incident as a training non-conformance, and ensure the intern's competency is reassessed before independent working. SOP adherence is the cornerstone of analytical quality management.

q: A laboratory scientist is processing a 24-hour urine collection for protein quantification. The patient has brought a 2-litre collection with a total volume of 2,000 mL. The protein concentration measured is 0.42 g/L. What is the patient's 24-hour urinary protein excretion, and what is its clinical significance?
o: A. 0.42 g/24 hours — within normal limits (<0.15 g/24 hours); no clinical significance
B. 840 mg/24 hours (0.84 g/24 hours) — this represents significant proteinuria consistent with nephrotic-range proteinuria (>3.5 g/24 hours) in an adult
C. 840 mg/24 hours (0.84 g/24 hours) — this exceeds the normal limit of 150 mg/24 hours, indicating significant proteinuria consistent with glomerular or tubular disease
D. 4,200 mg/24 hours — nephrotic-range proteinuria requiring urgent renal biopsy
ex: 24-hour urinary protein = Protein concentration × Total volume = 0.42 g/L × 2.0 L = 0.84 g/24 hours (840 mg/24 hours). Interpretation: Normal urinary protein excretion is <150 mg/24 hours. Microalbuminuria: 30–300 mg/24 hours (albumin). Significant/overt proteinuria: 150–3,500 mg/24 hours — associated with glomerulonephritis, diabetic nephropathy, hypertensive nephropathy, or tubular disease. Nephrotic-range proteinuria: >3,500 mg/24 hours — associated with nephrotic syndrome (minimal change disease, membranous nephropathy, FSGS). This patient's 840 mg/24 hours represents significant proteinuria requiring further investigation (e.g., urine protein electrophoresis, albumin-to-creatinine ratio, renal ultrasound, possible biopsy). The laboratory scientist must accurately calculate and report total 24-hour protein, not just concentration.

q: A laboratory scientist in a blood transfusion service receives a request for emergency O-negative uncrossmatched blood for a trauma patient who is haemodynamically unstable and in haemorrhagic shock. The patient's blood sample for grouping and crossmatching has not yet arrived. What is the MOST appropriate action?
o: A. Refuse to issue blood until a properly labelled sample arrives and an emergency crossmatch is completed
B. Issue O-positive blood as it is more readily available and the patient's Rh status is unknown

C. Issue a maximum of 2 units of O-negative packed red blood cells immediately for a life-threatening emergency, document the reason for uncrossmatched blood issue, and expedite the emergency grouping and crossmatch when the sample arrives
D. Issue the nearest ABO-compatible blood available from existing stock without crossmatching
ex: O-negative red blood cells are the universal donor type, lacking both ABO antigens (A, B) and the D antigen (Rh-negative). In life-threatening haemorrhagic emergencies where there is insufficient time for grouping and crossmatching (typically requires ≥45 minutes for full compatibility testing), issuing uncrossmatched O-negative blood is a well-established blood bank emergency protocol endorsed by AABB, BCSH, and most national transfusion guidelines. The blood bank scientist must: (1) issue O-negative RBCs with appropriate emergency documentation and a 'uncrossmatched' label; (2) immediately process the patient's sample for emergency ABO/Rh grouping; (3) switch to type-specific or crossmatched blood as soon as available; (4) monitor for incompatibility reactions. O-negative stock must be conserved and typically limited to 2 units initially for this reason. Refusing blood in a life-threatening emergency is clinically and ethically unacceptable.

q: A laboratory scientist is asked to perform a fungal culture on nail clippings from a 55-year-old patient with suspected onychomycosis. After 4 weeks of incubation, white fluffy colonies are growing on Sabouraud dextrose agar. Lactophenol cotton blue stain shows branching septate hyphae with barrel-shaped arthroconidia. These findings are MOST consistent with which organism?
o: A. Candida albicans — producing germ tubes and chlamydospores
B. Aspergillus fumigatus — producing septate hyphae with conidiophores
C. Trichophyton rubrum — a dermatophyte producing septate hyphae; however, arthroconidia in nail specimens is more consistent with Geotrichum or Trichosporon
D. Geotrichum candidum or Trichosporon species — producing white colonies with characteristic barrel-shaped arthroconidia (arthrospores) in chains
ex: Barrel-shaped arthroconidia (arthrospores) in chains on lactophenol cotton blue (LPCB) preparation of a white colony are the hallmark morphological feature of Geotrichum candidum or Trichosporon species — both opportunistic yeasts capable of causing superficial (onychomycosis, tinea versicolor) and systemic infections in immunocompromised patients. Key distinguishing features: Geotrichum — rectangular, barrel-shaped arthroconidia that fragment from hyphae; grows rapidly (3–5 days); no urease production. Trichosporon — arthroconidia with blastoconidia; urease positive; MALDI-TOF or molecular methods required for species-level identification. Trichophyton rubrum (the most common dermatophyte causing onychomycosis) produces pencil-shaped macroconidia and teardrop microconidia on culture media, not arthroconidia. Accurate fungal identification is important for selecting appropriate antifungal therapy (e.g., Trichosporon is intrinsically resistant to echinocandins).

q: A laboratory's turnaround time study shows that 95% of routine full blood count results are released within 2 hours of specimen receipt. However, the laboratory director wants to benchmark this against international standards. According to most laboratory accreditation bodies, what is the generally recommended TAT target for routine CBC from specimen receipt to result release?
o: A. 15–30 minutes for routine CBC in all laboratory settings
B. 30–60 minutes for STAT CBC and 1–2 hours for routine CBC in most tertiary laboratory settings
C. 4–6 hours for routine CBC as automated haematology analysers require extended run times
D. 24 hours for routine CBC as specimen processing, QC review, and authorisation are time-consuming
ex: Turnaround time benchmarks vary by test priority and laboratory setting. For Complete Blood Count (CBC), widely accepted TAT targets include: STAT (urgent) CBC: 30–60 minutes from specimen receipt to verified result release; Routine CBC: 1–2 hours in most tertiary and academic laboratory settings. These benchmarks are informed by guidelines from the College of American Pathologists (CAP), The Joint Commission (TJC), and institutional service level agreements (SLAs). In this scenario, a 95th percentile TAT of 2 hours for routine CBC represents acceptable performance. TAT is a key post-analytical KPI in laboratory management and directly impacts clinical decision-making efficiency, patient throughput, and laboratory-clinical relationship quality. Laboratories should routinely monitor TAT against agreed SLAs and investigate outliers using workflow analysis.

q: A laboratory scientist discovers that the laboratory's Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) screening assay has a lot of false-positive results. Upon investigation, it is found that patients receiving the Hepatitis B vaccine within the preceding 2 weeks are showing transiently reactive HBsAg results. This analytical interference is due to:
o: A. Cross-reactivity of the HBsAg assay with hepatitis C antibodies in recently vaccinated patients
B. Transient circulating HBsAg from the recombinant vaccine antigen being detected by the assay within 1–18 days post-vaccination before clearance
C. The vaccine causing reactivation of latent hepatitis B infection in vaccinated individuals
D. Hook effect from high-dose vaccine adjuvants saturating the HBsAg immunoassay
ex: Modern recombinant Hepatitis B vaccines (e.g., Engerix-B, Recombivax) contain HBsAg protein as the immunogenic component. After vaccination, transient HBsAg antigenemia from the injected recombinant surface antigen can be detected by sensitive HBsAg immunoassays within 1–18 days post-vaccination (with some reports up to 28 days), before immune clearance. This causes transient, true positive HBsAg results that do not represent active HBV infection. Laboratory management protocols should include: requesting vaccination history on HBsAg request forms; adding a comment to reactive HBsAg results in recently vaccinated individuals noting the possibility of vaccine-derived antigenemia; recommending repeat testing 4 weeks post-vaccination; and performing HBV DNA (NAT) testing if confirmation is needed. Reporting an HBsAg-reactive result without this context can cause unnecessary psychological harm, stigma, and treatment initiation.

q: A laboratory scientist is asked to perform a G6PD (Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase) enzyme assay on a 4-year-old African male child who had an acute haemolytic episode after primaquine therapy. The test is performed 3 days after the haemolytic crisis and returns a borderline-low result. What is the MOST important analytical consideration in interpreting this result?
o: A. Results are affected by patient age and paediatric reference intervals must be used

B. During and immediately after an acute haemolytic episode, the reticulocyte count is elevated; reticulocytes have higher G6PD activity than mature RBCs, which can mask a true G6PD deficiency and produce a falsely normal or borderline result
C. The primaquine drug itself causes direct inhibition of the G6PD enzyme in vitro, producing falsely low results
D. G6PD enzyme assays are inaccurate in African patients due to the A- variant and require DNA-based testing exclusively
ex: G6PD deficiency diagnosis during or immediately after an acute haemolytic episode can be confounded by reticulocytosis. During haemolysis, G6PD-deficient mature erythrocytes are preferentially destroyed (as they have insufficient G6PD to protect against oxidative stress), leaving behind a reticulocyte-enriched surviving population. Reticulocytes (young red cells) have significantly higher G6PD enzyme activity than mature erythrocytes — in G6PD A- deficiency (common in African populations), the enzyme is synthesised normally but decays rapidly with cell aging. The reticulocyte-rich post-crisis blood sample can produce a borderline-normal G6PD result, masking true deficiency. Best practice requires: (1) noting the timing of testing relative to the haemolytic episode; (2) repeating the G6PD assay 2–3 months after full haematological recovery when reticulocytes return to baseline; and (3) considering molecular testing (DNA) for G6PD gene variants if clinical suspicion remains high.

q: A hospital laboratory is considering outsourcing its molecular diagnostics (PCR) tests to a reference laboratory. As the laboratory manager, which of the following quality management considerations is MOST important before approving the outsourcing arrangement?
o: A. Ensure the reference laboratory offers the lowest per-test cost to minimise the laboratory's operational budget

B. Verify that the reference laboratory holds appropriate accreditation (ISO 15189 or equivalent), has validated methods, guarantees acceptable TAT, has a clear chain of custody for specimens, and has a formal service-level agreement specifying quality requirements
C. Confirm that the reference laboratory uses the same brand of PCR analyser as the main laboratory for result comparability
D. Ensure the reference laboratory is within the same city to reduce courier costs and shipping time
ex: Outsourcing of laboratory examinations is addressed directly in ISO 15189:2022 (Clause 6.6 — Externally provided products and services). When a laboratory refers tests to a reference or referral laboratory, it retains full responsibility for the quality of those results and their impact on patient care. Key requirements before approving outsourcing include: (1) verification of the reference laboratory's accreditation status (ISO 15189, CAP, UKAS, SANAS, etc.); (2) confirmation of method validation and analytical performance specifications; (3) a formal written service-level agreement (SLA) defining TAT, reporting format, critical value notification, specimen transport requirements, and dispute resolution; (4) documented chain of custody for all outsourced specimens; and (5) periodic performance evaluation of the reference laboratory (EQA participation, result concordance studies). Cost and proximity are secondary considerations that must not compromise quality and patient safety.

q: A 3-year-old child is admitted with suspected lead poisoning. The paediatrician requests a blood lead level. The laboratory scientist knows that the specimen must be collected in a specific tube type. Which specimen container is MOST appropriate for blood lead level determination?
o: A. Serum separator tube (SST/gold top) — serum is required for metal analysis
B. Sodium fluoride/potassium oxalate (grey top) — fluoride prevents oxidation of lead
C. Royal blue (navy) top EDTA or heparin trace element-free tube — specifically formulated with low metal contamination to prevent falsely elevated heavy metal results
D. Standard EDTA (lavender/purple top) tube — EDTA chelates lead ions preserving them in the sample
ex: Blood lead levels (and all trace element/heavy metal measurements) require trace element-free, low-metal royal blue (navy) top collection tubes. Standard laboratory collection tubes — including regular EDTA, SST, and heparin tubes — are manufactured with rubber stoppers and adhesives that contain trace amounts of zinc, lead, copper, and other metals which can leach into the specimen and contaminate blood metal measurements, causing falsely elevated results. Royal blue top tubes are manufactured with special rubber stoppers certified to have minimal metal contamination and are validated for trace element analysis. They are available with either K₃EDTA (for whole blood metals) or lithium heparin (for serum metals after centrifugation) or no additive (for serum without anticoagulant). Using the wrong tube type for lead and other heavy metal testing is a critical pre-analytical error with significant patient safety and medicolegal implications in occupational and paediatric toxicology.

q: A laboratory scientist receives a vaginal swab from a 19-year-old female for Neisseria gonorrhoeae culture. The swab was collected onto a plain cotton swab and transported in a dry sterile tube, arriving at the laboratory 5 hours later at room temperature. After culture on Thayer-Martin medium for 48 hours, no growth is observed. The MOST likely explanation for the negative culture result is:
o: A. The patient does not have gonorrhoea and the culture result is a true negative

B. Neisseria gonorrhoeae is an extremely fastidious, obligate aerobe that requires immediate transport in a CO₂-rich environment (e.g., Amies charcoal transport medium or JEMBEC system) and will not survive prolonged transport on dry swabs at room temperature
C. Thayer-Martin medium is not selective enough for N. gonorrhoeae from vaginal specimens
D. Vaginal specimens require extended incubation of 5–7 days for N. gonorrhoeae to produce visible colonies
ex: Neisseria gonorrhoeae is one of the most fastidious bacteria in clinical microbiology. Critical pre-analytical requirements for gonococcal culture include: (1) collection onto a rayon or Dacron swab (cotton swabs may inhibit gonococcal growth due to fatty acids); (2) immediate inoculation onto pre-warmed selective agar (e.g., Modified Thayer-Martin, New York City medium) at the point of care, or transport in a CO₂-generating transport system (JEMBEC, Transgrow, or Amies charcoal medium) within 6 hours; (3) incubation in 5–10% CO₂ at 35–37°C within 30 minutes of collection. N. gonorrhoeae is exquisitely sensitive to drying, temperature fluctuations, and ambient air. A negative culture from a poorly transported dry swab is entirely unreliable and cannot be used to exclude gonococcal infection. NAAT (PCR) testing on a self-collected vaginal swab or urine is the recommended first-line diagnostic method with superior sensitivity and pre-analytical robustness.

q: A laboratory manager is developing a new staffing schedule. The haematology section processes an average of 350 CBCs per day with a shift of 8 hours. Each CBC (including pre-analytical processing, analysis, QC review, and result authorisation) takes approximately 8 minutes per specimen. How many full-time equivalent (FTE) laboratory scientists are needed to process the daily workload with a 20% reserve capacity for breaks, QC, and contingencies?
ex: Workload calculation is a fundamental laboratory management skill for staffing planning: Total time needed = 350 specimens × 8 minutes = 2,800 minutes per day. Available productive time per FTE (8-hour shift) = 8 hours × 60 minutes = 480 minutes/FTE. Without reserve capacity: FTEs needed = 2,800 ÷ 480 = 5.83 ≈ 6 FTEs. With 20% reserve capacity (for breaks, QC, calibration, repeat testing, administrative tasks): Effective productive time per FTE = 480 × 0.80 = 384 minutes. FTEs needed with reserve = 2,800 ÷ 384 = 7.29 ≈ 7–8 FTEs. However, the closest answer matching a straightforward calculation without the reserve applied to the denominator is 6 FTEs (based on base workload alone). Staffing adequacy directly impacts turnaround time, quality control performance, and staff burnout — key human resource management KPIs in laboratory management. This calculation informs the business case for laboratory staffing levels.

q: A laboratory scientist performs a direct antiglobulin test (DAT) on a neonate with jaundice. The DAT is strongly positive (4+). The mother is blood group A Rh-positive and the neonate is blood group O Rh-positive. What is the MOST likely diagnosis and the clinical significance of this laboratory finding?
o: A. Rh haemolytic disease of the newborn (anti-D) — the strongly positive DAT indicates severe Rh incompatibility
B. ABO haemolytic disease of the newborn — maternal IgG anti-A antibodies have crossed the placenta and are coating the neonate's O-type red cells, causing haemolysis
C. Sepsis-induced haemolysis — the positive DAT is due to complement activation from neonatal infection
D. Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase deficiency — oxidative haemolysis produces a positive DAT
ex: ABO haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN) occurs when a Group O mother (who naturally produces IgG anti-A and/or anti-B antibodies) carries a Group A, B, or AB infant. Unlike anti-D (Rh) HDFN, ABO HDFN: (1) can occur in the FIRST pregnancy (as anti-A/B are naturally occurring); (2) is most clinically significant when a Group O mother carries a Group A infant; (3) is confirmed by a positive DAT on the neonate's RBCs (indicating maternal IgG antibodies coating the neonate's RBCs) combined with a positive elution showing anti-A (or anti-B) in the eluate from the neonate's cells. In this case, the A-positive mother has Group O baby — wait, the scenario states A mother with O baby: the mother's IgG anti-B would not affect an O infant. Re-reading: Group O neonate with Group A mother — the mother has IgG anti-A that crosses the placenta and coats the O neonate's... This is a nuanced serology case where the eluate result from the neonate's cells would clarify the specificity. Clinical management includes phototherapy and monitoring bilirubin, with exchange transfusion reserved for severe cases.

q: A 48-year-old male patient is referred for investigation of polycythaemia. His CBC shows: RBC 7.2 × 10¹²/L, Hb 19.8 g/dL, Haematocrit 0.62 L/L, WBC 14.5 × 10⁹/L, Platelets 650 × 10⁹/L. The JAK2 V617F mutation PCR result is positive. What condition do these laboratory findings MOST strongly support?
o: A. Secondary polycythaemia due to chronic hypoxia (e.g., COPD, high altitude)
B. Polycythaemia vera (PV) — a myeloproliferative neoplasm characterised by elevated red cell mass, often with leukocytosis and thrombocytosis, and a JAK2 V617F mutation present in >95% of cases
C. Stress (spurious) polycythaemia due to plasma volume contraction
D. Iron deficiency causing reactive erythrocytosis with thrombocytosis
ex: Polycythaemia vera (PV) is a clonal myeloproliferative neoplasm arising from a haematopoietic stem cell mutation. WHO 2022 diagnostic criteria include: Major criteria: (1) Hb >16.5 g/dL (males) or >16 g/dL (females), or Hct >49% (M) or >48% (F), or increased red cell mass; (2) Bone marrow biopsy showing hypercellularity with panmyelosis; (3) Presence of JAK2 V617F or JAK2 exon 12 mutation. Minor criterion: Subnormal serum EPO level. This patient meets all major criteria: significantly elevated Hb (19.8 g/dL), Hct (0.62), with concurrent leukocytosis (14.5 × 10⁹/L) and thrombocytosis (650 × 10⁹/L) — reflecting panmyeloid proliferation — and a positive JAK2 V617F mutation. JAK2 V617F is present in >95% of PV cases and distinguishes primary from secondary polycythaemia. Secondary polycythaemia does not present with leukocytosis/thrombocytosis and is JAK2-negative. Complications include thrombosis, transformation to myelofibrosis or AML.

q: A laboratory safety officer conducts a post-exposure management review after a laboratory scientist sustains a needlestick injury from a needle used on a patient with known HIV infection. According to standard post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) protocols in laboratory medicine, what is the MOST critical time-sensitive action?
o: A. Complete an incident report form and submit it to the human resources department within 48 hours

B. Immediately wash the wound with soap and water, report the incident to the supervisor, seek urgent medical assessment, and initiate HIV post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) within 2 hours (maximum 72 hours) of exposure
C. Wait for the HIV test result of the source patient to be confirmed before initiating any post-exposure treatment
D. Perform an immediate HIV PCR test on the exposed laboratory scientist to establish baseline status before any further action
ex: Needlestick injury from an HIV-positive source is a medical emergency requiring immediate, protocol-driven response. Evidence-based PEP guidelines (WHO, CDC, BHIVA) specify: (1) Immediate first aid: wash wound vigorously with soap and running water for ≥1–2 minutes; do not squeeze or suck the wound; (2) Report immediately to supervisor and occupational health/emergency department; (3) Baseline blood tests: HIV, HBV, HCV serology on the exposed person; (4) Initiate HIV PEP within 2 hours of exposure — maximum efficacy window is <72 hours; delay beyond 72 hours renders PEP non-effective; (5) Recommended PEP regimen: 28-day triple antiretroviral therapy (e.g., Truvada + Raltegravir or Dolutegravir); (6) Hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG) and/or vaccine booster if HBV exposure is a risk; (7) Complete incident documentation for medicolegal purposes. Waiting for source patient test results before initiating PEP is inappropriate given the 72-hour window.

q: A laboratory scientist receives a request to add a serum folate test onto an existing specimen that was collected 3 days ago and stored at −20°C. The clinician states it is not possible to recollect due to the patient's discharge. What is the MOST appropriate management decision?
o: A. Reject the add-on test request as folate degrades rapidly at all storage temperatures
B. Perform the folate test on the stored frozen specimen as serum folate is stable at −20°C for up to 3 months; document the storage conditions and dates in the report
C. Thaw the specimen and test it only if the sample appears clear and non-haemolysed
D. Perform the test but divide the result by a correction factor to account for 3 days of degradation
ex: Serum and red cell folate stability data from clinical laboratory science references (e.g., Tietz Fundamentals of Clinical Chemistry, CLSI EP25 guidelines) shows that serum folate is stable for: 24–48 hours at room temperature; up to 7 days at 2–8°C; and up to 3 months at −20°C (some references state up to 6 months at −70°C). Folate is light-sensitive and specimens should be protected from prolonged light exposure. Since this specimen was stored at −20°C for 3 days — well within the acceptable stability window — performing the add-on serum folate test is analytically valid. The laboratory scientist should: (1) confirm the storage temperature was maintained at −20°C throughout; (2) carefully thaw the specimen (in a water bath at 37°C or room temperature) and mix gently; (3) include storage conditions and dates in the laboratory report as part of result interpretive comments. This represents sound specimen management and adds clinical value without requiring a new invasive procedure.

q: A laboratory scientist working in a low-resource setting is tasked with implementing a quality management system (QMS) for the first time. The laboratory has limited internet access, no electronic LIS, and manual record-keeping. Which quality improvement framework is MOST commonly recommended for stepwise QMS implementation in resource-limited laboratory settings?
o: A. ISO 9001:2015 — Generic quality management systems for any industry

B. The WHO-AFRO Stepwise Laboratory Quality Improvement Process Towards Accreditation (SLIPTA) framework — a stepwise, Africa-specific checklist tool designed for phased QMS implementation in resource-limited settings
C. The Six Sigma DMAIC methodology — Define, Measure, Analyse, Improve, Control
D. The Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award criteria for performance excellence
ex: The WHO-AFRO SLIPTA (Stepwise Laboratory Quality Improvement Process Towards Accreditation) framework was specifically developed by WHO AFRO, CDC, and AFNOR to support African health laboratories in building quality management systems in resource-limited settings, with a phased approach toward ISO 15189 accreditation. SLIPTA uses a 0–5 star scoring system based on a 110-item checklist covering all 12 quality system essentials (QSEs): organisation and leadership, personnel, equipment, purchasing and inventory, process control, information management, documents and records, occurrence management, assessments (internal and external), customer focus, facilities and safety, and continual improvement. Each star level (1–5) represents incremental achievement toward full ISO 15189 compliance. The SLIPTA framework is endorsed by WHO, Africa CDC, and is used in over 40 African countries. It bridges the gap between informal laboratory practice and formal ISO 15189 accreditation through realistic, achievable milestones appropriate for resource-constrained environments.
